# 3.2 Experience listing

# Subject: THRT not fully engaged while pulling tubing hanger free. Section: NO 15/9-F-1 B, Pull Completion

Rep date: 03.Feb.2014

Keywords: TUBING HANGER

Downtime:

Pot time improvements:

Comp inv: GE Vetco

References:

Synergi no:

Cost:

Synergi desc:

Description:

RIH with THRT and engaged TH as per GE Vetco procedure. Set 2 MT weight on hanger, marked pipe. Rotated tool RH and observed tool drop. Turned tool LH. Did not observe positive stop in J-slot profile. Suspected lock ring rotating. Applied 5 MT overpull and confirmed tool engaged. Pulled ring free with 40 MT overpull. Continued to pull, observed weight dropping to string weight at 140 MT hookload. POOH and inspected retrieval tool. No damage observed. RIH and set 2 MT on TH. Observed mark at same spot. Rotated tool RH and observed tool drop. Rotated LH and did not observe positive stop. Confirmed tool holding with 140 MT overpull. Set down 4 MT weight on hanger and turned LH to force tool fully into rotating lock ring. Repeated process. Picked up and pulled TH and completion free at 190 MT hookload. POOH and landed TH on c-plate. Tool was fully engaged when inspecting same on surface. Required several attempts to release THRT from TH. Had to rotate tool using chain tongs, suspected alignment/hung-up on communication collar. Inspected TH and observed 1/3 lock ring J-slot was bent upwards due to THRT not fully engaged during first pull. Immediate solution:

POOH and inspected retrieval tool. No damage observed. Future recommended solution:

THRT with hydraulic line should be used. Then the THRT can be locked in properly with use of hydraulics. This takes some more time, but is a safer solution. # Subject: 2 7/8" tubing equipment not fit for purpose

Section: NO 15/9-F-1 B, Permanent P&A (DP) w/ RIG

Rep date: 13.Feb.2014

Keywords: TUBING

Downtime:

Pot time improvements:

Comp inv: Statoil

References:

Synergi no:

Cost:

Synergi desc:

Description:

Prior to run 2 7/8" tubing it was discovered that it had only been supplied manual slips. 2 7/8" tubing and handling equipment had been ordered by Statoil. This resulted in a lot of time spent both on SJA/TBT/TOFS and also while tripping in hole. While picking up the first joint various means of stabbing remotely was attempted with no success. Rigged up tong tailing arm and function tested same. Continued running in with the stinger. Immediate solution:

Performed a TOFS, SJA and TBT prior to use of manual slips. Future recommended solution:

Non-standard tubular and related equipment should be specified by Statoil, but ordered by the rig contractor. That way we will get the correct equipment. Classification: Open                                  Status: Final      Expiry date:              Page 7 of 85

# Subject: 2 7/8" tubing equipment not fit for purpose

# Section: NO 15/9-F-1 B, Permanent P&A (DP) w/ RIG

Rep date: 13.Feb.2014

Keywords: TUBING

Downtime:

Pot time improvements:

Comp inv: Statoil

References:

Synergi no:

Cost:

Synergi desc:

# Description:

Attempted to P/U first 2 7/8" joint from HTS with the air operated elevator. Unable to tilt and close center latch elevator, bail rotator not designed for this type of elevator.

Evaluated different options. Attempted elevator using tugger. Unable to latch elevator. Postponed the operation to a later stage. 2 7/8" tubing and handling equipment had been ordered by Statoil. # Immediate solution:

Decided to use the BX elevator, but discovered that correct inserts were missing. Had some problems with the TDS at the same time, and decided together with town to go ahead and repair the TDS. Rigged down the Weatherford equipment. # Future recommended solution:

Non-standard tubular and related equipment should be specified by Statoil, but ordered by the rig contractor. That way we will get the correct equipment. # Subject: Make up torque for 2 7/8" tubing

# Section: NO 15/9-F-1 B, Permanent P&A (DP) w/ RIG

Rep date: 14.Feb.2014

Keywords: CEMENT PLUG

Downtime:

Pot time improvements:

Comp inv: Maersk Drilling

References:

Synergi no:

Cost:

Synergi desc:

# Description:

2 7/8" P110 8,7 # TPS TS-6 tubing was used as cement stinger. 2 7/8" drill pipe was not used due setup of Iron Roughneck. The optimum make up torque for this tubing is 5339 Nm with maximum makeup of 5935 Nm. A 300 m plug was to be set inside 7" liner and up inside 9 5/8" casing. When conditioned mud prior to cement an RPM of 20 was used giving 8 kN. This is above the makeup torque for 2 7/8". Due to this rotation was stopped. When displacing cement and rotating pipe with 30 RPM 8 kN was seen. However it was necessary for a good cement job. # Immediate solution:

# Future recommended solution:

Evaluate to use tubing/pipe with higher makeup torque. Classification: Open    Status: Final  Expiry date:

# Subject: No rotator sent out for Weatherford BX elevator

# Section: NO 15/9-F-1 B, Permanent P&A (DP) w/ RIG

# Rep date: 16.Feb.2014

# Keywords: CASING/LINER

# Downtime:

# Pot time improvements:

# Comp inv: Weatherford

# References:

# Synergi no:

# Cost:

# Synergi desc:

# Description:

No rotator was sent out with the Weatherford BX elevator for pulling the 10 3/4" x 9 5/8" casing and 13 3/8" casing. Weatherford said that they did not have any available rotators to send out to the rig. # Immediate solution:

Used Maersk elevator

# Future recommended solution:

Ensure that rotator is shipped out together with the casing handling equipment. Attempt getting one though a different vendor if none available with casing contractor. # Subject: Lockdown assembly retrieval tool overfilled with hydraulic oil and air

# Section: NO 15/9-F-1 B, Permanent P&A (DP) w/ RIG

# Rep date: 16.Feb.2014

# Keywords: WELLHEAD/ASA

# Downtime:

# Pot time improvements:

# Comp inv: GE Vetco

# References:

# Synergi no:

# Cost:

# Synergi desc:

# Description:

The lockdown assembly/seal assembly retrieval tool (SRT) is set in retrieval position on surface by pushing up a sleeve on the outer body of the tool past a ledge, locking the sleeve and tool in position for being able to engage and retrieve the lockdown assembly. The SRT hydraulic oil chamber was overfilled with oil and air, causing the sleeve never to pass the ledge that locks the SRT in retrieving position. As a result the sleeve slid back down again once the force that pushed the sleeve upwards was removed. It was not noticed that the sleeve slipped back down again prior to running the SRT in hole for retrieving the lockdown assembly. After the 1st mis-run the tool operator noticed hydraulic oil coming out of the chamber when pushing up the sleeve to set the tool in retrieval position. The operator bleed of oil and air from the hydraulic oil chamber, but did not notice that the sleeve once again did not pass the locking ledge, and slid back down again prior to running in hole. When re-setting the tool in retrieving position after the 2nd mis-run, the operator noticed that the sleeve slid back down, and bleed of more oil and air from the hydraulic chamber enabling the sleeve to be properly locked in retrieving position. # Immediate solution:

Bleed of oil and air from the tool hydraulic chamber, allowing the tool to be properly set in retrieving position. # Future recommended solution:

Verify that the retrieval tool is in retrieval mode prior to running the tool in hole. Ensure that the tool has proper amount of hydraulic oil in chamber prior to shipping the tool offshore. # Classification: Open

# Status: Final

# Expiry date:

# Page 9 of 85




Statoil

# Subject: Oil leakage in seal assembly retrieval tool

Section: NO 15/9-F-1 B, Permanent P&A (DP) w/ RIG

Rep date: 16.Feb.2014

Keywords: WELLHEAD/ASA

Downtime:                                 Pot time improvements:

Comp inv: GE Vetco

References:

Synergi no:                               Cost:

Synergi desc:

# Description:

After breaking out the seal assy retrieving tool (SRT) from the jet sub stand, the lock down assy was removed. When spinning the SRT back into the jet sub stand the outer body continued to rotate after the make-up was done. This was caused by the weight of the outer body and spinning in too fast with the iron roughneck spinner. As the outer body continued to rotate, the lower inner piston that keeps the SRT hydraulic oil in place was spun out (left hand threads), resulting in the hydraulic chamber being emptied. # Immediate solution:

Screwed the sleeve back into place and re-filled the tool with hydraulic oil. Used chain tongs instead of IRN for next ops. # Future recommended solution:

Ensure that caution is taken when spinning the SRT tool back into the jetting sub stand. Evaluate to use chain tongs instead of IRN in future ops. # Subject: Excessive amount of slops created by not circulating over Hi-Vis pill prior to cement job

Section: NO 15/9-F-1 B, Permanent P&A (DP) w/ RIG

Rep date: 23.Feb.2014

Keywords: CEMENT PLUG

Downtime:                                 Pot time improvements:

Comp inv: Statoil

References:

Synergi no:                               Cost:

Synergi desc:

# Description:

Excessive amount of slops was created when setting the KO plug on the F-1 B P&A. The cement stinger was run in hole to the cut 10 3/4" casing, and the well circulated and the mud conditioned prior to setting a Hi-Vis pill and setting the KO cement plug. The Hi-Vis pill was set from the top of the cut 10 3/4" casing at 1457 m MD and up to the cut 13 3/8" casing at 1399 m MD (where the cement KO plug would be set from). After spotting the Hi-Vis pill the stinger was pulled out to top of the cut 13 3/8" casing at 1399 m MD, and the KO cement plug set before pulling 80 m above theoretical top of cement and circulating out the spacer and Hi-Vis pill. The result from the above was that excessive slops was created from the Hi-Vis pill mixing with mud and spacer as the top of the Hi-Vis pill had not been circulated out prior to setting the cement plug. # Immediate solution:

N/A

# Future recommended solution:

It is recommended for future KO plugs to spot the Hi-Vis pill, then pull out to the top of the pill with the stinger, and then circulate out the top of the pill while conditioning the well for the cement job. Classification: Open    Status: Final  Expiry date:     Page 10 of 85

# Subject: Excessive time spent on circulating hole clean and weighting up mud. # Section: NO 15/9-F-1 C, 17 1/2"

Rep date: 18.Mar.2014

Keywords: DRILLING FLUID

Downtime:

Pot time improvements:

Comp inv: Baroid

References:

Synergi no:

Cost:

Synergi desc:

# Description:

Circulated 1 x BU, pumped a high-vis pill and circulated out of hole with little cutting over the shakers. Pumped a 2nd high-vis pill and circulated out of hole before starting to weight up mud from 1.40 sg to 1.41 sg. # Immediate solution:

NA

# Future recommended solution:

If little cuttings are seen over the shakers with the 1st hi-vis pill, then evaluate to weight up mud while circulating the 2nd hi-vis pill. # Subject: Weight up mud from 1.40 sg to 1.41 sg while circulating 17 1/2" hole clean at TD

# Section: NO 15/9-F-1 C, 17 1/2"

Rep date: 18.Mar.2014

Keywords: DRILLING FLUID

Downtime:

Pot time improvements:

Comp inv: Baroid

References:

Synergi no:

Cost:

Synergi desc:

# Description:

Increased MW from 1.40 sg to 1.41 sg prior to POOH with 17 1/2" BHA to compensate for weight of cuttings in well to ensure good hole conditions for running 13 3/8" casing. The 13 3/8" casing was RIH without experiencing any drag. When running the 13 3/8" casing on well F-15 D, the casing had to be washed down the last stand to get past a restriction. # Immediate solution:

NA

# Future recommended solution:

Evaluate to increase MW while circulating hole clean to compensate for weight of cuttings in the well, to ensure good hole conditions for running of casing. # Classification: Open

# Subject: Problem while making up the casing crossover (10 3/4" x 9 5/8")

Section: NO 15/9-F-1 C, 12 1/4"

Rep date: 07.Mar.2014

Keywords: CASING/LINER

Downtime:

Pot time improvements:

Comp inv: Weatherford

References:

Synergi no:

Cost:

Synergi desc:

# Description:

After picking the 10 3/4" x 9 5/8" crossover; the crossover body was found too big to be grabbed and made up by Weatherford tong. Some adjustment was done to fit the crossover in the tong but it led to have some compromise on make-up curve. The torque curve showed the base and the optimum torque but curve continuity could not be verified due to the adjustment. # Immediate solution:

Changing the jaws and lifting the Weatherford tong to grab the top 10 3/4" casing instead of crossover. # Future recommended solution:

It is suggested to have pre-made 10 3/4" and 9 5/8" pups to the crossover. Classification: Open

Status: Final

# Subject: Leaking cement head swivel

Section: NO 15/9-F-1 C, Liner

Rep date: 19.Mar.2014

Keywords: CEMENTING

Downtime:

Pot time improvements:

Comp inv: Weatherford Norge AS

References:

Synergi no:

Cost:

Synergi desc:

# Description:

when circulating the hole clean with the 7" liner on setting depth rotation was estbalished with 20 rpm. It was observed that the swivel on the Weatherford cement head started leaking. # Immediate solution:

It was decided to stop rotation to prevent further damage to the swivel and maintain the option to rotate at a later stage. During the cement job rotation was established and maintained while cement was displaced into the annulus. # Future recommended solution:

Improved quality of tools sent to the rig

# Subject: Encountered tight hole when running 7" liner

Section: NO 15/9-F-1 C, Liner

Rep date: 25.Mar.2014

Keywords: CASING/LINER

Downtime:

Pot time improvements:

Comp inv: Weatherford

References:

Synergi no:

Cost:

Synergi desc:

# Description:

Tight hole was encountered when RIH with the 7" liner that was managed to slide through with 10-15 MT WOB. Also experienced pack-offs that had to be worked and reamed through with 20 rpm. The above resulted in the liner being set a few meters shallow. The liner was RIH with a reamer shoe that had an OD of 8,25". # Immediate solution:

Reamed and worked though the restrictions and pack-offs. # Future recommended solution:

Evaluate to not run a reamer shoe with the liner as the OD of the reamer shoe is 8.25", while the OD of a regular guide shoe is 7.75". It is thought that the large OD of the reamer shoe could be what is causing the problems with tight hole. By running a regular shoe with a smaller OD, one might be able to avoid any problems with tight hole. Classification: Open    Status: Final  Expiry date:     Page 13 of 85

# 4.1 NO 15/9-F-1 B, Preparations (25.Jan.2014 11:00)

START: 25.Jan.2014 11:00 3465 mMD

END : 31.Jan.2014 07:45 3465 mMD

# OBJECTIVE

Kill the well and prepare the tubing for pulling. # SUMMARY

The well was handed over from production at 11:00 hrs 25.01.14. Rigged up W/L BOP and riser. Removed xmas tree cap and installed short riser and W/L BOP. Schlumberger built and tested the W/L toolstring for setting the EV0 plug. Filled the riser with 40% MEG and 60% water with the production MEG manifold. Pressure tested the wireline rig up and made up BHA for running the EV0 plug. The EV0 plug was successfully set at 3147m MD. Rigged up for W/L run # 2 (Tubing punch) and RIH and punched at 2942-2945m MD. Pumped 70m3 of 1.03sg packer fluid followed by 10m3 of 1.50 sg CaBa brine pill followed by 42m3 of packer fluid, waited for 30 minutes to allow the brine pill to settle into the 7" tubing/liner. Pumped 100m3 1.37sg OBM at 600-900 lpm until OBM in returns. Circulated to condition mud, in total 4 x B/U. M/U BHA for run # 3 (tubing cutter) and RIH. Positioned cutter at 2948.5m MD and performed cut. Removed flowline from F-1 tree, drained xmas tree and removed same. Nippled up BOP, installed diverter and R/D the equipment. # 4.2 NO 15/9-F-1 B, Pull Completion (31.Jan.2014 07:45)

START: 31.Jan.2014 07:45 3147 mMD

END : 03.Feb.2014 20:30 3465 mMD

# OBJECTIVE

Clear the borehole for tubing to make access for slot recovery

# SUMMARY

P/U THRT and landed same in rotary. Installed bushings. M/U 5 1/2" DP stand to THRT. RIH w/ THRT on 5 1/2" DP stand from surface to 21 m. Engaged TH as per GE Vetco instructions. Pulled to 110 t (40t overpull), observed actuating ring release. Continued to pick up, weight dropped off at 140 t hookload. POOH and inspected RT, no damage observed. RIH and engaged TH as per GE Vetco instructions, no clear indication of tool being fully engaged. Suspected actuator ring rotating. Picked up to 140 t, set back weight to 3 t on TH. Re-engaged retrieving tool. Picked up to 190 t hookload (120 t overpull) and pulled TH free. POOH w/ tubing and tubing hanger on THRT and 5 1/2" DP stand from 21 to surface and landed TH in c-plate. Attempted to break out TDS, observed high torque on motor. Confirmed IBOPs not breaking out. Broke out from stand, 20 - 30 kNm torque. Released THRT from tubing hanger. Required several attempts to come free. Inspected hanger, observed first quarter of actuator ring shoulder bent upwards. R/B stand and THRT in aft PRS. Removed communication collar and BPV from hanger. R/U 7" tubing handling equipment and Weatherford power tong. Installed TH handling tool. P/U string and installed Weatherford compact spider slips. POOH and L/O 7" tubing from 2926 m to 1860 m, removed cable clamps and spooled control line/cable. Continued to POOH with 7" tubing. Classification: Open

# 4.3 NO 15/9-F-1 B, Permanent P&A (DP) w/ RIG (03.Feb.2014 20:30)

START: 03.Feb.2014 20:30 3147 mMD

END : 19.Feb.2014 21:00 3148 mMD

# SUMMARY

Attempted to P/U first 2 7/8" joint from HTS. Unable to tilt and close center latch elevator, bail rotator not designed for this type of elevator. Evaluated different options. Attempted to tilt elevator using tugger. Unable to latch elevator. Decided to go ahead with TDS repair/change out of torque encoder. RIH and sat asafelock plug at 619,9 m. Pulled casing hanger and 10 3/4" Tieback. Removed diverter. Pulled overshot and removed BOP tensioners. Nippled down BOP. Disconnected surface riser and pulled same. Rigged down cylinders, tensioner ring and centralizer. Removed hatches and installed sheaves for guidewires. Ran guidewires and minireel. Disconnected production riser and pulled same. Had to wait due to weather window for running the HPDR. Ran drilling riser joints, tensioning joint and BOP joint. Ran down with riser until just above guide funnel. Lowered connector down inside guide funnel without any issues. Landed drilling riser connector and sat down 20 T. Installed hot stab. Pressured up by minireel to lock the connector. Performed OP test. Installed hatches and build scaffolding. Tested riser and connector to 100 bar. Installed centralizers and tensioning system. Moved BOP and installed diverter. Finalized nippling of BOP. Performed connector test to 232 bar. P/U Safe Lock retrieving tool and RIH, stopped at 170 m and displaced riser to 1,37 OBM. Continued RIH to above plug. Established circulation and latched onto plug. Released same. POOH and L/D plug and pulling tool. P/U and M/U cement stinger with a muleshoe. RIH 350 m 2 7/8" tubing and continued on 5 1/2" DP. Washed down and tagged EVO plug at 3148 m. Pulled up and conditioned mud. Placed a 300 m cement plug. Pulled out of cement and circulated btm up before POOH. M/U and RIH with 8 1/2" Clean-out assy. Washed down and drilled cement to confirmed hard cement at 2878 m. Confimed same by setting down 10 T. Circulated btm up and POOH to 1457 m. Circulated to take down MW from 1,38 to 1,28 SG. Continued POOH with 8 1/2" Clean-out assy. M/U and RIH with a 9 5/8" cutting assembly to 1457 m MD. Cut the 9 5/8" casing with 800 lpm, and POOH and layed down assembly. P/U a jet sub and a lockdown assembly retrieval tool and RIH to retrieve the 10 3/4" lockdown assembly. Experienced 2 mis-runs due to problems with the retrieval tool before retrieving the lockdown assembly on the 3rd attempt, with 20 ton overpull. After retrieving the lockdown assembly, the extension sleeve was removed from the retrieval tool, and the tool RIH again to retrieve the 10 3/4" seal assembly. The assembly was retrieved on the 1st attempt, with 12 ton overpull, with no trapped pressure below. A 10 3/4" fishing spear was then P/U and RIH, engaging the 10 3/4" casing right below the hanger. The cut 10 3/4" x 9 5/8" casing was pulled free without any overpull, and the well circulated to 1.40 sg OBM before pulling and retrieving the casing at surface. The 13 3/8" wearbushing was installed, and a 13 3/4" cutting BHA RIH and the 13 3/8" casing cut at 1399 m MD with 1000 lpm and 80 rpm. The BHA was POOH before retrieving the 13 3/8" wearbushing and seal assembly, no trapped pressure below. A 13 3/8" spear assembly was P/U and RIH, engaging the 13 3/8" casing at 140 m, and the cut 13 3/8" casing pulled free and retrieved at surface with no overpull. The nominal bore protector was installed, and a 5 1/2" cement stinger RIH to top of the cut 10 3/4" casing at 1457 m MD. The well was circulated and the mud conditioned for setting a Hi-Vis bentonite pill and the KO cement plug. The Hi-Vis pill was spotted from top of the cut 10 3/4" casing to the top of the cut 13 3/8" casing before pulling above and setting a balanced cement kick-off plug to 50 m inside the 20" casing, at 1298 m MD. Described in Liner section below. # MWD/BHA

A Hughes Christensen TD606X type bit, IADC M233 was selected by Baker for this section. The bit was part of the misrun BHA and was re-run on run # 2. A Baker Hughes 6 3/4" AutoTrak steering unit was run together with a Baker Hughes MWD and LWD (logging while drilling) including radioactive sources for formation evaluation, directional control and vibration monitoring purposes. 5 ½" heavy weight drill pipe (HWDP) and 5 ½" drill pipe were used above the MWD/LWD string. # CEMENTING

Pumped 15 m3 of 1.56 sg spacer w/700 l/min/83-58 bar. Isolated rig pumps from cement line, lined up and applied 30 bar against IBOP with rig pumps. Lined up through cement hose and verified line-up. Dropped dart # 1 for bottom plug. Mixed and pumped 18.4 m3 1.90 sg cement slurry w/650 l/min. Pumped 890 l drill water after cement job. Rotated liner while pumping cement, but had to stop rotation due to leaking swivel. Released dart # 2 for top plug. Displaced cement with 900 l/min. Slowed down to 700 l/min prior to first dart releasing bottom WP at 570 strokes, 61 bar SPP. Continued pumping with 900 l/min/35-96 bar. Reduced pump rate at 1350 strokes to 700 l/min/67 bar. Observed second dart releasing top WP. Continued displacement with 1000 l/min. Established liner rotation with 20 rpm prior to cement entering the annulus. Lowered rate to 800 l/min after experiencing loss tendencies. Slowed down to 500 l/min on the last 2 m3. Bumped the plug after 2430 strokes/52.7 m3, 95% pump efficiency, 120 bar. Increased pressure to 190 bar with rig pumps. Equalized with cement unit and pressure tested the liner to 300 bar/10 min. Classification: Open

# 4.8 NO 15/9-F-1 C, Liner (15.Mar.2014 16:30)

START: 15.Mar.2014 16:30 4094 mMD

END: 18.Mar.2014 16:15 4087 mMD

# OBJECTIVE

Run and cement the 7" liner

# SUMMARY

P/U 7" reamer shoe joint. M/U shoe joint, 3 x intermediate joints and float/landing collar joint and RIH to 59 m. Tested shoe float holding, filled the liner and checked for flow through. Installed swedge and verified circulation before it was layed out. 92 standard joints was run, together with 3 pups for later correlation. The liner was RIH on a 5 1/2" HWD and 5 1/2" DP landingstring, broke circulation at the 9 5/8" casing shoe and circulated one annulus volume. Continued to RIH until 4036 m MD. Established circulation and washed and worked the liner down to 4075m MD w/ 250-450 LPM and 30-60 bar. Took weight and experienced pack-offs. Pulled above restrictions to clear pack-off and worked the string down. Continue to wash and work the string to 4081m MD, reamed down w/ 20 RPM to 4084m MD and was able to get the string to 4090 m MD. Decided to set the liner at 4087m MD (6m shallower than planned depth). Classification: Open

# Volve Field

# Rev. no. # 1 Well objectives and results

# 1.1 Well objectives

The objective of the well is to produce oil from the Hugin Formation in the Northwest (NW) segment. The well planning includes geosteering as a means to optimally place the well within the reservoir, aiming to connect up the different fault blocks, maximise reservoir length and cover the full Hugin stratigraphy to allow for flexibility in perforation strategy when the well is completed. In addition the geosteering will minimize the length of any non-productive intervals, mitigating the risk of exit into either the Heather Formation above or Sleipner Formation below, especially when drilling across faults where the throw is uncertain. The main reference wells in the NW segment are pilot 15/9-F-11 A and failed water injector 15/9-F-1 B, which will help to guide the geosteering. The well is planned to enter the Hugin Formation in an upflank position within the NW segment, from where it will be geosteered as required, and will cross several prognosed faults as it is drilled down flank within the NW with TD based on crossing the NW-SE oriented Volve Main Field boundary fault and entering the Heather Formation. # Top Hugin depth map 2013

# 1.3 Well results

The sidetrack 15/9-F-1 C reached TD on 14.03.2014 (after crossing the field boundary fault ‘F5’ (at 4004 m MD RKB) at 4094 m MD / 3180.5 m TVD RKB in the Heather Formation. The well entered the Hugin reservoir within the NW segment most likely crossing the prognosed fault ‘F1’ and found water-filled Hugin Formation in this upflank NW section, contrary to pre-drill expectation. The well continued to drill water-filled Hugin Formation in the upflank section before crossing the largest prognosed fault ‘F3’ and entering the ‘F-11 A block’ which was oil-filled. The well did not exit the Hugin Formation on crossing the fault ‘F3’ which was highlighted as one of the main pre-drill risks; instead drilled sand-sand contact crossing from Lower Hugin in the upflank footwall, into Upper Hugin in the hanging wall of fault ‘F3’. This was one of the main objectives of the pre-drill well planning, to ensure a section within Upper

# Final Well Report

Doc. No. Well NO 15/9-F-1 C

Volve Field

Valid from

Rev. no. Hugin in the ‘F-11 A block’. The saturation change from water to oil across the fault ‘F3’, despite a sand-sand juxtaposition, may be attributed to drainage effects in the upflank area which is communicating with existing producers in the Volve Main Field, 15/9-F-14 and 15/9-F-12. After achieving an Upper Hugin section in the ‘F-11 A block’ the well was steered down in relation to the dipping Hugin Formation and drilled the full Hugin package from Upper Hugin then crossing the ‘offshore-shale’ into the thief zone unit (zones 2.3; 2.2 and 2.1) and eventually into the Lower Hugin zones in a down flank location in the ‘F-11 A block’. After meeting the objective of drilling a Lower Hugin section in the ‘F-11 A block’ the well was geosteered up relative to formation dip so as not to exit into Sleipner Formation below and in order to build and enter the Hugin Formation in the ‘F-1 B block’ across fault ‘F4’. The well drilled from Lower Hugin in ‘F-11 A block’ across the fault ‘F4’ as prognosed, and into the Lower Hugin in the ‘F-1 B block’. The well-TD was set in the Heather Formation, after crossing the Volve Main Field boundary fault ‘F5’ down flank to the northeast, which came in later than prognosed and indicated a larger volume than prognosed in the ‘F-1 B block’; and the additional reservoir length allowed for more perforation intervals in this block. Overall, the total geosteered section in the Hugin Formation was 774 m MD long. Excluding water flooded upflank section, a total of 500 m MD in oil-bearing productive Hugin Formation were encountered in the F-11 A and F-1 B blocks. Contrary to pre-drill expectations and modelling, no well exits into the overlying Heather Formation were encountered during geosteering. Initial perforations are placed in good to intermediate quality sandstones in the Upper Hugin and mid-thief-zone (2.2) in ‘F-11 A block’ and in Lower Hugin in the ‘F-1 B block’. A total of 207 m were perforated in 13 runs (3 runs with 9 m and 10 runs with 18 m). Production was started Q2 2014 with an initial water-free rate of ~1500 Sm³/d; but this decreased to ~900 Sm³/d within a few weeks. Classification: Internal

# 2.1 Geological setting and results

Reservoir section

The well entered the Hugin Formation within the NW segment most likely crossing the prognosed fault ‘F1’ and found water-filled Hugin reservoir in this upflank NW section, contrary to pre-drill expectation. The well continued to drill water-filled Hugin Formation in the upflank section before crossing the largest prognosed fault ‘F3’ and entering oil-filled Upper Hugin reservoir in the ‘F-11 A block’. The well did not exit the Hugin Formation on crossing the fault ‘F3’ which was highlighted as one of the main pre-drill risks; instead drilled a sandstone-sandstone contact crossing from Lower Hugin in the upflank footwall, into Upper Hugin in the hanging wall of fault ‘F3’. The saturation change from water to oil across the fault ‘F3’, despite a sandstone-sandstone juxtaposition, may be attributed to drainage effects in the upflank area which is communicating with existing producers in the Volve Main Field, 15/9-F-14 and 15/9-F-12. After achieving an Upper Hugin section in the ‘F-11 A block’ the well was steered down in relation to the NE dipping Hugin Formation and drilled the full Hugin package, from Upper Hugin then crossing the ‘offshore-shale’ into the thief zone unit (zones 2.3; 2.2 and 2.1) and eventually into the Lower Hugin zones in a down flank location in the ‘F-11 A block’. After meeting the objective of drilling a Lower Hugin section in the ‘F-11 A block’ the well was geosteered up relative to formation dip so as not to exit into Sleipner Formation below and in order to build and enter the Hugin Formation in the ‘F-1 B block’ across fault ‘F4’. The well drilled from Lower Hugin in ‘F-11 A block’ across the fault ‘F4’ as prognosed, and into the Lower Hugin in the ‘F-1 B block’. The well-TD was set in the Heather Formation, after crossing the Volve Main Field boundary fault ‘F5’ down flank to the northeast, which came in later than prognosed and indicated a larger volume in the ‘F-1 B block’; and the additional reservoir length allowed for more perforation intervals in this block. Table 2-1 provides the reservoir formation tops and zonation from Top Hugin Formation to well-TD. The reservoir zonation is based on all available log information, mainly real-time image log interpretation from azimuthal gamma ray (GR) and azimuthal density (by LithoTrak). Data are compared to a pre-drill Baker RNS model and offset wells and re-matched in real-time during drilling in cooperation with PETEK G&G. The zonation is somewhat uncertain in some sections; mainly upflank, as the well enters a thief zone (2.1) across a fault; and the down flank resolution of the individual Lower Hugin sections; but overall the confidence is high and the zonation assisted by correlation with the existing wells in the NW, 15/9-F-11 A and 15/9-F-1 B. The main pre-drill risks and uncertainties in the NW included:

- Uncertainty on main faults, both lateral position along the planned well in m MD as well as throw component

Rev. no. # 2.2  Seismic interpretation versus well results

In the 15/9-F-1 C oil producer Top Hugin Formation came in - 3 m TVD shallower than prognosed depth, well inside the uncertainty on the depth estimate, which was ± 15 m TVD. Sonic logging or VSP was neither planned nor performed in this high deviated well. The well was expected to encounter 3-6 faults along the reservoir section. The drilled wellpath proved a total of 7 faults, all with generally less fault throws than the prognosed faults. Faults F-3, F-4 and F-5 were confirmed in the well. F5 was encountered 50 m MD after progosis. The other faults encountered were all below seismic resolution. A seismic reservoir section with old and updated interpretation is illustrated in Figure 2-2. Rev. no. # 17 ½” section (1364 – 2514 m MD)

The inclination ranges in this section by 9.5°-13.3°, 21.6° (start-end, max). The sidetrack was initiated by time drilling from 1364 m MD. The well was then turned left from 300° to 230° azimuth and into a 430 m tangent section at near 20° inclination. At 1481 m MD a survey was taken and a planned wiper trip into the casing shoe was performed to check the hole condition. This along with subsequent trips showed no indications in the shoe/sidetrack area. Several times the ROP was controlled to below optimum due to cuttings handling and only two pumps online. Drilling the Grid sandstones demanded higher bit weight and gave more torque in general. It was not possible to downlink while drilling with more than 5 t weight on bit due to stalling the rotary, so the weight was picked off for downlinking on bottom. Drilling into the Grid Formation showed a noticeable increase in over pull on connections. Drilling the Balder Formation gave higher stick slip in general, but did not affect the ROP. A hard stringer on top of the Sele Formation was observed. At 2360 m MD the well path continued with a right turn and a build started ending at TD with 13.3° inclination and 26.6° azimuth. # 12 ¼” section (2514-3056 m MD)

The inclination ranges in this section by 16.9°-57.6°, 57.6° (start-end, max). Drilling was commenced from 2514 m steering with DLS of 2.6°/30 m build while drilling the Lista and the Ty formations. In the harder Tor and Hod limestones an increased WOB to 20 t was required to achieve a ROP of around 20-25 m/h. Also the build rate dropped dramatically and for most of the time 80 to 87% build force was required to achieve the same build rate of 2.6°/30 m. However towards the end of the run, in the Hod Formation, it was necessary to use 100% build force and hold back the ROP to 20 m/h, despite of that the well path ended up 2 m below the plan at TD. Some erratic torque was observed while drilling the Hod Formation. Attempted to run the Gyro, but due to no communication with the tool on surface this was cancelled. Throughout the run the vibration was negligible. The CoPilot data was used to correct the surface WOB to the downhole WOB. Both matched perfectly indicating good weight transfer to the bit. Average ROP for the run was 23.5 m/h. # 8 ½” section (3056-3059 m & 3059-4094 m)

The inclination ranges in this section by 61.0°-69.1°, 84.1° (start-end, max). 1st run: Drilled out cement and 3 m new formation. After FIT was performed, it was observed that the communication with the AutoTrak was lost and decided to POOH. 2nd run: Initial build response was low. High build forces used to drill in the Hod, Hidra, Rødby formations to achieve the required build rate of 3°/30 m without sacrificing the ROP. More or less continuous stick slip of severity level 5 was observed down to 3164 m MD. Except for this, drilling dynamics for all mode stayed within the expected window. The Draupne Formation was drilled with reduced flow rate due to hole stability concerns. Continued operation in Heather Formation until 3229 m, where Hugin reservoir was entered after a fault. Geosteered at ROP of ~29 m/h. Built inclination to 84° before starting to drop from 3376 m with DLS of up to 3.4°/30 m. The Hugin formation was drilled with relative low weight. Section/well TD was set at 4094 m. Classification: Internal

Rev. no.

MD. Well trajectory was deviated significant (TVD-wise) from the original plan due to geosteering but all directional and geological objectives were fulfilled. # 2.3.2 Geosteering experiences

The pre-model from Baker Hughes’ RNS indicated high contrast towards top reservoir, i.e. Heather Formation and very low contrasts internally, similar to previous recent geosteered wells, e.g. 15/9-F-15 D. Several faults along the well path were prognosed, causing down-faulted reservoir segments. With uncertainty on the throw component there was a risk of exit into Heather Formation above and Sleipner Formation below. For dip calculations the near-bit azimuthal GR image data and azimuthal DEN image data (24 m behind bit) were used. Geosteering decisions were made by the PETEK and the RNS team. The RNS supervisor was present in the Statoil office in Bergen during office hours, while RNS support outside office hours was covered by the BEACON centre in Stavanger. In total, 9 real-time geosteering decisions were performed. Changes in trajectory were done in order to optimise wellbore positioning in the reservoir, both to maximise reservoir length whilst joining up potential different fault blocks and to achieve specific well objectives such as targeting an Upper Hugin section after crossing fault ‘F3’ and a Lower Hugin section down flank before crossing fault ‘F4’. The near bit GR with 4 sector image helped significantly while geosteering, providing early information on drilling relative up/down stratigraphy. Overall, the total geosteered section in the Hugin Formation was 774 m MD long. Excluding water flooded upflank section, a total of 500 m MD in oil-bearing productive Hugin reservoir were encountered in the ‘F-11 A’ and ‘F-1 B’ blocks. Contrary to pre-drill expectations, no well exits into the overlying Heather Formation were encountered during geosteering, apart from the planned exit across the boundary fault ‘F5’ down flank. In Figure 2-5 the interpretation from Statoil PETEK is given, according to the recent valid geological understanding. Differences to the interpretation from Baker Hughes’ RNS in Figure 2-6 can be remarked. Both will be kept to provide the base for possible further investigations and reviews. PETEK’s interpretation today is based on:

- In the regional extensional regime more normal than reverse faults are expected and can be determined. Also less faults can be assumed; to explain thickness variation not necessary structural events rather sedimentological changes have triggered them. - The properties of the distinct Hugin zone varies field-wide, thus to base the RES responses on just 1 reference well, can cause to strong restriction. Especially in Lower Hugin (zones 1.6-1.1) the internal zonation can not be correlated field-wide with high certainty. Classification: Internal

# 3.5 Pressure points

A total of 13 pressures measurements were taken along the well path, proving all 3 different blocks (Table 3-1). This was performed while POOH after TD on 16ᵗʰ of March 2014. At the time of drilling 15/9-F-1 C producer both water injectors in the Volve Main Field (15/9-F-4 and 15/9-F-5) were in operation, according to plan and maintaining the reservoir pressure at a level ~10-12 bar higher than initial. In well 15/9-F-1 C the pressure points can be grouped into two main gradients, a shallow upflank section with relative lower pressures corresponding well with Volve Main Field level and the other corresponding to the downflank section with relatively higher pressures. The interpretation is that the upflank area is in good communication with the Volve Main Field (+12 bar) and as a result, initial oil in this area had already been drained by existing producers 15/9-F12 and F-14 by the time F-1 C was drilled. The increased pressure deeper and further downflank can be attributed to earlier water injection in 15/9-F-1 B. The pressure was much higher downflank after water injection in F-1 B but since this was stopped, the pressure is gradually bleeding back to equilibrium with the Volve Main Field, but had not quite reached an equilibrium at the time of the logging in F-1 C and was therefore higher (+15 bar) relative to the shallower section of the Northwest. Rev. no. # 5 Pore pressures and hole stability

There were no indications that the prognosis were wrong. No observations of higher pore pressure or cavings or hole instability have been reported. # 5.1 Formation pressure above the reservoir

The prediction of the pore pressure gradient (PP) above the reservoir is based on the Eaton Sonic method. - A hydrostatic pore pressure gradient is expected down to top Ty Formation. The pore pressure within permeable Ty sandstones was measured in wells 15/9-F-12 and 15/9-F-15 S as 0.87 sg. This indicates some 40 bar pressure depletion caused by production from the northern Sleipner Øst area. Recent pressure data from Sleipner well 15/9-A-24, the well closest to the Volve Field, show stable pressure since 2005, in line with the stop of production in the northern Sleipner Øst area. The pore pressure in the Ty Formation within the Volve area is expected to be 0.87 sg, as measured in 2007. - In the underlying upper part of the Shetland Group the pore pressure is assumed to increase gently up to 1.05 sg within the Hod Formation. For the rest of the Shetland and the Cromer Knoll Group a rapid increase in the pore pressure to 1.26 sg is prognosed. - The maximum pore pressure within the Viking Group is expected to be 1.29 sg in the organic rich Draupne shales at the top. The pore pressure within the underlying Heather Formation is expected to be lower. # 5.2 Pore pressure in the reservoir

The reservoir pressure in the Hugin Formation Volve Main Field is currently maintained at approximately 12 bar over initial pressure by injecting water downflank in wells 15/9-F-4 and 15/9-F-5. Throughout the field an oil gradient of 0,0704 bar/m (datum depth 2855 m TVD, 320 bar) is estimated. Pressure measurements were undertaken in all 3 penetrated blocks (Chapter 3.5). In general 2 pressure levels have been differentiated. 12 bar overpressure compared to initial Volve Main Field oil gradient, thus in good communication to the Volve Main Field, have been observed in the upflank block with partly water-flooded Hugin reservoir and the oil-filled Hugin reservoir in the upper part of the ‘F-11 A’ block. However, 15 bar overpressure compared to initial Volve Main Field oil gradient encountered deeper, in the oil-filled Hugin reservoir in the lower part of the ‘F-11 A’ block and the ‘F-1 B’ block, is because of the former short injection test periods in well 15/9-F-1 B. The pressure was much higher and is gradually bleeding back to equilibrium with the Volve Main Field. It was not quite there at the time of the logging. Rev. no. # 5.3 Formation strength

The overburden gradient (OBG) is calculated from a density curve from well 15/9-F-12. A minimum horizontal stress gradient has been calculated from a Breckels and van Eekelen relation based on a method from Per Horsrud (2011). Initial reservoir pressure is used. - Formation strength in the Balder Formation is reduced due to the presence of tuff layers. Nevertheless, XLOT data from well 15/9-F-14 showed a minimum horizontal stress gradient (ShG) of 1.68 sg in the upper part of the Balder Formation. In the lower 32 m of the Balder Formation the formation strength is reduced to 1.55 sg to account for local experiences. - Within the Ty Formation the formation strength is assumed to be reduced due to depletion, but should not cause any hole stability problems. It is well above the anticipated pore pressure in the overburden section. - The Minifrac data from Hugin Formation in well 15/9-19 A was revaluated in 2010. The result is a 19 bar reduction in the minimum horizontal stress gradient for the Hugin reservoir and the formations below. - The minimum horizontal stress gradient at initial pressure has been calculated. The fracture gradient for the Volve area is set as 5% above the minimum horizontal strength, according to the model. - In the upper part of the Balder Formation an adjustment to only 1.73 sg was done to account for reduced formation strength due to the presence of tuff layers. A collapse gradient has been generated based on the method of Stassi d’Alia. Included in this method are the initial pore pressure, the minimum horizontal stress, considerations of the UCS (unconfined compressive strength), and the well path. - The collapse curve in the Nordland and upper Hordaland Group shows a general increase towards the hydrostatic pore pressure and surpasses it in the Skade Formation. The 36” & 26” sections were drilled with 1.03 sg seawater/spud mud successfully. - The Hordaland shales are normally associated with high smectite content and under-compaction, causing instability and possibly higher pore pressure in the lower Hordaland Group. However, in the Volve area the sandstones of the Grid Formations are generally present, acting as a pressure vent and thereby preventing abnormal pore pressure. Nevertheless, shales within the Hordaland Group have not been observed as unstable, when drilled at high inclinations and particularly when exposed to water based drilling fluid. - - The UCS has been updated/increased while planning and executing the 2ⁿᵈ drilling campaign and re-evaluating results from the 1st campaign. Affected intervals are in the lower Hordaland Group and the lower Lista Formation. No incidents have been observed in the Cromer Knoll Group. - The organic-rich Draupne Formation, and particularly the upper part, generally represents an interval with risk for tight hole and collapse. No incidents have been observed when drilling with 1.32 sg standard OBM. However, the highest inclination drilled was 71º. - Only minor claystones have been observed in the sandstones of the Viking, Vestland and Hegre groups. No incidents have been reported. # 6.2.1 Corrections, editing

* Note that in the 8 ½” section a re-scaled NearBit GR (NBGRCFM) is spliced into GR for the last ~ 9 m. NearBit GR has been re-scaled by -8 API in order to match GRCFM level. Note in general that the final «average» density curve - RHOB (BDCFM) - from Baker is weighted with respect to which of the 16 sectors / bins that provide the best density measurement (smallest standoff). NPHI (NPCKLFM) is in limestone units, Caliper and salinity corrected and filtered. Logtek’s RDEP (Resistivity Deep) curve = RPCELM, named RD in the OW Volve project. Logtek’s RMED (Resistivity Medium) curve = RPCEHM, named RM in the OW Volve project. Note that the RT curve used for saturation calculation, has been edited in intervals where RPCEHM is affected by polarization horns or is saturated. This editing is kept on a specific curve in Geolog, named RT_EDIT. # 6.2.2 Depth shifts, splicing

No depth shifting has been applied, except in limited overlap intervals around kick off depth and hole sections in Overburden. These depth shifts and the splicing of the individual runs are reported in Logtek’s info file; WLC_COMPOSITE_1_INF_1.PDF which is stored in Petrobank. All depths are referenced to Driller’s depth. Logtek’s general “philosophy” with respect to depth shifting in the transition zone between a mother well and a kick off well is as follows: aim to keep the stop-coupled depths as shallow as possible, preferably at KOP. But sometimes it has to be sat deeper (a few meters deeper, preferably in a shale if possible) due to a) if overlapping curves are not on depth at KOP, or b) if the depth shifts have been so large, that the logs else will

# 6.3.1 General

Petrophysical evaluation is performed according to the Volve petrophysical field model, described in report: “Sleipner Øst and Volve Model 2006, Hugin and Skagerrak Formation, Petrophysical Evaluation”. November 2006. Author: Elin Solfjell, Karl Audun Lehne. The petrophysical evaluation software used is Geolog, and the Geolog project is SLEIPNER_OST (at Stavanger server: FROST_SVG). # 6.3.2 Porosity

Total porosity, PHIF (f), is derived from the density log, which is calibrated to overburden corrected core porosity for wells drilled with either OBM or WBM. The neutron log, NPHI, has been used to correct for varying mud filtrate invasion. f = fD + A × (NPHI - fD) + B

where:

- f = rma - rb [fraction]
- rma is the matrix density [g/cm³]
- rb is the measured bulk density (RHOB), [g/cm³]
- rfl is the pore fluid density [g/cm³]. - A and B are regression coefficients
- NPHI: neutron log in limestone units [fraction]

Rev. no. # 6.3.3 Shale volume, VSH

To determine VSH, the standard model VSHGR from linear GR relationship is applied:

VSH = VSHGR =  GR - GRmin

GRmax - GRmin

where:

- GR = gamma ray reading [API]
- GRmin = gamma ray reading in clean sand [API]
- GRmax = gamma ray reading in shale [API]

VSH is used quantitatively in deriving permeability, and quantitatively indirectly in Netsand cutoff. In order to pick GRmin and GRmax in a more objective way, and to avoid over-estimating of VSH, GR is “normalized” against VSHDN (VSH from DEN/NEU) by cross plotting GR versus VSHDN. VSHDN is considered to be the most reliable shale indicator (except in intervals dominated by mica and heavy minerals). This shale indicator compares porosity derived from the density log with porosity from the neutron log. If the neutron log reads higher porosity than the density, is this believed to be due to the hydrogen content of present clay minerals. VSHDN is derived from the response equation for the neutron log:

VCLDN = NPHICS - (PHIT * HIFL)

HICL

VSHDN = VCLDN

(1- PHISH)

where:

- VCLDN = Dry shale volume from density / neutron logs [fraction]
- NPHICS = Neutron log corrected to sandstone matrix [fraction]
- PHIT = Total log porosity [fraction]
- HIFL = Hydrogen Index to fluid
- HICL = Hydrogen Index to clay
- PHISH = Shale porosity [fraction]

# 6.3.4 Water saturation

Water saturation is calculated using Archie equation, giving a total water saturation:

Sw = a ¥ Rw · 1

t = f ¥ m ¥ Rt

where:

- a = Archie (tortuosity) factor
- Rw = resistivity of formation water [Ohmm]
- f = total porosity [fraction]
- m = cementation exponent
- Rt = true resistivity [Ohmm]
- n = saturation exponent

# 6.3.5 Permeability

The horizontal log permeability, KLOGH, is derived from the following equation based on multivariable regression analysis between log porosity and shale volume (“normalised” against VSHDN) against overburden corrected core permeability:

Heather Formation: KLOGH = 10(²⁺⁸¥PHIF⁻⁹¥VSH)

Hugin Formation: KLOGH = 10(²⁺⁸¥PHIF⁻⁹¥VSH)

WOW for visibility to improve on template. Engage hot-stab for centraliser system and operated the rams for F-14 and retracted same.

Circulated SW at 250 lpm increased to 1000 lpm in 250 lpm steps. Still only returns coming out from F-7. Pumped 30 m³ 1.7 SG mud, 1000 lpm, 5.7 bars 85 MT string weight. # Summary of activities (24 Hours)

Positioned 30" conductor at cementing depth at 196.8 m. Mixed and pumped 106 m³ 1.52 SG Tuned Light XL cement slurry.
Got returns up F-7, but after 70 m³ pumped, had full returns up F-14. Displaced cement slurry with 6.5 m³ of SW. Waited on cement to set.

Meanwhile performed general maintenance. Lined up for flushing of top-up line. # Summary of planned activities (24 Hours)

Test top-up system and perform top-up cement if required. Release top-up line and CART tool. Continue top hole on F-5. # Operations

# Summary of activities (24 Hours)

Pressure tested UWG top-up system using the cement unit, rupture disk burst at 53 bars (769 psi). Performed cement top-up job, pumped 10.4 m³, 1.56 SG G-cement 82 bars at 500 lpm.
Released with CART tool and POOH with the landing string.

Prepared for measuring the double on top of the CART tool. Laser measuring device was dropped onto the rig floor. Racked back the CART tool in derrick. Laid out the aluminium inner string. Prepared for skidding of the cantilever from F-14 to F-5. # Summary of planned activities (24 Hours)

Start operation on F-5 36" top hole. Prepared the 36" BHA. drill the 36" hole. # Operations

# Summary of activities (24 Hours)

Installed centralizers, tensioner ring and boat collision stool. Transferred tension from TDS to tension cylinders. Installed half-moon hatch on F-5. Erected scaffolding tower around Claxton tool. Integrity tested HPDR20/195 bar for 5/10 min. Ok. Disconnected and LD Claxton tool and landing string. Started to NUBOP/diverter. # Summary of planned activities (24 Hours)

NUBOP/diverter and pressure test BOP/diverter/connector/20" csg. MU and RIH with 12 1/4" BHA. RIH with 12 1/4" BHA on 5 1/2" DP to 1370 m MDRT. # Operations

Statoil Final Well Report
# Well NO 15/9-F-11, F-11 T2, F-11 A, F-11 B

# 1 Well objectives and result

# 1.1 Well objectives

Objectives of the 15/9-F-11 exploration pilot well were to prove oil in the Volve North prospect and to define the field OWC. If the pilot would find an oil-down to situation (ODT), an optional openhole side-track further down flank was planned. In addition the pilot was placed such that it could prove a shallower Hugin interpretation based on the previous 2009 interpretation, which represented an additional prospect upside. An oil fluid sample was planned to collect in any commercial find scenario. Objectives of the second pilot 15/9-F-11 A were to prove oil and sufficient reservoir thickness in the Northwest fault segment. If the pilot found an oil-water contact (OWC), a water sample would be taken to prove for initial (perched water) or flooded conditions. If the 15/9-F-11 North pilot well would encounter a sufficient oil column in Volve North segment, well 15/9-F-11 B would be drilled to an optimal location further upflank and completed as an oil producer. If 15/9-F-11 North pilot would find no or insufficient oil in the North prospect, 15/9-F-11 B would be sidetracked to an infill location between existing producers 15/9-F-12 and 15/9-F-14 in the main field and downflank towards existing injector 15/9-F-5. The aim was to geosteer the well in the upper part of the Hugin reservoir, which was expected to be only partially drained. Figure 1-1 shows the pre-drill Top Hugin map and the planned well paths. Statoil Final Well Report
# Well NO 15/9-F-11, F-11 T2, F-11 A, F-11 B

# 1.3 Well results

# 1.3.1 15/9-F-11 / 15/9-F-11 T2 pilot Volve North prospect

The well reached on May 4ᵗʰ 2013 at 4561.8 m MD / 3345.2 m MSL its total depth (TD) in the Sleipner Formation. It encountered water-filled Hugin with some residual oil on logs and cuttings. Top Hugin Formation was encountered 27 m TVD deeper than expected at 3181 m MSL. Full Hugin stratigraphy was present (104 m TVD / ~98 m TVT thickness). Reservoir properties were slightly poorer than expected, compared to offset wells at similar depth. Pressure measurements were showing a reservoir pressure 13-14 bar over initial, relative to 15/9-19 BT2 and 15/9-F-15 A water gradient, but in accordance with the main field pressure, which was ~12 bar over initial at the time. No hydrocarbon indications in Upper Jurassic were observed. The well proved a dry Volve North prospect. Pre-drill prospect evaluation assumed a Pg of 34% with main risk elements trap geometry (55%) due to poor seismic control and source migration (70%) given a possible charge through the Upper Jurassic. The prospect model included a potentially deep OWC contact (down to 3200 m MSL with no OWC drilled on Volve). Post-drill well results support a migration fairway through the Hugin Formation into the main field. The prospect trap however failed due to depth and geometry. Pressure measurements indicate that the aquifer communicates with the main field. Top Hugin Formation at 3181 m MSL can be considered a water-up to (WUT) observation for the main field. A dry North prospect resulted in the cancellation of an immediate 15/9-F-11 B North producer and subsequent 15/9-F-1 A injector option. # 1.3.2 15/9-F-11 A pilot Volve Northwest segment

The planned sidetrack reached TD on May 21ˢᵗ 2013 at 3762.0 m MD / 3071.6 m MSL in the Sleipner Formation. It encountered oil-filled Hugin Formation at 2944 m MSL, thus 25 m TVD deeper than expected. Full Hugin stratigraphy was present (82 m TVD / ~64 m TVT thickness). Reservoir properties were good, but generally higher water saturations than in main field oil-bearing wells were observed. Initial pressure measurements were ~12 bar over initial and in accordance with main field pressure at the time. This indicates pressure communication to the main field. Time lapse pressure points were taken 48 h later, after injection in main field was closed. This led to 6 bar pressure drop in the main field well 15/9-F-15 C. In the 15/9-F-11 A pilot a ~0.2-0.9 bar pressure drop was observed, which was consistent with prognosis from the simulation model. The well proved a thick upside oil column in the NW segment, which is in pressure communication with the main field. In-place oil volumes in the segment are estimated with ~1.4 MSm³. Rev. no. Well results triggered planning and drilling of a dedicated injector from slot 15/9-F-1. The injector is supposed to drive oil towards the existing upflank producer 15/9-F-14 in the main field. # 1.3.3 15/9-F-11 B IOR producer Volve Main Field

The planned sidetrack reached TD on June 6th 2013 at 4770.3 m MD / 3203 m MSL in the Sleipner Formation. It encountered an oil-filled Upper Hugin (zone 3) in heel and toe with good reservoir qualities similar to offset wells. The total geosteered section in the Hugin Formation was ~1220 m MD long. A total of ~760 m MD in gross oil-bearing productive Upper Hugin were encountered. Some non-productive Heather Formation and Hugin Thief Zone (zone 2) intervals were drilled, mainly due to predominantly sub-seismic faulting. Towards the toe, the well was dropped into water-flooded Thief Zone and Lower Hugin (zone 1) due to steering tool failure. TD was set in the Sleipner Formation. An exit of the field through the main fault, as expected, was not observed. With high oil saturation in both toe and heel, the well encountered upside volumes in the targeted Upper Hugin zone. Expectation was that the heel would be water-filled as indicated by reservoir simulation and 4D seismic, while mainly the downflank toe section would encounter undrained oil. At ~4700 m MD / 3147 m MSL a marked drop in resistivity in the Lower Hugin may indicate that the well penetrated the initial field OWC. Water-filled zones just above showed resistivities, which are consistent with injected Utsira water (+/ produced water). Initial perforations were placed in good to intermediate quality Upper Hugin sandstones in the toe of well with optional later perforation in the heel. A total of 93 m were perforated in 8 runs. Production was started July 24th 2013 with an initial nearly water-free rate of ~1000 Sm³/d. Drawdown is limited to avoid early water breakthrough. # 2.1 Geological setting and results

# 2.1.1 15/9-F-11 T2 pilot Volve North prospect

Overburden

Figure 2-1 shows the stratigraphy in the pilot from the Hod Formation down to TD in the Sleipner Formation. Prior to entering the Hugin reservoir the well encountered a thick package of Upper Jurassic Draupne and Heather formations as expected. No significant Intra Draupne sandstones were observed. Thin sandy stringers are mostly cemented. No indications for hydrocarbons on cuttings or logs were observed. Figure 2-1 also shows image log interpretation on memory data as delivered by Baker Hughes (reservoir navigation services, RNS) on memory data. Note that tadpoles are shown undifferentiated/unclassified for type or confidence level and hence have a high ‘noise level’ (due to picks of cemented intervals etc.). Overall, they are consistent with the expected structural grain. The well path is transported out in the Shetland Group and dropped into the downfaulted North prospect while crossing the major fault separating the prospect from the main field. Tadpoles down to the Draupne Formation indicate a generally North dipping structure with angles of ~10-20°. At the Heather Formation level structural dip jumps to ~50°, then decreasing with depth into the Hugin Formation to ~30°. This indicates a structural drag along the large fault to the South. Formation is dipping generally to the NNE to NE which is approximately consistent with the formation dip on the Top Hugin Formation depth map. Reservoir section

A complete Hugin stratigraphy was encountered in the well. Lithostratigraphic subzonation can easily be correlated with offset wells indicating no significant lateral facies changes. Figure 2-2 shows the reservoir section on logs with the Hugin subzonation in track 3. - Upper Hugin (zones 3.3-3.1): 4393-4415 m MD
- with ~4 m TVD thick offshore shale at base
- Middle Hugin or ‘Thief Zone’ (zones 2.3-2.1): 4415-4444 m MD
- with typical high permeability upper and lower zone as confirmed by high mobilities
- thick calcareous cemented interval at base (zone 2.1)
- Lower Hugin (zones 1.6-1.1): 4444-4497.4 m MD
- thick calcareous cemented interval at top (zone 1.6)

The thickness of the Hugin Formation was with 101 m TVD / ~98 m TVT approximately as expected. No indications for faults on image logs (Figure 2-2., image log tadpoles in track 11). Classification: Internal    Status: Final    Expiry date: 2010-12-30    Page 13 of 110

The reservoir section is overall very sandy, but has a reduced N/G of 80.8% compared to offset wells (mostly >85%) due to some thick calcareous cemented intervals in lower Thief Zone (zone 2.1) and upper Lower Hugin (zone 1.6). Average porosity of 16.8% is poorer compared to offset well 15/9-19 BT2 at similar depth. Reference to Chapter 6 for full petrophysical evaluation and CPI. # Pre-drill vs post-drill segment evaluation

Oil shows in the well are documented in Chapter 4. Residual oil intervals on logs are documented in Chapter 6. The presence of some hydrocarbons in the Hugin Formation supports a migration through the Hugin Formation from the kitchen in the North into the main field via the 15/9-F-11 T2 location. An additional fairway in Intra Draupne sandstones has not been proven. Pre-drill prospect evaluation assumed a Pg of 34% with main risk elements trap geometry (55%) due to poor seismic control and source migration (70%) given a possible charge through the Upper Jurassic. The prospect model included a potentially deep OWC contact (down to 3200 m MSL; no OWC drilled on Volve). With top reservoir coming in deeper, the area is below the Volve Field closure and was hence bypassed (Figure 1-2). Pressure points indicate that the drilled aquifer communicates with the main field (Chapter 3.5.1). Top Hugin Formation at 3181 m MSL can be considered a WUT observation for the main field. # Well NO 15/9-F-11, F-11 T2, F-11 A, F-11 B

# Volve Field

# 2.1.2 15/9-F-11 A pilot Volve Northwest segment

# Reservoir section

A complete Hugin stratigraphy was encountered in the well. Lithostratigraphic subzonation can easily be correlated with offset wells indicating no significant lateral facies changes. Fig 2.3 shows the reservoir section on logs with the Hugin subzonation in track 3. - Upper Hugin (zones 3.3-3.1): 3594.6-3612.5 m MD
- With ~3 m TVD thick offshore shale at base
- Middle Hugin or ‘Thief Zone’ (zones 2.3-2.1): 3612.5-3653 m MD
- with typical high permeability upper and lower zone as confirmed by high mobilities
- Lower Hugin (zones 1.6-1.1): 3653-3684 m MD

The thickness of the Hugin Formation was with 82 m TVD / ~64 m TVT near twice the expected. No direct indications for faults on RT image logs are observed (Figure 2-3; image log tadpoles track 11). Note that downfaulting would lead to an apparent thickening of the Hugin unit. The reservoir section is overall very sandy as indicated by a N/G of 86%. Average porosity of 20% is comparable to offset wells at similar depth. Reference to Chapter 6 for full petrophysical evaluation and CPI. Main differences to offset wells in the main field are the higher water saturations (Sw) observed consistently throughout the entire Hugin section. Average Sw is 32%, and hence >10% higher than in main field. Based on the fact that these high saturations are observed throughout the Hugin reservoir and not bound to individual zones, they are not considered drainage effects, and overall difficult to explain. # Pre-drill vs post-drill segment evaluation

Prior to drilling, the segment was risked with a Pg of 63%. The main risk elements were reservoir presence (70%) due to potential erosion and source migration (90%) given a charge through Hugin via the main field and a possible disconnect due to faulting. The well proved a thick upside oil column in the NW segment. Pressure points show full communication with the main field (Chapter 3.5.2). In-place oil volumes in the segment are estimated with ~1.4 MSm³. The main uncertainties for in-place volumes are thickness of Hugin further upflank from the 15/9-F-11 A location and the location of the major segment bounding faults especially also towards the salt collapse area in the South. # 2.1.3  15/9-F-11 B IOR producer Volve Main Field

# Reservoir section

Figure 2-4 shows the reservoir section with the reservoir zonation in track 4. Table 2-1 provides the zonation as list. Geosteering experiences are summarised in Chapter 2.3.4. Reservoir zonation is based on the curtain model provided by Baker Hughes’ realtime reservoir navigation service (RNS) using all available log information, specifically azimuthal resistivity readings (by AziTrak) and real-time image log interpretation from azimuthal gamma ray (GR) and azimuthal density (by LithoTrak). Data is compared to a pre-drill model and offset wells (mainly well 15/9-F-5), and re-matched in real-time during drilling in cooperation with PETEK G&G. Zonation documented in this report deviates from the final Baker Hughes delivery in some small intervals. Overall confidence is high, due to the Heather Formation and Thief Zone ‘calibration points’. The well encountered an oil-bearing Upper Hugin in heel and toe with good reservoir qualities similar to offset wells. The total geosteered section in the Hugin Formation was ~1220 m MD long. A total ~760 m MD in gross oil-bearing productive Upper Hugin (zones 3.1-3.3) was encountered. Non-productive Heather intervals were mainly due to predominantly sub-seismic faulting. Thief Zone intervals were water-flooded as expected. A short upflank interval of water flooded Upper Hugin at 3528-3539 m MD (zone 3.2) was not repeated further down in the well. Towards the toe, the well was dropped into water-flooded Thief Zone and Lower Hugin due to tool failure. TD was set in the Sleipner Formation. An exit of the field through the main fault, as expected, was not observed. With high oil saturation in both toe and heel, the well encountered upside volumes in the targeted Upper Hugin zone. Pre-drill expectation was that the heel would be water filled as indicated by reservoir simulation and 4D seismic, while mainly the downflank toe section would encounter undrained oil. At ~ 4700 m MD / 3147 m MSL a marked drop in resistivity to <0.7 Ohm.m in the Lower Hugin may indicate that the well penetrated the initial field OWC. Water-flooded zones just above show resistivities of approximately 1 Ohm.m, which are consistent with injected Utsira water (+/ produced water). # 2.2 Seismic interpretation versus well results

Sonic logs (both compressional and shear) were acquired in the two pilot wells 15/9-F-11 T2 and 15/9-F-11 A. Checkshots and VSP were acquired in 15/9-F-11 T2. # 2.2.1 15/9-F-11 T2 pilot Volve North prospect

Top and Base Hugin Formation depth prognosis in the North prospect were initially very uncertain. The seismic reflectivity in this area is very poor in all datasets and together with the presence of an undrilled thick Upper Jurassic package in this downfaulted segment the uncertainties on Top and Base Hugin Formation prognoses were set to ±70 m TVD. Top Hugin Formation came in +29 m TVD deeper and Base Hugin Formation came in +33 m TVD deeper than prognosis. Checkshot pairs and VSP (normal incident and rig source) were acquired. The synthetic seismic from well logs confirmed a poor seismic signal on Top Hugin Formation and a weak seismic signal on Base Hugin Formation compared to Base Hugin Formation on the main field (Figure 2-5). Using checkshots as time depth curve a deeper time interpretation of Top and Base Hugin Formation is initiated. This information has been implemented in the updated maps. VSP image data is processed by Schlumberger and preliminary results indicate a more northern placement of the large main bounding fault compared to initial seismic interpretation. These preliminary observations have also been implemented in the updated maps. It was not observed any improvement of Top and Base Hugin reflectors on the preliminary results. Final imaging results of VSP processing are estimated to be delivered during November 2013. Classification: Internal    Status: Final    Expiry date: 2010-12-30    Page 23 of 110

# 26” section

The inclination ranges in this section by 0.0°-2.5°, 2.5° (start-end, max). The Utsira Formation was entered at 887 m, where the inclination was build up to 24º. Rotating in order to observe behaviour resulted in a 2º drop. The remaining of the Utsira sandstones was drilled in orienting mode in an attempt to hold or build the inclination to 22º. Toolface control in Utsira Formation was ultimate difficult due to intermittent hardness of the formation was encountered, resulting in an uncontrollable deviation. Entering the Skade Formation the steering deteriorated even further. Very soft formations were encountered, making it impossible to rotate through it (losing angle). Steering required a lot of effort and time. Classification: Internal

# Volve Field

# 17 ½” section

The inclination ranges in this section by 22.9°-36.3°, 36.3° (start-end, max). The deviation was 22.9 º inclination and 278º azimuth at the start. The well trajectory was very far from the plan due to having been unable in the previous 26'' section to build and turn in unconsolidated sandstones of the Utsira and the Skade formations with a motor. To move progressively towards the original plan involved building inclination from 24º-35.8° and turning left from 283º-253° azimuth by 1592 m at a rate of 2.5°/30 m. Then a tangent section was drilled to 1699 m, followed by a drop and turn with 2.5°/30 m to 2128 m. Inclination was dropped down to 4.6° and the well turned right to 339° azimuth. Finally, a continuous build and turn with 2.5°/30 m was performed to TD at 2580 m where the inclination is 37° and the azimuth 69.6°. At TD the 17 1/2" section was well placed with 1.36 m below and 0.46 m left of the planned well path. # 8 ½” section

This almost 2000 m MD long section started with an inclination of 36.1º, built up to 82.0º over the first 450 m in the Shetland Group, held tangent over 700 m and dropped back to 14.4º above reservoir. No turn was planned, thus the well continued on the same 69.9º azimuth and 14.4º inclination for a further 200 m to TD. The initial build in the Lista and Ty formations went without problems, the tool gave more than the theoretical calculated rate. This changed dramatically in the Tor limestone, where it became extremely difficult to achieve the required 3.0º/30 m build rate. With the AutoTrak tool in steer mode with 100% steer force, it was still necessary to cut back on the flow rate and hold back ROP to 5 m/h to get it to build. Consequently, over approximately 160 m, the well path dropped some 14 m below the line. This was recovered in the tangent section by increasing the sail angle from 80.2 to 82.0º. Stick/slip values increased in the Shetland Group. The drop from 80.0° to 14.0º inclination was started from 3710 m. Initially the tool response was as expected, but in the lower Hod Formation and into the Cromer Knoll Group the drop rate showed a large increase. Drilling continued to section TD without further problems. # Objectives

The IOR well 15/9-F-11 B was planned to be placed in the Upper Hugin zone to drain remaining reserves
between the production wells 15/9-F-12 and 15/9-F-14 and continuing downflank towards water injector 15/9-F-5. The planned well path was already characterised by challenging steering, thus beside the main focus on
horizontal steering, also sufficient turn in azimuth to prolong the reservoir section as deep into the structure as
possible needed to be ensured. # Summary

Against interpretation from 4D seismic analysis and reservoir simulation the target zone within Upper Hugin
showed good oil saturation throughout the reservoir section. Estimates of true vertical thicknesses (TVT) indicate
thicker reservoir upflank than expected. TD was set at 4770 m MD, as the wellbore entered Sleipner Formation
below Hugin Formation, without penetrating the anticipated southwesterly field-bounding fault. The total
geosteered section in the Hugin Formation was ~1220 m MD long. A total ~760 m MD in gross oil-bearing
productive Upper Hugin (zones 3.1-3.3) was encountered. # Geosteering

The pre-model from Baker Hughes (RNS) indicated higher contrast towards top reservoir, i.e. Heather Formation
and very low contrasts towards Thief Zone below. Additionally are several faults along the well path prognosed,
causing down-faulted reservoirs. For dip calculations the azimuthal GR (11 m behind bit) and azimuthal DEN (24 m behind bit) were used, also to
some extent distance to bed (D2B) calculations. Hence geosteering commands were affected by often significant
time delays. Several faults were crossed, causing 3 times an unexpected/non-pre-warned exit of top reservoir
(D2B ranged from 3,5-4,1 m TVD). Entering of the reservoir again was just estimated based on the geological
model and steering with maximum dogleg downwards. According to Baker Hughes’ RNS top reservoir cannot be
detected earlier than 1-2 m TVD from above. D2B calculations were not always applicable for bed boundary
detection, due to complex resistivity profile. Another 3 times the well continued from Upper Hugin into the
underlying Thief Zone (yellow circle). Here no distinct signal response was observed. Distances to bed were
roughly estimated to range from 1,5-2,0 m TVD. Geosteering decisions were made by the Volve PETEK with support from GEOP and the Baker Hughes’ RNS
team. The RNS supervisor was present in the Statoil office in Bergen during office hours, while RNS support
outside office hours was covered by the Baker Hughes’ BEACON center in Stavanger. # Wellpath description

28 real-time geosteering decisions were performed. Any changes in trajectory was done to optimise wellbore
positioning in the reservoir and to avoid shaly and water-filled zones respectively. - Started drilling reservoir section from 3198,0 m MD / 2783,8 m TVD. Increased inclination from 63° to 71°. - Entered Heather Formation at 3351,6 m MD / 2846,6 m TVD. Possible fault with minor throw at 3414 m
MD, indicated by high angle dip. Possibly repetition of penetrated stratigraphy. Classification: Internal        Status: Final                  Expiry date: 2010-12-30    Page 31 of 110

- Entered reservoir (Hugin 3.3) at 3467.5 m MD / 2884.0 m TVD. Top of reservoir more oil saturated than expected. Drilling down through stratigraphy. Started to build angle to follow Hugin 3.3. - Encountered more water saturated zone in Hugin 3.2. Continued to build angle to re-enter Hugin 3.3. - Re-entered Hugin 3.3 with 9° angle between formation and wellbore. Started to drop angle to avoid low angle exit of reservoir. - Exited reservoir into Heather Formation at 3812.0 m MD by faulting, estimated throw 6.5 m. Increased drop for faster re-entry of reservoir. - Low angle re-entry (4°) and exit (6°) of reservoir, while geosteering up and down to follow Hugin 3.3. High angle features indicates faulting at entry and exit of Heather Formation. Estimated throws are ~0.5 m. - Hugin 3.3 thickness >9 m TVD. Drilled down through Hugin 3, penetrated base Hugin 3 twice (due to faulting at 4062 m MD) and entered the “Thief Zone” (Hugin 2) at 4108.0 m MD. Estimated throw of fault F2 ~3 m. Building angle to enter lowermost part of reservoir before crossing major prognosed fault F3. - Entered base of reservoir at 4188.0 m MD by faulting (F3), estimated throw 11 m. Continuously dropping angle to align with formation dip and land wellbore in Hugin 3.3. Stopped planned left turn of well path to maximise drop force. Minor faulting while drilling upwards in Hugin 3. - Exited reservoir through fault (F4) with estimated throw of 18 m and 24° angle between wellbore and fault. Drilled downwards into Heather Formation. Continuously dropping angle to re-enter reservoir. Stopped planned left turn of well path to maximise drop force and enter reservoir before TD. - Re-entered reservoir at 4483.5 m MD with an angle of 13° between wellbore and formation. Continued dropping angle to penetrate complete reservoir interval and assess reservoir thickness. Possible minor fault around 4515 m MD (F5?). Exited Hugin 3 at 4573.0 m MD / 3113.9 m TVD. - Holding angle of 42° after Hugin 2.2. No active steering possible after 4685 m MD, due to AutoTrak failure. TD at 4770.6 m MD, when entering Sleipner Formation without penetrating prognosed fault F6. The section and plan views for the wellbore (from slot to TD at 4770 m MD) are shown in Figure 2-12. # Section View - Actual: 15/9-F-11 B (F-11/15/9-F-11 B)

# Data acquisition

# Cuttings, biostratigraphic and mud samples

The contractor for the mud logging services was Baker Hughes. Standard cuttings samples for lithostratigraphic control and biostratigraphic analysis have been collected from commencing the 17 ½” section in general at 10 m intervals to 30 m MD above the BCU and from there at 3 m intervals to TD in the pilot wells, whereas at constant 10 m intervals for the producer well. Two sets of unwashed, wet samples (0.5 kg each) and one set of washed and dried cuttings have been taken. This corresponds to:

- F-11 T2: 1370-4110 (10 m intervals) & 4120-4562 m MD (3 m intervals),
- F-11 A: 2580-3470 m MD (10 m intervals) & 3481-3762 m MD (3 m intervals),
- F-11 B: 2845-4770 m MD. In well F-11 T2 and well F-11 A each 1 sample, in F-11 B 8 samples were lost. Mud samples (1 liter) have been collected at 50 m MD intervals from in general Top Hugin Formation to TD:

- F-11 T2: 4362-4562 m MD,
- F-11 A: 3518-3762 m MD,
