Rev. no. # 3.4 Electrical logging, VSP

# Planning

In pilot well 15/9-F-11 T2 to Volve North prospect a vertical seismic profiling survey (VSP) was planned. Purpose of the survey was:

- primary: more accurate time-depth relationship,
- secondary objective: improve imaging of reservoir and faults. The feasibility study and pre-job modelling was performed by Thor Allan Garden (thgar@statoil.com, VSP group). Based on previous results from 15/9-F-15 A and recent seismic data a walk-away survey with 2 sources was recommended. Schlumberger’s VSI-4 had been selected and after evaluation of operational risk, especially the highly deviated well path (maximum 82° in open hole), finally a pump down VSP inside a simple BHA was selected. # Operations

A rig source VSP and a vertical incidence VSP (flip-flop configuration) were recorded in the 8 ½” open hole, the 14” and the 20” cased hole. The sources were 3x250 cu.in G-guns, and a Trisor OFS used for advanced gun performance control. Vertical incidence survey interval was recorded between 4530.0-2228.8 m MD, the rig source survey 4530-582.9 m at 15 m intervals. While RIH checkshots were taken at 1786.6 and 2518.4 m MD; a correlation pass was run from 4450 m up. While POOH and measuring communication interference between rig and the vessel Dina Star were experienced at 2228.8 m. The survey was continued with the rig source only. # Results

Preliminary VSP imaging results indicated a more northern placement (~30-50 m) of the main bounding fault to the north compared to the initial seismic interpretation. Final processing is pending. Classification: Internal    Status: Final    Expiry date: 2010-12-30    Page 59 of 110

# 3.5 Pressure points

# 3.5.1 15/9-F-11 T2 pilot Volve North prospect

In pilot well 15/9-F-11 T2 at 6 depths pressures points in the water-filled Hugin reservoir were taken (Table 3-5). This was performed while POOH after TD on May 04ᵗʰ and 5ᵗʰ 2013. At this time both water injectors (15/9-F-4 and 15/9-F-5) were in operation. 6 of 6 tests were successful and of good technical quality (all reached a stable pressure). Pressure points were showing a reservoir pressure 13-14 bar over initial, relative to 15/9-19 BT2 and 15/9-F-15 A water gradient, but in accordance with the main field pressure, which was ~12 bar over initial at the time (Figure 3-1). In pilot well 15/9-F-11 A all in all 24 pressure stations in the oil-bearing Hugin Formation were taken. 17 (+2 repeated) were performed while POOH after TD on May 21st 2013 (Table 3-6). At this time both water injectors (15/9-F-4 and 15/9-F-5) were in operation. On May 23rd 2013 additional 5 pressure measurements were repeated. This time after both water injectors were shutdown over a period of almost 48 hours. 18 of 24 tests (2 sets of data with time delay) were successful and of good technical quality (one might be supercharged). 6 tests were tight. Formation pressure points in the Hugin Formation were 12 bar above initial formation pressure in oil zone (measured in 15/9-F-12, February 2008) (Figure 3-1). This indicates communication with the Hugin Formation in the Volve main field, since reservoir pressure has been increased ~12 bar above initial pressure by injection at the time. Time lapse repeated pressure points (Figure 3-1) after closing in main field injections were 0.2-0.9 bar lower than 48hrs earlier. At the same time a pressure drop of 6 bar was recorded in the main field pressure gauge in well 15/9-F-15 C. The small pressure drop was consistent with prognosis from the simulation model. # Well: 15/9-F-11 A

# Well NO 15/9-F-11, F-11 T2, F-11 A, F-11 B

# Volve Field

# 3.6 Reservoir fluid sampling

# Objectives

In both pilot wells optional fluid sampling was planned. - 15/9-F-11 (T2): 1-2 oil samples for compositional analysis, density and viscosity & 1 water sample for resistivity measurements as water saturation reference for the petrophysical model as well compositional analysis, density, viscosity were planned to take, if the Hugin Formation in North prospect is oil filled or an oil-water contact is been encountered. - 15/9-F-11 A: 1 water sample for compositional analysis, density and viscosity to define water type (initial formation water or perched water), if the Hugin Formation in Northwest segment is partly oil-bearing. Since the open hole wellpaths of both 8 ½” pilot sections were planned with inclinations of 82° in 15/9-F-11 (T2) and 75° in 15/9-F-11 A respectively a feasibility study by Schlumberger recommended pipe conveyed wireline logging for running MDT. # Summary

In none of the pilot wells the criteria were achieved. Well 15/9-F-11 T2 found water-filled the Hugin Formation in the North prospect/segment, whereas well 15/9-F-11 A proved the entire Hugin reservoir oil-bearing in the Northwest segment. Therefore all MDT runs were cancelled. # 3.7 Logging remarks

Information about tool/equipment failure is already been mentioned in the specific run summaries above (Table 3-1 to Table 3-4). The M/LWD logging data suite was achieved completely for all wells. The mud logging data suite is been reduced. The chromatograph failed from start of the 8½” section. Classification: Internal Status: Final Expiry date: 2010-12-30 Page 65 of 110

# Rev. no. # 4  Hydrocarbon indications

For the mud logging services Baker Hughes did the gas analysis by using HP FID gas system. # 4.1  15/9-F-11 & 15/9-F-11 T2 pilot Volve North prospect

The 36” & 26” sections were drilled with 1.03 sg seawater/polymer spud mud and HiVis sweeps from seabed down to 1365 m MD. No returns were recovered. The 17 ½” section was drilled with 1.40 sg standard OBM, Enviromul Yellow. Total gas varied from 0.01-1.80 % with an average of 0.52 %. No indications of shallow gas have been observed. # 5 Pore pressures and hole stability

# 5.1 Formation pressure above the reservoir

The prediction of the pore pressure gradient (PP) above the reservoir is based on the Eaton Sonic method. - A hydrostatic pore pressure gradient is expected down to top Ty Formation. The pore pressure within permeable Ty sandstones was measured in wells 15/9-F-12 and 15/9-F-15 S as 0.87 sg. This indicates some 40 bar pressure depletion caused by production from the Sleipner Øst Field in the South. Recent pressure data from Sleipner well 15/9-A-24, the last gas condensate producer, show stable pressure since 2005, in line with the stop of production in the northern Sleipner Øst area. The pore pressure in the Ty Formation within the Volve area is expected to be 0.87 sg, as measured in 2007. - In the underlying upper part of the Shetland Group the pore pressure is assumed to increase gently up to 1.05 sg within the Hod Formation. For the rest of the Shetland and the Cromer Knoll Group a rapid increase in the pore pressure to 1.26 sg is prognosed. - The maximum pore pressure within the Viking Group is expected to be 1.29 sg in the organic rich Draupne shales at the top. The pore pressure within the underlying Heather Formation is expected to be lower. # 5.2 Pore pressure in the reservoir

The reservoir pressure in the Hugin Formation main field is currently maintained at approximately 10-12 bar over initial pressure by injecting water downflank in wells 15/9-F-4 and 15/9-F-5. Throughout the field an oil gradient of 0.0704 bar/m and a water gradient of 0.103 bar/m is assumed. Pressure measurements were undertaken in both pilot wells (Chapter 3.5). - In pilot well 15/9-F-11 T2 pressure range was measured to be 13-14 bar over initial in the water zone. - Pilot well 15/9-F-11 A proved a pressure of 12 bar above initial formation pressure in oil zone. - For IOR well 15/9-F-11 B the pressure in the Hugin Formation has been estimated to be 12 bar over initial (June 2013), referring to time equivalent pressure measurements at downhole pressure gauges in Volve main field. At the base of the Heather Formation likely bad to moderate sandstones in the North prospect and Northwest segment are expected to be present. Even there is not often interpreted flow potential at all; this zone has been anticipated to correspond to reservoir pressure. # 5.3 Formation strength

The overburden gradient (OBG) is calculated from a density curve from well 15/9-F-12. Classification: Internal    Status: Final    Expiry date: 2010-12-30                 Page 69 of 110

# Valid from

# Rev. no. A minimum horizontal strength gradient has been calculated from a Breckels and van Eekelen relation based on a method from Per Horsrud (2011). Initial reservoir pressure is used. - Formation strength in the Balder Formation is reduced due to the presence of tuff layers. Nevertheless, XLOT data from well 15/9-F-14 showed a minimum horizontal stress gradient (ShG) of 1.68 sg in the upper part of the Balder Formation. In the lower 32 m of the Balder Formation the formation strength is reduced to 1.55 sg to account for local experiences. - Within the Ty reservoir the formation strength is assumed to be reduced due to depletion, but should not cause any hole stability problems. It is well above the anticipated pore pressure in the overburden section. - The Minifrac data from the Hugin Formation in well 15/9-19 A was revaluated. The result is a 19 bar reduction in the minimum horizontal stress gradient for the Hugin reservoir and the formations below. The fracture gradient for the Volve area is set as 5% above the minimum horizontal strength, according to the model. - In the upper part of the Balder Formation an adjustment to only 1.73 sg was done to account for reduced formation strength due to the presence of tuff layers. A collapse gradient has been generated based on the method of Stassi d’Alia. Included in this method are the initial pore pressure, the minimum horizontal stress, considerations of the UCS (unconfined compressive strength), and the well path. - The collapse curve in the Nordland Group and upper Hordaland Group shows a general increase towards the hydrostatic pore pressure and surpasses it in the Skade Formation. The 36” & 26” sections were drilled with 1.03 sg seawater/spud mud successfully. - The Hordaland shales are normally associated with high smectite content and under-compaction, causing instability and possibly higher pore pressure in the lower Hordaland Group. However, in the Volve area the sandstones of the Grid Formation are generally present, acting as a pressure vent and thereby preventing abnormal pore pressure. Nevertheless, shales within the Hordaland Group have not been observed as unstable, when drilled at high inclinations and particularly when exposed to water based drilling fluid. - - The UCS has been updated/increased by a factor of 1.2 while planning the 2ⁿᵈ drilling campaign and re-evaluating earlier results. Positive experiences from well 15/9-F-5, where the lower Hordaland and the Rogaland Group have been drilled with 1.35 sg OBM at inclinations of 27°-31° without any incidents and on the other hand tight hole conditions in well 15/9-F-12 with an inclination <10° also with 1.35 sg OBM were taken into account. - A maximum collapse gradient of 1.34 sg is estimated (maximum 23° inclination). No incidents have been observed when drilling with 1.40 sg standard OBM. A peak in the collapse gradient is calculated at the base of the Lista Formation. There is no definite evidence for significant unstable conditions in this interval, but washouts were observed on MWD logs and sticky problems occurred when running casing on some previous wells. However post-drilling the UCS was adjusted/increased by a factor 1.15 to account for no incidents when drilling this lower part with 1.28 sg standard OBM, and several kick-off from the here placed casing shoe. - No incidents have been observed in the Cromer Knoll Group. # 6 Petrophysical evaluation

# 6.1 Summary

# 6.1.1 15/9-F-11 T2 pilot Volve North prospect

The Hugin Formation was water-filled, except of two pockets of residual oil that seem to be structurally trapped. (Lowest Sw achieved in these is ~ 0.4) One residual oil pocket is situated below the Heather Formation and the other below the “offshore shale” which separates Upper Hugin from the “Thief Zone” (Hugin zone 2). These 2 residual oil pockets are observed in the following intervals:

- 4393.8-4399 m MD / 3182.3-3187.3 m MSL,
- 4415.8-4419.7 m MD / 3203.6-3207.4 m MSL. An oil show is described on cuttings @ 4411 m MD. The rest of the Hugin Formation, which was water-filled, showed very low resistivities < 0.05 Ohm.m in the best sands, is reflecting the very saline formation water on Volve (~ 130000 ppm). This might indicate that the Hugin Formation never has been oil-filled, and that the two observed oil pockets are results of hydrocarbon migration. The Hugin Formation properties seem to be poorer than encountered in previous Volve wells, with an average porosity of 0.17. This is 4 p.u. less than the porosity in the Hugin Formation in the exploration well 15/9-19 BT2, which is the closest well with respect to TVD depth. Net/Gross is however high as expected (Hugin > 0.8); in this well 0.81. Porosity from MagTrak is a lithology independent porosity, and MagTrak porosity in 15/9-F-11 T2 confirms in general the porosity from the density log. Formation pressure points in the Hugin Formation were 13.7 bar above initial formation pressure measured in 15/9-19 BT2 (January 1998). This indicates communication with the Hugin Formation in the Volve main field, since reservoir pressure has been increased ~12 bar above initial pressure by injection, prior to this drilling campaign. LWD log data is of good quality. No oil and water samples were taken since no commercial find. Classification: Internal    Status: Final    Expiry date: 2010-12-30    Page 72 of 110

# Well NO 15/9-F-11, F-11 T2, F-11 A, F-11 B

# Volve Field

# 6.1.2 15/9-F-11 A pilot Volve Northwest segment

The Hugin Formation was oil-filled, with porosity being slightly lower and water saturation being higher than previous Hugin wells. An average porosity of 0.21 in 15/9-F-11 A is 2 p.u. less than the porosity in the Hugin Formation in wells 15/9-F-4 and 15/9-F-15 A, which are the most comparable wells with respect to TVD depth. Net/Gross is however as expected > 0.8, in this well 0.86. An oil-down to situation (ODT) ~ 3702 m MD / 3025.5 m MSL was proven, which depth corresponds to Top Sleipner Formation. Formation pressure points in the Hugin Formation were 12 bar above initial formation pressure measured in 15/9-F-12 (February 2008). This indicates communication with the Hugin Formation in the Volve main field, since reservoir pressure has been increased ~12 bar above initial pressure by injection, prior to this drilling campaign. To get a picture of the time lapse effect, injection was closed, and a second set of formation pressure (only 5 depth stations were repeated) was logged 48 hours after the first formation pressure data set. This showed only minor decrease in formation pressure, in the range: 0.2-0.9 bar. LWD log data is of good quality. No water sample was taken since water was not encountered below the oil column. # 6.1.3 15/9-F-11 B IOR producer Volve Main Field

The Hugin Formation was oil-filled in large parts of the well, and encountered also oil in the first part (shallowest) of the Hugin Formation, which was not expected from 4D and simulation model. Averages for the Hugin Formation are given in Chapter 6.6.1 to 6.6.3. Flooded intervals are given in Table 6-5. A flag curve showing flooded intervals is named FLOODED_FLAG (in flooded intervals is FLOODED_FLAG = 1), and plotted as turquoise intervals in track no. 8 in the CPI plot. Below ~ 4701 m MD / 3148 m MSL (a coal layer) the water seems to be initial, due to the low resistivity. This is plotted as dark blue interval in track no. 8 in the CPI plot. Hence 15/9-F-11 B might indicate a WUT = 3148 m MSL, which is not in conflict with the observed ODT (3145 m MSL) in the main field. Since there is a huge contrast between the very saline Hugin Formation water and the fresh, low saline Utsira Formation (injection) water, 3 cases with different formation water salinity are presented in the 15/9-F-11 B CPI in order to illustrated the impact on water saturation. No depth shifting has been applied, except in limited overlap intervals around kick off depths and hole sections in Overburden. These depth shifts and the splicing of the individual runs are reported in Logtek’s info file for each well; WLC_COMPOSITE_1_INF_1.PDF, which is stored in Petrobank. All depths are referenced to driller’s depth. Logtek’s general “philosophy” with respect to depth shifting in the transition zone between a mother well and a kick off well is as follows: aim to keep the stop-coupled depths as shallow as possible, preferably at KOP. But sometimes it has to be set deeper (a few meters deeper, preferably in a shale if possible) due to a) if overlapping curves are not on depth at KOP, or b) if the depth shifts have been so large, that the logs else will be too compressed. # Rev. no. # 6.3 Petrophysical evaluation method and input parameters

# 6.3.1 General

Petrophysical evaluation is performed according to the Volve petrophysical field model, described in report: “Sleipner Øst and Volve Model 2006, Hugin and Skagerrak Formation, Petrophysical Evaluation”. November 2006. Author: Elin Solfjell, Karl Audun Lehne. The petrophysical evaluation software used is Geolog, and the Geolog project is SLEIPNER_OST (at Stavanger server: FROST_SVG). # 6.3.2 Porosity

Total porosity, PHIF (φ ), is derived from the density log, which is calibrated to overburden corrected core porosity for wells drilled with either OBM or WBM. The neutron log, NPHI, has been used to correct for varying mud filtrate invasion. φ = φF + A × (NPHI − φD) + B

where:

φD = (ρma − ρb) / (ρma − ρfl) [fraction]

ρma is the matrix density [g/cm3]

ρb is the measured bulk density (RHOB), [g/cm3]

ρfl is the pore fluid density [g/cm3]

A and B are regression coefficients

NPHI: neutron log in limestone units [fraction]

# 6.3.3 Shale volume, VSH

To determine VSH, the standard model VSHGR from linear GR relationship is applied:

VSHGR = GR − GRmin

VSH = GRₘₐₓ − GRmin

where:

- GR gamma ray reading [API]
- GR min gamma ray reading in clean sand [API]
- GR ₘₐₓ gamma ray reading in shale [API]

VSH is used quantitatively in deriving permeability, and quantitatively indirectly in Netsand cutoff. In order to pick GR min and GRₘₐₓ in a more objective way, and to avoid over-estimating of VSH, GR is “normalized” against VSHDN (VSH from DEN/NEU) by cross plotting GR versus VSHDN. VSHDN is considered to be the most reliable shale indicator (except in intervals dominated by mica and heavy minerals). This shale indicator compares porosity derived from the density log with porosity from the neutron log. If the neutron log reads higher porosity than the density, is this believed to be due to the hydrogen content of present clay minerals. VSHDN is derived from the response equation for the neutron log:

VCLDN = NPHICS − (PHIT * HIFL) / HICL

VSHDN = VCLDN / (1− PHISH)

where:

- VCLDN dry shale volume from density / neutron logs [fraction]
- NPHICS neutron log corrected to sandstone matrix [fraction]
- PHIT total log porosity [fraction]
- HIFL Hydrogen Index to fluid
- HICL Hydrogen Index to clay
- PHISH shale porosity [fraction]

# 6.3.4 Water saturation

Water saturation is calculated using Archie equation, giving a total water saturation:

Sw = (a × Rw) / (φ × Rt) ^ m

where:

- a - Archie (tortuosity) factor
- Rw - resistivity of formation water [Ohmm]
- φ - total porosity [fraction]
- F - cementation exponent
- Rt - true resistivity [Ohmm]
- m - saturation exponent

# 6.3.5 Permeability

The horizontal log permeability, KLOGH, is derived from the following equation based on multivariable regression analysis between log porosity and shale volume (“normalized” against VSHDN) against overburden corrected core permeability:

- Hugin Formation: KLOGH = 10(²⁺⁸×PHIF−⁹×VSH)
- Sleipner Formation: KLOGH = 10(−³⁺³²×PHIF−²×VSH)
- Skagerrak Formation: KLOGH = 10(−¹.85⁺17.4×PHIF−³×VSH)

# 6.4 Flag curves

# Netsand flag

Cut-offs on PHIF and VSH are used to determine Netsand; SAND_FLAG = 1. Hugin Formation: SAND_FLAG = 1 when PHIF > 0.10 and VSH < 0.50. These cut offs correspond to a overburden corrected core permeability of 0.5 mD, and are also applied for the other formations. In addition some manual editing might have been performed on the SAND_FLAG curve. # Carbonate flag

A carbonate flag curve, CARB_FLAG, has been determined manually by visual inspection of the logs. Whenever CARB_FLAG = 1 is SAND_FLAG = 0. # Coal flag

15/9-F-11 T2: 2 coal layers were observed in the Sleipner Formation. 15/9-F-11 A: No coal layers were observed. 15/9-F-11 B: 2 coal layers were observed in the Hugin Formation. The coal flag curve, COAL_FLAG, has been determined by a manually decided cut off on the density log. In addition some editing was needed due to shoulder effects. Whenever COAL_FLAG = 1 is SAND_FLAG = 0. # 15/9-F-11 B: Flooded interval flag

# 6.5 Nuclear magnetic resonance logging (LWD)

In pilot well 15/9-F-11 T2 Baker’s LWD NMR tool, MagTrak, was run in the 8 ½” section. The tool was ran in silent mode, hence data only in memory.

MagTrak sensor offset to bit was 50.45 m. # 6.5.1 Logging mode and parameters

The tool was logged in “Dual Wait Time” (DTW) mode, also called “PoroPermMT+Light HC” mode, in order to process a salinity independent water saturation. An average ROP of 15 m/h was held in the reservoir interval to assure a sufficient vertical resolution of the MagTrak data, and also to keep the signal/noise ratio high. Vertical resolution with DTW mode and ROP = 20 m/h was calculated to be 1.7 m. Vertical resolution with DTW mode and ROP = 30 m/h was calculated to be 2.6 m. The MagTrak data was logged and final post processed with the following parameters:

- running average (data stacking) of 16: RA = 16
- T2 cutoffs: CBW = 3.3 ms, BVI = 33 ms (standard sandstone T2 cut-offs)
- number of T2 bins: 27 over the range of 0.5-4096 ms. Classification: Internal    Status: Final    Expiry date: 2010-12-30    Page 82 of 110

# Rev. no. # 6.5.3 MagTrak purpose and achievements

# Purpose with running MagTrak was to achieve:

- lithology independent porosity,
- independent (semi-quantitative) permeability indicator, possibility of detecting thief zone,
- salinity independent water/oil saturation,
- discriminate between movable and bound fluid,
- irreducible water saturation,
- independent estimation of saturation exponent, n,
- pore size distribution. Since the reservoir turned out to be more or less water filled, some of the purposes were not any longer applicable. # Achievements

- MagTrak provides both a total and an effective porosity which is lithology independent. Total porosity from MagTrak, MPHS, is plotted (magenta curve) in same track as total porosity, PHIF, from conventional logs in the F-11 T2 CPI. The overall trend in the Hugin Formation is that the MPHS match well with PHIF, which should not be a surprise in a water-filled reservoir. MPHS has (and shows) however a poorer vertical resolution than PHIF. - In the Heather Formation PHIF is lower than MPHS, indicating perhaps a too low matrix density applied for this formation. - MagTrak provides an independent semi-quantitative permeability indicator, MPERM. This curve is plotted (magenta curve) in same track as the permeability from conventional logs and field model, KLOGH, in the 15/9-F-11 T2 CPI. The overall trend is that MPERM > KLOGH with the applied standard sandstone T2 cut-offs. - The thief zone is not detected by MPERM. From the T2 distribution (Figure 6-2) it is however seen that the sand in interval 4449-453 m MD (lower Thief Zone / Hugin 2.1 (7)) has the complete T2 signal coming from the oil-based mud filtrate, hence indicating qualitatively the most permeable zone of this Hugin Formation. Invasion will however of course be dependent of different factors, such as mud properties and ROP. - SCAL data from 15/9-19 A was investigated to check if the default coefficients for the Coates-Timur permeability (MPERM) equation were appropriate (Chapter 6.5.4). This resulted in a permeability curve called MPERM_ADJUSTED which is plotted (light green curve) in the permeability track (track no. 9) in the 15/9-F-11 T2 CPI. - Since the reservoir was more or less water filled, it was no point to order a processing from Baker Hughes to get independent oil saturation. - MagTrak provides discrimination between movable and bound fluid. This is illustrated in track no. 8 in the 15/9-F-11 T2 CPI. Brown shading = clay bound fraction, turquoise shading = capillary bound fraction and yellow shading = free or movable fluid fraction. The data from mercury injection should be disregarded. Data from the CCAL (Conventional Core Analysis) plugs are shown as purple squares in Figure 6-3. The water saturation is measured by Dean and Stark method (OBM) and is measured together with porosity at vertical plugs. Permeability is however not measured on same vertical plugs, but on horizontal plugs. Due to this, permeability has been picked from adjacent plugs with approximately same porosity. # Remarks regarding well 15/9-F-11 B CPI

Note that for well 15/9-F-11 B is the CPI separated in 3 parts for better visualisation. Note also that the 15/9-F-11 B CPIs include 3 different formation water salinity cases:

- First case is Hugin Formation water salinity, second case is a mixture of Hugin and Utsira Formation water salinity, third case is pure Utsira Formation water salinity. The details in difference and colour coding are explained in the header of the CPI’s. There is a huge contrast between the very saline Hugin Formation water and the fresh, low saline Utsira Formation water (injection water), and the different cases illustrate the impact on the water saturation. Note also that the salinity is changed in the whole evaluated intervals, and that all other parameters are kept unchanged. # Summary/results:

- entire Hugin Formation found oil-bearing (82 m TVD) and thicker than expected
- proved communication to main field and injectivity by 2 sets of pressure measurements (1st set after drilling, 2nd set after shut-in injectors and waited 48 h)

# Comments:

- 8 1/2" section: smooth drilling; waited 2 times due to top drive issues and other operations in rigs safety zone
- no hole instabilities have been reported

# Objectives:

- oil production from infill location between existing producers F-12 and F-14 in the main field
- geosteer in the upper part of the Hugin reservoir, which is expected to be only partially drained

# Summary/results:

- encountered oil-filled Upper Hugin reservoir in heel and toe with good reservoir qualities
- total geosteered section in Hugin Formation was ~1220 m MD long of which ~780 m MD in oil-bearing productive Upper Hugin
- exit field through main fault, as expected, was not observed; TD was set in Sleipner Formation due to dropping towards toe

# Comments:

- 12 1/4" section: trip due to bit change
- 8 1/2" section: exit several times into Heather Fm. above and water-filled Hugin below; dir-MWD failure at 4682 m changed TD criterion

# Subject:

Running 14" casing - several improvements identified on AAR

# Section:

NO 15/9-F-11 T2, 17 1/2"

# Rep date:

24.Apr.2013

# Keywords:

CASING/LINER

# Downtime:

Pot time improvements:

# Comp inv:

Weatherford Norge AS

# References:

Synergi no:

Cost:

Synergi desc:

# Description:

After running of 14" casing on the F-11 T2 well Maersk Drilling performed an After Action Review. The following improvements were identified:

- During the first phase of running casing, the driller used the "drill" function on the link tilt to clear the BX elevator from the casing. Then the bails were tilted out to pick up the next joint. When using the "drill" function the tilt arms are pushed against the bails (compared to "tilt" which pulls on the bails). This stress resulted in nuts from u-clamps coming loose and dropping to the floor inside the red zone (impact 5 joule). The running sequence was revised and the dolly retract was used to clear the top drive/elevators from the casing string. - Weatherford BX elevator need to have correct rotator, it should be supplied with the casing running equipment package. - Ensure stabbing guides and telescopic arms are adjusted correctly on Weatherford casing tong frame. Adjustments should be done offline prior to R/U. - Remove one FMS topguide while running shoetrack to allow centralizers to pass through. - Orientate eye bolts on BX inserts so that they run parallel with the bails and don't interfere with the casing couplings. - The threads are prepared for Baker lock on the pipe deck, when lifting them up use a clean protector cap instead of a rag to protect the threads. # Immediate solution:

Follow up with Weatherford

# Future recommended solution:

Recommended to get the equipment list sent offshore prior to sending equipment offshore. If this is done before the operation takes place, we can check what equipment is compatible with the rig equipment. Classification: Internal

Status: Final

# 4 Activity highlights

# 4.1 NO 15/9-F-11, 36" (07.Mar.2013 00:00)

START: 07.Mar.2013 00:00 146 m MD

END: 14.Mar.2013 20:00 208 m MD

# OBJECTIVE

Spud and drill 36” section to 208 m MD/208 m TVD. # SUMMARY

The well was spudded, and a 36” hole was drilled from 146 m MD to section TD 208 m MD in one run with an average rate of penetration (ROP) of 5.7 m/hr. The hole was drilled with a 36” x 26” hole opener above a 17 ½” bit. At TD 25 m³ sweep was pumped and a wiper trip was performed and the well displaced to 1.40 sg spud mud. After several hours of waiting on weather (WOW) the bit was run back to bottom to check for fill and displaced again with 1.40 sg mud. When on surface found that BHA had parted with top of fish at F-11 template funnel. Recovered 115,91 m of BHA to surface. ROV performed template survey with poor visibility and found a section of BHA laying on sea bed next to template. Evaluated situation and investigated further to determine details of fish in hole and laying on sea bed. Using a serial no. observed on top of fish at template funnel identified that the upper most part of the fish was a 9 11/16" OD wear sleeve which forms the upper part of 11 3/4" stabilizer on the OnTrack tool. Length of fish in hole re-calculated to be 11,90 m, approximately 4-5 ton (in air). Length of BHA on seabed estimated to be 46.62 m, approximately 7 ton (in air). M/U fishing BHA spear assembly to attempt to engage 3" ID opening at top of fish. POOH and M/U 2 1/2" - 3 5/8" Taper Tap BHA. The fish was successfully engaged and fish was layed out on seabed. A new 36” BHA was M/U and RIH. The hole was circulated clean and displaced to 1.40 sg mud, before the BHA was pulled to surface and laid down. The conductor was then run to 202 m MD and cemented to surface without incidents. The conductor was held in place while the cement set up. # BIT RUNS

# RUN # 1

A 17 1/2” Hughes Christensen VM-T303DX0 type bit on an Odfjell 26” x 36”, two stage hole opener (HO) and a Baker Hughes MWD string was run in hole on 5 ½” HWDP and 5 ½” drill pipe (DP) and drilled to TD in one run. Drilling Parameters: 3400 lpm, 77 bar, 62m, 10.8 drill hrs, 14.8 circ hrs, 5.7 m/hr, 2 – 3 tons, 60 – 70 rpm, 4 - 8 kNm

Bit grading: N/A (on seabed)

# OBJECTIVE

Drill and orient 26” hole to section TD at The 20” casing is set below Skade Fm to seal off Utsira Fm and Skade Fm. # SUMMARY

Ran in hole and drilled out the shoetrack from 197 to 202 m MD. The rat hole was cleaned out and reamed in stages to 208m MD. No restrictions were verified by pulling through the rat hole and the shoe, before commenced drilling. Drilled 26” hole to 281 m MD, evaluated survey data and wellpath. Reamed hole from 212 to 281m MD and evaluated new survey data. Recalculated projected wellpath and drilled from 281 m to 347 m MD. Decided to plug back 26” open hole from 347 m to 202 m MD due to close proximity and pulled out of hole (POOH). There was also a MWD tool failure on this run that made it necessary to change out most of the MWD part of the BHA. # BIT RUNS

The same 26” Hughes Christensen VG-03M type bit used for cleanout on previous section and a Baker Hughes MWD/LWD string was run in hole on 5 ½” HWDP and 5 ½” DP. Drilling Parameters: 4500 lpm, 155 bar, 123 m, 7.6 drill hrs, 36 circ hrs, 16.2 m/hr, 2 – 3 tons, 80 rpm, 6 - 12 kNm

# DRILLING FLUIDS

The section was drilled with 1.03 sg Seawater / Polymer spudmud and hivis sweeps. # MWD/BHA

A 26”, Hughes Christensen, VG-1, IADC 115 bit was selected by Baker Hughes for this section. A Baker Hughes 12 ¾” steerable motor was run to get maximum horse power down to the bit while at the same time minimizing vibrations and off center rotation. A Baker Hughes MWD and LWD (logging while drilling) were included for formation evaluation, directional control and vibration monitoring purposes. 9 ½” drill collars (DC), 5 ½” heavy weight drill pipe (HWDP) and 5 ½” drill pipe were used above the MWD/LWD string. # CASING

N/A

# OBJECTIVE

Drill and orient 26” hole to section TD. Set the 20” casing below Skade Fm to seal off Utsira Fm and Skade Fm. # SUMMARY

This section was sidetracked from F-11. P/U 26” BHA and ran in hole (RIH) and tagged top of cement (TOC) at 244 m MD. Originally planned to sidetrack from 202 m MD, but since not tagging cement before 246 m MD, started time drilling from this point. It was drilled in two bit runs, the first was pulled due to broken bearings in the crown sheaves and the GyroTrak also failed in this run. Drilled ahead to 454 m MD when an unusual noise from the crown block fast line sheave was observed. Checked crown block fast-line sheave and found bearing ceased onto shaft. Evaluated situation and prepared forward plan to POOH 26" BHA prior to working on fast-line sheave. After repairing the fast-line sheave the 26” BHA was RIH. Drilling commenced and at 885 m MD, the Utsira Fm was entered. Sticky formation was observed from 1100-1150 m MD and every stand was reamed prior to connection while drilling to TD. The BHA was pulled out of hole without problems to 460 m MD where tight hole was experienced. Reamed stand while circulating and pulled BHA to surface. # BIT RUNS

The section started with a 12 ¼” cleanout assembly for drilling out the 14” shoetrack. Reached TD at 4562 m MD without any problems. Circulated hole clean with 3 x B/U while reciprocating the string. Performed relogging and took pressure points with TesTrak on different depths while POOH. L/D the BHA and picked up new 8 ½” BHA for VSP on wireline inside drillpipe. RIH and tagged TD at 4562 m MD and circulated B/U. POOH with VSP BHA and rigged up for Gyro wireline and RIH to TD, Performed gyro wireline from 145m MD to 4562 m MD. Broke out and laid down gyro wireline toolstring. Started to POOH with 5 ½” drillpipe, but observed restrictions on several depths. Attemped to pass restriction with 30MT – no go. Established circulation, got pass restriction and pulled the string to surface. Rigged up and RIH with cement stinger to 4160 m MD, broke circulation and washed down to TD. Cement plug # 1 was set from 4562-4160 m MD in two stages. The cement stinger was pulled out to 2571 m MD to wait for cement to set. # OBJECTIVE

Prove oil in North West segment

# SUMMARY

The 8 ½” pilot to North West was kicked off from F-11 T2 at 2586 m MD and drilled in two stages. First stage was drilled to 3300 m MD and a gyro was run. While drilling through Ty Fm a 4m³ LCM pill per stand was added to prevent downhole losses, and no losses were observed. After performing the gyro run, the BHA was pulled into the 14” casing shoe and operation stopped while Lewek Connector was finishing gas pipe work. The hole was circulated ~1 hr every 12 hrs with 1100 lpm. After approx. 50 hr waiting, the hole was circulated clean prior to run back on bottom to continue drilling to 3380 m MD. At this depth the MW was increased from 1.28 sg to 1.32 sg with two circulations, the same method as for the previous pilot hole. Drilled to TD at 3762 m MD without any problems, though TD was set deeper than planned due to Hugin Fm was entered deeper than expected and were also thicker. The hole was circulated clean with 3 x B/U prior to take pressure points with TesTrak. The first set of pressure points were taken when POOH, and it was decided to take a second set of pressure points. After waiting approx. 48 hrs the BHA was RIH to 3762 m MD and five new pressure points were taken and the BHA pulled to surface. # OBJECTIVE

Sidetrack and drill 12 ¼” hole from 14” casing shoe and perform a gyro verification run when POOH to 14” casing shoe. # SUMMARY

This section was drilled in two runs. A 12 ¼” rotary steerable drilling BHA was picked up and RIH in cased hole. Tagged top of the second cement plug at 2531m MD. The cement inside the casing was drilled while treating the mud. Prior to entering the Ty Fm. A 4m³/stand 1.28 sg LCM pill was bled into the system to prevent any losses. When entering the Shetland chalk, the ROP reduced from 19 m/hr to 11 m/hr and then finally to 5 m/hr. After slow drilling (13 m for 25 hrs) it was decided to POOH for a bit change. The mud motor, CoPilot, AutoTrak and the bit were layed out. These components were replaced prior to RIH. TD was reached at 3197 m MD without any problems, hole circulated clean with 3.5 x B/U while reciprocating the string. A gyro was dropped before pulling the BHA to surface. A 9 5/8” x 10 ¾” casing was run and cemented 400m above casing shoe. Displaced the cement with calculated displacement volume plus 1/2 shoetrack volume. Total displacement volume with rig pumps 116 m3 (5227 strokes). No indication of top plug bumped. Total losses while displacement 7-9 m3. Bleed off pressure and checked for back flow. Monitored for back flow on trip tank. Recorded 1.2 m3 back flow over a periode of 1 hr. Closed full open safety (FOSV) valve and monitored annulus on trip tank. L/D cement head and checked for back flow. Observed flow from string. Closed FOSV and installed circulation swedge. Lined up surface lines to cement unit and applied 32 bar monitoring for pressure build-up. Recorded 35 bar pressure build-up to 67 bar. Pressured up to 69 bar and held back pressure. After 8 hrs, bled off pressure and monitored for pressure build-up. No pressure build-up observed. Before the casing hanger seal assembly was set, the 14 x 9 5/8” annulus was bullheaded with 82 m³ of 1.10sg NaCl, corrosion inhibited packer fluid through a closed BOP and the PADPRT at wellhead level. This was done in order to prevent annulus pressure build up (APB). A casing scraper assembly was picked up and RIH to 140 m MD and the riser mud was displaced to 1.28sg. Scraped inside the 9 5/8” casing. Performed USIT logging of the 9 5/8” casing cement. Top of cement fund at 2679 m MD. # BIT RUNS

# Objective

To cement 10 ¾” x 9 5/8” casing 400m above casing show to provide sufficient cement for production packer. # Execution

Pumped 27 m3 of 1.52 sg spacer, mixed and pumped 16.8 m3 of 1.90 sg cement slurry. Displaced the cement to the rig floor with 3 m3 and zeroed stroke counters and displaced the cement with 92 m3 (4152 strokes) 1.28 sg OBM, 2500 lpm 36 bar. Observed indication of losses. Slowed down pumps and displaced cement to 110 m3 (4954 strokes) with 2000 lpm, 32 bar. Slowed down pumps to 500 lpm (FCP 40 bar) and displaced cement with calculated displacement volume plus 1/2 shoetrack volume. Total displacement volume with rig pumps 116 m3 (5227 strokes). No indication of top plug bumped. # Classification

# Remarks

# Objective:

Bring TOC to TOL 3099 mtr MD to isolate liner lap. Provide structural support to the liner and zonal isolation across the interval. # Execution:

Mix water was mixed with sea water using CMS pumps that get signals from mix water pump and then give accurate volume of chemicals. This happened in one operation right before the dry cement is added. Spacer pit was cleaned, filled with water and then taken a chloride test. The test shows 350 ppm (maximum 2000 accepted for spacer). Then we added the rest of additives to the spacer and checked the rheology to be sure the spacer is as supposed. No losses during the cement job. It was 6 m3 less in return at the end of displacing. Liner was rotating while cementing. After 10 min mixing we lost cement transferring from the silo. We then went to our back-up silo and continued as planned. The silos was function tested before the job. Wiper plug sheared as planned. Classification: Internal

# Objective

Set 2 P&A cement plugs one for plugging the reservoir and one set 50 m into the 14" casing. The first P&A plug will contain 1.90 SG ThermaCem cement and cover the Hugin reservoir section and upwards with 50m above Top Heather sandstones, 4562-4320 m MD. TOC planned at 4160 m. Plug will be set in two steps. (This is step two)

# Execution

Cement plug was set as a balanced plug on bottom of well. Pipe was pulled dry throughout the hole plug interval. Cement was mixed on the fly using CMS. Spacer was mixed in PIT 12 and 14. Underdisplaced plug with 2.5 m3. # Classification

Statoil Final Well Report
# Well NO 15/9-F-11, F-11 T2, F-11 A, F-11 B

# 1 Well objectives and result

# 1.1 Well objectives

Objectives of the 15/9-F-11 exploration pilot well were to prove oil in the Volve North prospect and to define the field OWC. If the pilot would find an oil-down to situation (ODT), an optional openhole side-track further down flank was planned. In addition the pilot was placed such that it could prove a shallower Hugin interpretation based on the previous 2009 interpretation, which represented an additional prospect upside. An oil fluid sample was planned to collect in any commercial find scenario. Objectives of the second pilot 15/9-F-11 A were to prove oil and sufficient reservoir thickness in the Northwest fault segment. If the pilot found an oil-water contact (OWC), a water sample would be taken to prove for initial (perched water) or flooded conditions. If the 15/9-F-11 North pilot well would encounter a sufficient oil column in Volve North segment, well 15/9-F-11 B would be drilled to an optimal location further upflank and completed as an oil producer. If 15/9-F-11 North pilot would find no or insufficient oil in the North prospect, 15/9-F-11 B would be sidetracked to an infill location between existing producers 15/9-F-12 and 15/9-F-14 in the main field and downflank towards existing injector 15/9-F-5. The aim was to geosteer the well in the upper part of the Hugin reservoir, which was expected to be only partially drained. Figure 1-1 shows the pre-drill Top Hugin map and the planned well paths. Statoil Final Well Report
# Well NO 15/9-F-11, F-11 T2, F-11 A, F-11 B

# 1.3 Well results

# 1.3.1 15/9-F-11 / 15/9-F-11 T2 pilot Volve North prospect

The well reached on May 4ᵗʰ 2013 at 4561.8 m MD / 3345.2 m MSL its total depth (TD) in the Sleipner Formation. It encountered water-filled Hugin with some residual oil on logs and cuttings. Top Hugin Formation was encountered 27 m TVD deeper than expected at 3181 m MSL. Full Hugin stratigraphy was present (104 m TVD / ~98 m TVT thickness). Reservoir properties were slightly poorer than expected, compared to offset wells at similar depth. Pressure measurements were showing a reservoir pressure 13-14 bar over initial, relative to 15/9-19 BT2 and 15/9-F-15 A water gradient, but in accordance with the main field pressure, which was ~12 bar over initial at the time. No hydrocarbon indications in Upper Jurassic were observed. The well proved a dry Volve North prospect. Pre-drill prospect evaluation assumed a Pg of 34% with main risk elements trap geometry (55%) due to poor seismic control and source migration (70%) given a possible charge through the Upper Jurassic. The prospect model included a potentially deep OWC contact (down to 3200 m MSL with no OWC drilled on Volve). Post-drill well results support a migration fairway through the Hugin Formation into the main field. The prospect trap however failed due to depth and geometry. Pressure measurements indicate that the aquifer communicates with the main field. Top Hugin Formation at 3181 m MSL can be considered a water-up to (WUT) observation for the main field. A dry North prospect resulted in the cancellation of an immediate 15/9-F-11 B North producer and subsequent 15/9-F-1 A injector option. # 1.3.2 15/9-F-11 A pilot Volve Northwest segment

The planned sidetrack reached TD on May 21ˢᵗ 2013 at 3762.0 m MD / 3071.6 m MSL in the Sleipner Formation. It encountered oil-filled Hugin Formation at 2944 m MSL, thus 25 m TVD deeper than expected. Full Hugin stratigraphy was present (82 m TVD / ~64 m TVT thickness). Reservoir properties were good, but generally higher water saturations than in main field oil-bearing wells were observed. Initial pressure measurements were ~12 bar over initial and in accordance with main field pressure at the time. This indicates pressure communication to the main field. Time lapse pressure points were taken 48 h later, after injection in main field was closed. This led to 6 bar pressure drop in the main field well 15/9-F-15 C. In the 15/9-F-11 A pilot a ~0.2-0.9 bar pressure drop was observed, which was consistent with prognosis from the simulation model. The well proved a thick upside oil column in the NW segment, which is in pressure communication with the main field. In-place oil volumes in the segment are estimated with ~1.4 MSm³. Rev. no. Well results triggered planning and drilling of a dedicated injector from slot 15/9-F-1. The injector is supposed to drive oil towards the existing upflank producer 15/9-F-14 in the main field. # 1.3.3 15/9-F-11 B IOR producer Volve Main Field

The planned sidetrack reached TD on June 6th 2013 at 4770.3 m MD / 3203 m MSL in the Sleipner Formation. It encountered an oil-filled Upper Hugin (zone 3) in heel and toe with good reservoir qualities similar to offset wells. The total geosteered section in the Hugin Formation was ~1220 m MD long. A total of ~760 m MD in gross oil-bearing productive Upper Hugin were encountered. Some non-productive Heather Formation and Hugin Thief Zone (zone 2) intervals were drilled, mainly due to predominantly sub-seismic faulting. Towards the toe, the well was dropped into water-flooded Thief Zone and Lower Hugin (zone 1) due to steering tool failure. TD was set in the Sleipner Formation. An exit of the field through the main fault, as expected, was not observed. With high oil saturation in both toe and heel, the well encountered upside volumes in the targeted Upper Hugin zone. Expectation was that the heel would be water-filled as indicated by reservoir simulation and 4D seismic, while mainly the downflank toe section would encounter undrained oil. At ~4700 m MD / 3147 m MSL a marked drop in resistivity in the Lower Hugin may indicate that the well penetrated the initial field OWC. Water-filled zones just above showed resistivities, which are consistent with injected Utsira water (+/ produced water). Initial perforations were placed in good to intermediate quality Upper Hugin sandstones in the toe of well with optional later perforation in the heel. A total of 93 m were perforated in 8 runs. Production was started July 24th 2013 with an initial nearly water-free rate of ~1000 Sm³/d. Drawdown is limited to avoid early water breakthrough. # 2.1 Geological setting and results

# 2.1.1 15/9-F-11 T2 pilot Volve North prospect

Overburden

Figure 2-1 shows the stratigraphy in the pilot from the Hod Formation down to TD in the Sleipner Formation. Prior to entering the Hugin reservoir the well encountered a thick package of Upper Jurassic Draupne and Heather formations as expected. No significant Intra Draupne sandstones were observed. Thin sandy stringers are mostly cemented. No indications for hydrocarbons on cuttings or logs were observed. Figure 2-1 also shows image log interpretation on memory data as delivered by Baker Hughes (reservoir navigation services, RNS) on memory data. Note that tadpoles are shown undifferentiated/unclassified for type or confidence level and hence have a high ‘noise level’ (due to picks of cemented intervals etc.). Overall, they are consistent with the expected structural grain. The well path is transported out in the Shetland Group and dropped into the downfaulted North prospect while crossing the major fault separating the prospect from the main field. Tadpoles down to the Draupne Formation indicate a generally North dipping structure with angles of ~10-20°. At the Heather Formation level structural dip jumps to ~50°, then decreasing with depth into the Hugin Formation to ~30°. This indicates a structural drag along the large fault to the South. Formation is dipping generally to the NNE to NE which is approximately consistent with the formation dip on the Top Hugin Formation depth map. Reservoir section

A complete Hugin stratigraphy was encountered in the well. Lithostratigraphic subzonation can easily be correlated with offset wells indicating no significant lateral facies changes. Figure 2-2 shows the reservoir section on logs with the Hugin subzonation in track 3. - Upper Hugin (zones 3.3-3.1): 4393-4415 m MD
- with ~4 m TVD thick offshore shale at base
- Middle Hugin or ‘Thief Zone’ (zones 2.3-2.1): 4415-4444 m MD
- with typical high permeability upper and lower zone as confirmed by high mobilities
- thick calcareous cemented interval at base (zone 2.1)
- Lower Hugin (zones 1.6-1.1): 4444-4497.4 m MD
- thick calcareous cemented interval at top (zone 1.6)

The thickness of the Hugin Formation was with 101 m TVD / ~98 m TVT approximately as expected. No indications for faults on image logs (Figure 2-2., image log tadpoles in track 11). Classification: Internal    Status: Final    Expiry date: 2010-12-30    Page 13 of 110

The reservoir section is overall very sandy, but has a reduced N/G of 80.8% compared to offset wells (mostly >85%) due to some thick calcareous cemented intervals in lower Thief Zone (zone 2.1) and upper Lower Hugin (zone 1.6). Average porosity of 16.8% is poorer compared to offset well 15/9-19 BT2 at similar depth. Reference to Chapter 6 for full petrophysical evaluation and CPI. # Pre-drill vs post-drill segment evaluation

Oil shows in the well are documented in Chapter 4. Residual oil intervals on logs are documented in Chapter 6. The presence of some hydrocarbons in the Hugin Formation supports a migration through the Hugin Formation from the kitchen in the North into the main field via the 15/9-F-11 T2 location. An additional fairway in Intra Draupne sandstones has not been proven. Pre-drill prospect evaluation assumed a Pg of 34% with main risk elements trap geometry (55%) due to poor seismic control and source migration (70%) given a possible charge through the Upper Jurassic. The prospect model included a potentially deep OWC contact (down to 3200 m MSL; no OWC drilled on Volve). With top reservoir coming in deeper, the area is below the Volve Field closure and was hence bypassed (Figure 1-2). Pressure points indicate that the drilled aquifer communicates with the main field (Chapter 3.5.1). Top Hugin Formation at 3181 m MSL can be considered a WUT observation for the main field. Statoil Final Well Report
# Well NO 15/9-F-11, F-11 T2, F-11 A, F-11 B

# Volve Field

# 2.1.2 15/9-F-11 A pilot Volve Northwest segment

# Reservoir section

A complete Hugin stratigraphy was encountered in the well. Lithostratigraphic subzonation can easily be correlated with offset wells indicating no significant lateral facies changes. Fig 2.3 shows the reservoir section on logs with the Hugin subzonation in track 3. - Upper Hugin (zones 3.3-3.1): 3594.6-3612.5 m MD
- With ~3 m TVD thick offshore shale at base
- Middle Hugin or ‘Thief Zone’ (zones 2.3-2.1): 3612.5-3653 m MD
- with typical high permeability upper and lower zone as confirmed by high mobilities
- Lower Hugin (zones 1.6-1.1): 3653-3684 m MD

The thickness of the Hugin Formation was with 82 m TVD / ~64 m TVT near twice the expected. No direct indications for faults on RT image logs are observed (Figure 2-3; image log tadpoles track 11). Note that downfaulting would lead to an apparent thickening of the Hugin unit. The reservoir section is overall very sandy as indicated by a N/G of 86%. Average porosity of 20% is comparable to offset wells at similar depth. Reference to Chapter 6 for full petrophysical evaluation and CPI. Main differences to offset wells in the main field are the higher water saturations (Sw) observed consistently throughout the entire Hugin section. Average Sw is 32%, and hence >10% higher than in main field. Based on the fact that these high saturations are observed throughout the Hugin reservoir and not bound to individual zones, they are not considered drainage effects, and overall difficult to explain. # Pre-drill vs post-drill segment evaluation

Prior to drilling, the segment was risked with a Pg of 63%. The main risk elements were reservoir presence (70%) due to potential erosion and source migration (90%) given a charge through Hugin via the main field and a possible disconnect due to faulting. The well proved a thick upside oil column in the NW segment. Pressure points show full communication with the main field (Chapter 3.5.2). In-place oil volumes in the segment are estimated with ~1.4 MSm³. The main uncertainties for in-place volumes are thickness of Hugin further upflank from the 15/9-F-11 A location and the location of the major segment bounding faults especially also towards the salt collapse area in the South. # Well NO 15/9-F-11, F-11 T2, F-11 A, F-11 B

# Volve Field

# 2.1.3  15/9-F-11 B IOR producer Volve Main Field

# Reservoir section

Figure 2-4 shows the reservoir section with the reservoir zonation in track 4. Table 2-1 provides the zonation as list. Geosteering experiences are summarised in Chapter 2.3.4. Reservoir zonation is based on the curtain model provided by Baker Hughes’ realtime reservoir navigation service (RNS) using all available log information, specifically azimuthal resistivity readings (by AziTrak) and real-time image log interpretation from azimuthal gamma ray (GR) and azimuthal density (by LithoTrak). Data is compared to a pre-drill model and offset wells (mainly well 15/9-F-5), and re-matched in real-time during drilling in cooperation with PETEK G&G. Zonation documented in this report deviates from the final Baker Hughes delivery in some small intervals. Overall confidence is high, due to the Heather Formation and Thief Zone ‘calibration points’. The well encountered an oil-bearing Upper Hugin in heel and toe with good reservoir qualities similar to offset wells. The total geosteered section in the Hugin Formation was ~1220 m MD long. A total ~760 m MD in gross oil-bearing productive Upper Hugin (zones 3.1-3.3) was encountered. Non-productive Heather intervals were mainly due to predominantly sub-seismic faulting. Thief Zone intervals were water-flooded as expected. A short upflank interval of water flooded Upper Hugin at 3528-3539 m MD (zone 3.2) was not repeated further down in the well. Towards the toe, the well was dropped into water-flooded Thief Zone and Lower Hugin due to tool failure. TD was set in the Sleipner Formation. An exit of the field through the main fault, as expected, was not observed. With high oil saturation in both toe and heel, the well encountered upside volumes in the targeted Upper Hugin zone. Pre-drill expectation was that the heel would be water filled as indicated by reservoir simulation and 4D seismic, while mainly the downflank toe section would encounter undrained oil. At ~ 4700 m MD / 3147 m MSL a marked drop in resistivity to <0.7 Ohm.m in the Lower Hugin may indicate that the well penetrated the initial field OWC. Water-flooded zones just above show resistivities of approximately 1 Ohm.m, which are consistent with injected Utsira water (+/ produced water). # 2.2 Seismic interpretation versus well results

Sonic logs (both compressional and shear) were acquired in the two pilot wells 15/9-F-11 T2 and 15/9-F-11 A. Checkshots and VSP were acquired in 15/9-F-11 T2. # 2.2.1 15/9-F-11 T2 pilot Volve North prospect

Top and Base Hugin Formation depth prognosis in the North prospect were initially very uncertain. The seismic reflectivity in this area is very poor in all datasets and together with the presence of an undrilled thick Upper Jurassic package in this downfaulted segment the uncertainties on Top and Base Hugin Formation prognoses were set to ±70 m TVD. Top Hugin Formation came in +29 m TVD deeper and Base Hugin Formation came in +33 m TVD deeper than prognosis. Checkshot pairs and VSP (normal incident and rig source) were acquired. The synthetic seismic from well logs confirmed a poor seismic signal on Top Hugin Formation and a weak seismic signal on Base Hugin Formation compared to Base Hugin Formation on the main field (Figure 2-5). Using checkshots as time depth curve a deeper time interpretation of Top and Base Hugin Formation is initiated. This information has been implemented in the updated maps. VSP image data is processed by Schlumberger and preliminary results indicate a more northern placement of the large main bounding fault compared to initial seismic interpretation. These preliminary observations have also been implemented in the updated maps. It was not observed any improvement of Top and Base Hugin reflectors on the preliminary results. Final imaging results of VSP processing are estimated to be delivered during November 2013. Classification: Internal    Status: Final    Expiry date: 2010-12-30    Page 23 of 110

# 26” section

The inclination ranges in this section by 0.0°-2.5°, 2.5° (start-end, max). The Utsira Formation was entered at 887 m, where the inclination was build up to 24º. Rotating in order to observe behaviour resulted in a 2º drop. The remaining of the Utsira sandstones was drilled in orienting mode in an attempt to hold or build the inclination to 22º. Toolface control in Utsira Formation was ultimate difficult due to intermittent hardness of the formation was encountered, resulting in an uncontrollable deviation. Entering the Skade Formation the steering deteriorated even further. Very soft formations were encountered, making it impossible to rotate through it (losing angle). Steering required a lot of effort and time. Classification: Internal

# Volve Field

# 17 ½” section

The inclination ranges in this section by 22.9°-36.3°, 36.3° (start-end, max). The deviation was 22.9 º inclination and 278º azimuth at the start. The well trajectory was very far from the plan due to having been unable in the previous 26'' section to build and turn in unconsolidated sandstones of the Utsira and the Skade formations with a motor. To move progressively towards the original plan involved building inclination from 24º-35.8° and turning left from 283º-253° azimuth by 1592 m at a rate of 2.5°/30 m. Then a tangent section was drilled to 1699 m, followed by a drop and turn with 2.5°/30 m to 2128 m. Inclination was dropped down to 4.6° and the well turned right to 339° azimuth. Finally, a continuous build and turn with 2.5°/30 m was performed to TD at 2580 m where the inclination is 37° and the azimuth 69.6°. At TD the 17 1/2" section was well placed with 1.36 m below and 0.46 m left of the planned well path. # 8 ½” section

This almost 2000 m MD long section started with an inclination of 36.1º, built up to 82.0º over the first 450 m in the Shetland Group, held tangent over 700 m and dropped back to 14.4º above reservoir. No turn was planned, thus the well continued on the same 69.9º azimuth and 14.4º inclination for a further 200 m to TD. The initial build in the Lista and Ty formations went without problems, the tool gave more than the theoretical calculated rate. This changed dramatically in the Tor limestone, where it became extremely difficult to achieve the required 3.0º/30 m build rate. With the AutoTrak tool in steer mode with 100% steer force, it was still necessary to cut back on the flow rate and hold back ROP to 5 m/h to get it to build. Consequently, over approximately 160 m, the well path dropped some 14 m below the line. This was recovered in the tangent section by increasing the sail angle from 80.2 to 82.0º. Stick/slip values increased in the Shetland Group. The drop from 80.0° to 14.0º inclination was started from 3710 m. Initially the tool response was as expected, but in the lower Hod Formation and into the Cromer Knoll Group the drop rate showed a large increase. Drilling continued to section TD without further problems. The section and plan views for the wellbore (from slot to TD at 4562 m MD) are shown in Figure 2-10. # Section View - Actual: 15/9-F-11 T2 (F-11/15/9-F-11 TZ)

# Objectives

The IOR well 15/9-F-11 B was planned to be placed in the Upper Hugin zone to drain remaining reserves
between the production wells 15/9-F-12 and 15/9-F-14 and continuing downflank towards water injector 15/9-F-5. The planned well path was already characterised by challenging steering, thus beside the main focus on
horizontal steering, also sufficient turn in azimuth to prolong the reservoir section as deep into the structure as
possible needed to be ensured. # Summary

Against interpretation from 4D seismic analysis and reservoir simulation the target zone within Upper Hugin
showed good oil saturation throughout the reservoir section. Estimates of true vertical thicknesses (TVT) indicate
thicker reservoir upflank than expected. TD was set at 4770 m MD, as the wellbore entered Sleipner Formation
below Hugin Formation, without penetrating the anticipated southwesterly field-bounding fault. The total
geosteered section in the Hugin Formation was ~1220 m MD long. A total ~760 m MD in gross oil-bearing
productive Upper Hugin (zones 3.1-3.3) was encountered. # Geosteering

The pre-model from Baker Hughes (RNS) indicated higher contrast towards top reservoir, i.e. Heather Formation
and very low contrasts towards Thief Zone below. Additionally are several faults along the well path prognosed,
causing down-faulted reservoirs. For dip calculations the azimuthal GR (11 m behind bit) and azimuthal DEN (24 m behind bit) were used, also to
some extent distance to bed (D2B) calculations. Hence geosteering commands were affected by often significant
time delays. Several faults were crossed, causing 3 times an unexpected/non-pre-warned exit of top reservoir
(D2B ranged from 3,5-4,1 m TVD). Entering of the reservoir again was just estimated based on the geological
model and steering with maximum dogleg downwards. According to Baker Hughes’ RNS top reservoir cannot be
detected earlier than 1-2 m TVD from above. D2B calculations were not always applicable for bed boundary
detection, due to complex resistivity profile. Another 3 times the well continued from Upper Hugin into the
underlying Thief Zone (yellow circle). Here no distinct signal response was observed. Distances to bed were
roughly estimated to range from 1,5-2,0 m TVD. Geosteering decisions were made by the Volve PETEK with support from GEOP and the Baker Hughes’ RNS
team. The RNS supervisor was present in the Statoil office in Bergen during office hours, while RNS support
outside office hours was covered by the Baker Hughes’ BEACON center in Stavanger. # Wellpath description

28 real-time geosteering decisions were performed. Any changes in trajectory was done to optimise wellbore
positioning in the reservoir and to avoid shaly and water-filled zones respectively. - Started drilling reservoir section from 3198,0 m MD / 2783,8 m TVD. Increased inclination from 63° to 71°. - Entered Heather Formation at 3351,6 m MD / 2846,6 m TVD. Possible fault with minor throw at 3414 m
MD, indicated by high angle dip. Possibly repetition of penetrated stratigraphy. Classification: Internal        Status: Final                  Expiry date: 2010-12-30    Page 31 of 110

- Entered reservoir (Hugin 3.3) at 3467.5 m MD / 2884.0 m TVD. Top of reservoir more oil saturated than expected. Drilling down through stratigraphy. Started to build angle to follow Hugin 3.3. - Encountered more water saturated zone in Hugin 3.2. Continued to build angle to re-enter Hugin 3.3. - Re-entered Hugin 3.3 with 9° angle between formation and wellbore. Started to drop angle to avoid low angle exit of reservoir. - Exited reservoir into Heather Formation at 3812.0 m MD by faulting, estimated throw 6.5 m. Increased drop for faster re-entry of reservoir. - Low angle re-entry (4°) and exit (6°) of reservoir, while geosteering up and down to follow Hugin 3.3. High angle features indicates faulting at entry and exit of Heather Formation. Estimated throws are ~0.5 m. - Hugin 3.3 thickness >9 m TVD. Drilled down through Hugin 3, penetrated base Hugin 3 twice (due to faulting at 4062 m MD) and entered the “Thief Zone” (Hugin 2) at 4108.0 m MD. Estimated throw of fault F2 ~3 m. Building angle to enter lowermost part of reservoir before crossing major prognosed fault F3. - Entered base of reservoir at 4188.0 m MD by faulting (F3), estimated throw 11 m. Continuously dropping angle to align with formation dip and land wellbore in Hugin 3.3. Stopped planned left turn of well path to maximise drop force. Minor faulting while drilling upwards in Hugin 3. - Exited reservoir through fault (F4) with estimated throw of 18 m and 24° angle between wellbore and fault. Drilled downwards into Heather Formation. Continuously dropping angle to re-enter reservoir. Stopped planned left turn of well path to maximise drop force and enter reservoir before TD. - Re-entered reservoir at 4483.5 m MD with an angle of 13° between wellbore and formation. Continued dropping angle to penetrate complete reservoir interval and assess reservoir thickness. Possible minor fault around 4515 m MD (F5?). Exited Hugin 3 at 4573.0 m MD / 3113.9 m TVD. - Holding angle of 42° after Hugin 2.2. No active steering possible after 4685 m MD, due to AutoTrak failure. TD at 4770.6 m MD, when entering Sleipner Formation without penetrating prognosed fault F6. The section and plan views for the wellbore (from slot to TD at 4770 m MD) are shown in Figure 2-12. # Section View - Actual: 15/9-F-11 B (F-11/15/9-F-11 B)

light grey to brownish yellow to reddish brown, fine grained, grading to siltstone soft to firm to occasional mod hard, subrounded to subangular, moderate sorted; common light grey to white clay (kaolinite/rock flour), non-calcareous, traces pyrite

# Siltstones

medium to light grey to reddish brown, soft to firm, occasional moderate hard, pyrite, carbonaceous, grading to sandstone

# Coal (traces)

black, blocky, brittle, moderate hard, vitreous, occasional disseminated pyrite

# Planning

In pilot well 15/9-F-11 T2 to Volve North prospect a vertical seismic profiling survey (VSP) was planned. Purpose of the survey was:

- primary: more accurate time-depth relationship,
- secondary objective: improve imaging of reservoir and faults. The feasibility study and pre-job modelling was performed by Thor Allan Garden (thgar@statoil.com, VSP group). Based on previous results from 15/9-F-15 A and recent seismic data a walk-away survey with 2 sources was recommended. Schlumberger’s VSI-4 had been selected and after evaluation of operational risk, especially the highly deviated well path (maximum 82° in open hole), finally a pump down VSP inside a simple BHA was selected. # Operations

A rig source VSP and a vertical incidence VSP (flip-flop configuration) were recorded in the 8 ½” open hole, the 14” and the 20” cased hole. The sources were 3x250 cu.in G-guns, and a Trisor OFS used for advanced gun performance control. Vertical incidence survey interval was recorded between 4530.0-2228.8 m MD, the rig source survey 4530-582.9 m at 15 m intervals. While RIH checkshots were taken at 1786.6 and 2518.4 m MD; a correlation pass was run from 4450 m up. While POOH and measuring communication interference between rig and the vessel Dina Star were experienced at 2228.8 m. The survey was continued with the rig source only. # Results

Preliminary VSP imaging results indicated a more northern placement (~30-50 m) of the main bounding fault to the north compared to the initial seismic interpretation. Final processing is pending. Classification: Internal    Status: Final    Expiry date: 2010-12-30    Page 59 of 110

# 3.5 Pressure points

# 3.5.1 15/9-F-11 T2 pilot Volve North prospect

In pilot well 15/9-F-11 T2 at 6 depths pressures points in the water-filled Hugin reservoir were taken (Table 3-5). This was performed while POOH after TD on May 04ᵗʰ and 5ᵗʰ 2013. At this time both water injectors (15/9-F-4 and 15/9-F-5) were in operation. 6 of 6 tests were successful and of good technical quality (all reached a stable pressure). Pressure points were showing a reservoir pressure 13-14 bar over initial, relative to 15/9-19 BT2 and 15/9-F-15 A water gradient, but in accordance with the main field pressure, which was ~12 bar over initial at the time (Figure 3-1). # Rev. no. # 3.5.2 15/9-F-11 A pilot Volve Northwest segment

In pilot well 15/9-F-11 A all in all 24 pressure stations in the oil-bearing Hugin Formation were taken. 17 (+2 repeated) were performed while POOH after TD on May 21st 2013 (Table 3-6). At this time both water injectors (15/9-F-4 and 15/9-F-5) were in operation. On May 23rd 2013 additional 5 pressure measurements were repeated. This time after both water injectors were shutdown over a period of almost 48 hours. 18 of 24 tests (2 sets of data with time delay) were successful and of good technical quality (one might be supercharged). 6 tests were tight. Formation pressure points in the Hugin Formation were 12 bar above initial formation pressure in oil zone (measured in 15/9-F-12, February 2008) (Figure 3-1). This indicates communication with the Hugin Formation in the Volve main field, since reservoir pressure has been increased ~12 bar above initial pressure by injection at the time. Time lapse repeated pressure points (Figure 3-1) after closing in main field injections were 0.2-0.9 bar lower than 48hrs earlier. At the same time a pressure drop of 6 bar was recorded in the main field pressure gauge in well 15/9-F-15 C. The small pressure drop was consistent with prognosis from the simulation model. # Well: 15/9-F-11 A

In both pilot wells optional fluid sampling was planned. - 15/9-F-11 (T2): 1-2 oil samples for compositional analysis, density and viscosity & 1 water sample for resistivity measurements as water saturation reference for the petrophysical model as well compositional analysis, density, viscosity were planned to take, if the Hugin Formation in North prospect is oil filled or an oil-water contact is been encountered. - 15/9-F-11 A: 1 water sample for compositional analysis, density and viscosity to define water type (initial formation water or perched water), if the Hugin Formation in Northwest segment is partly oil-bearing. Since the open hole wellpaths of both 8 ½” pilot sections were planned with inclinations of 82° in 15/9-F-11 (T2) and 75° in 15/9-F-11 A respectively a feasibility study by Schlumberger recommended pipe conveyed wireline logging for running MDT. # Summary

In none of the pilot wells the criteria were achieved. Well 15/9-F-11 T2 found water-filled the Hugin Formation in the North prospect/segment, whereas well 15/9-F-11 A proved the entire Hugin reservoir oil-bearing in the Northwest segment. Therefore all MDT runs were cancelled. # 3.7 Logging remarks

Information about tool/equipment failure is already been mentioned in the specific run summaries above (Table 3-1 to Table 3-4). The M/LWD logging data suite was achieved completely for all wells. The mud logging data suite is been reduced. The chromatograph failed from start of the 8½” section. Classification: Internal Status: Final Expiry date: 2010-12-30 Page 65 of 110

# 5 Pore pressures and hole stability

# 5.1 Formation pressure above the reservoir

The prediction of the pore pressure gradient (PP) above the reservoir is based on the Eaton Sonic method. - A hydrostatic pore pressure gradient is expected down to top Ty Formation. The pore pressure within permeable Ty sandstones was measured in wells 15/9-F-12 and 15/9-F-15 S as 0.87 sg. This indicates some 40 bar pressure depletion caused by production from the Sleipner Øst Field in the South. Recent pressure data from Sleipner well 15/9-A-24, the last gas condensate producer, show stable pressure since 2005, in line with the stop of production in the northern Sleipner Øst area. The pore pressure in the Ty Formation within the Volve area is expected to be 0.87 sg, as measured in 2007. - In the underlying upper part of the Shetland Group the pore pressure is assumed to increase gently up to 1.05 sg within the Hod Formation. For the rest of the Shetland and the Cromer Knoll Group a rapid increase in the pore pressure to 1.26 sg is prognosed. - The maximum pore pressure within the Viking Group is expected to be 1.29 sg in the organic rich Draupne shales at the top. The pore pressure within the underlying Heather Formation is expected to be lower. # 5.2 Pore pressure in the reservoir

The reservoir pressure in the Hugin Formation main field is currently maintained at approximately 10-12 bar over initial pressure by injecting water downflank in wells 15/9-F-4 and 15/9-F-5. Throughout the field an oil gradient of 0.0704 bar/m and a water gradient of 0.103 bar/m is assumed. Pressure measurements were undertaken in both pilot wells (Chapter 3.5). - In pilot well 15/9-F-11 T2 pressure range was measured to be 13-14 bar over initial in the water zone. - Pilot well 15/9-F-11 A proved a pressure of 12 bar above initial formation pressure in oil zone. - For IOR well 15/9-F-11 B the pressure in the Hugin Formation has been estimated to be 12 bar over initial (June 2013), referring to time equivalent pressure measurements at downhole pressure gauges in Volve main field. At the base of the Heather Formation likely bad to moderate sandstones in the North prospect and Northwest segment are expected to be present. Even there is not often interpreted flow potential at all; this zone has been anticipated to correspond to reservoir pressure. # 5.3 Formation strength

The overburden gradient (OBG) is calculated from a density curve from well 15/9-F-12. Classification: Internal    Status: Final    Expiry date: 2010-12-30                 Page 69 of 110

# Valid from

# Rev. no. A minimum horizontal strength gradient has been calculated from a Breckels and van Eekelen relation based on a method from Per Horsrud (2011). Initial reservoir pressure is used. - Formation strength in the Balder Formation is reduced due to the presence of tuff layers. Nevertheless, XLOT data from well 15/9-F-14 showed a minimum horizontal stress gradient (ShG) of 1.68 sg in the upper part of the Balder Formation. In the lower 32 m of the Balder Formation the formation strength is reduced to 1.55 sg to account for local experiences. - Within the Ty reservoir the formation strength is assumed to be reduced due to depletion, but should not cause any hole stability problems. It is well above the anticipated pore pressure in the overburden section. - The Minifrac data from the Hugin Formation in well 15/9-19 A was revaluated. The result is a 19 bar reduction in the minimum horizontal stress gradient for the Hugin reservoir and the formations below. The fracture gradient for the Volve area is set as 5% above the minimum horizontal strength, according to the model. - In the upper part of the Balder Formation an adjustment to only 1.73 sg was done to account for reduced formation strength due to the presence of tuff layers. A collapse gradient has been generated based on the method of Stassi d’Alia. Included in this method are the initial pore pressure, the minimum horizontal stress, considerations of the UCS (unconfined compressive strength), and the well path. - The collapse curve in the Nordland Group and upper Hordaland Group shows a general increase towards the hydrostatic pore pressure and surpasses it in the Skade Formation. The 36” & 26” sections were drilled with 1.03 sg seawater/spud mud successfully. - The Hordaland shales are normally associated with high smectite content and under-compaction, causing instability and possibly higher pore pressure in the lower Hordaland Group. However, in the Volve area the sandstones of the Grid Formation are generally present, acting as a pressure vent and thereby preventing abnormal pore pressure. Nevertheless, shales within the Hordaland Group have not been observed as unstable, when drilled at high inclinations and particularly when exposed to water based drilling fluid. - - The UCS has been updated/increased by a factor of 1.2 while planning the 2ⁿᵈ drilling campaign and re-evaluating earlier results. Positive experiences from well 15/9-F-5, where the lower Hordaland and the Rogaland Group have been drilled with 1.35 sg OBM at inclinations of 27°-31° without any incidents and on the other hand tight hole conditions in well 15/9-F-12 with an inclination <10° also with 1.35 sg OBM were taken into account. - A maximum collapse gradient of 1.34 sg is estimated (maximum 23° inclination). No incidents have been observed when drilling with 1.40 sg standard OBM. A peak in the collapse gradient is calculated at the base of the Lista Formation. There is no definite evidence for significant unstable conditions in this interval, but washouts were observed on MWD logs and sticky problems occurred when running casing on some previous wells. However post-drilling the UCS was adjusted/increased by a factor 1.15 to account for no incidents when drilling this lower part with 1.28 sg standard OBM, and several kick-off from the here placed casing shoe. - No incidents have been observed in the Cromer Knoll Group.
