# 6 Petrophysical evaluation

# 6.1 Summary

# 6.1.1 15/9-F-11 T2 pilot Volve North prospect

The Hugin Formation was water-filled, except of two pockets of residual oil that seem to be structurally trapped. (Lowest Sw achieved in these is ~ 0.4) One residual oil pocket is situated below the Heather Formation and the other below the “offshore shale” which separates Upper Hugin from the “Thief Zone” (Hugin zone 2). These 2 residual oil pockets are observed in the following intervals:

- 4393.8-4399 m MD / 3182.3-3187.3 m MSL,
- 4415.8-4419.7 m MD / 3203.6-3207.4 m MSL. An oil show is described on cuttings @ 4411 m MD. The rest of the Hugin Formation, which was water-filled, showed very low resistivities < 0.05 Ohm.m in the best sands, is reflecting the very saline formation water on Volve (~ 130000 ppm). This might indicate that the Hugin Formation never has been oil-filled, and that the two observed oil pockets are results of hydrocarbon migration. The Hugin Formation properties seem to be poorer than encountered in previous Volve wells, with an average porosity of 0.17. This is 4 p.u. less than the porosity in the Hugin Formation in the exploration well 15/9-19 BT2, which is the closest well with respect to TVD depth. Net/Gross is however high as expected (Hugin > 0.8); in this well 0.81. Porosity from MagTrak is a lithology independent porosity, and MagTrak porosity in 15/9-F-11 T2 confirms in general the porosity from the density log. Formation pressure points in the Hugin Formation were 13.7 bar above initial formation pressure measured in 15/9-19 BT2 (January 1998). This indicates communication with the Hugin Formation in the Volve main field, since reservoir pressure has been increased ~12 bar above initial pressure by injection, prior to this drilling campaign. LWD log data is of good quality. No oil and water samples were taken since no commercial find. Classification: Internal    Status: Final    Expiry date: 2010-12-30    Page 72 of 110

# 6.1.2 15/9-F-11 A pilot Volve Northwest segment

The Hugin Formation was oil-filled, with porosity being slightly lower and water saturation being higher than previous Hugin wells. An average porosity of 0.21 in 15/9-F-11 A is 2 p.u. less than the porosity in the Hugin Formation in wells 15/9-F-4 and 15/9-F-15 A, which are the most comparable wells with respect to TVD depth. Net/Gross is however as expected > 0.8, in this well 0.86. An oil-down to situation (ODT) ~ 3702 m MD / 3025.5 m MSL was proven, which depth corresponds to Top Sleipner Formation. Formation pressure points in the Hugin Formation were 12 bar above initial formation pressure measured in 15/9-F-12 (February 2008). This indicates communication with the Hugin Formation in the Volve main field, since reservoir pressure has been increased ~12 bar above initial pressure by injection, prior to this drilling campaign. To get a picture of the time lapse effect, injection was closed, and a second set of formation pressure (only 5 depth stations were repeated) was logged 48 hours after the first formation pressure data set. This showed only minor decrease in formation pressure, in the range: 0.2-0.9 bar. LWD log data is of good quality. No water sample was taken since water was not encountered below the oil column. # 6.1.3 15/9-F-11 B IOR producer Volve Main Field

The Hugin Formation was oil-filled in large parts of the well, and encountered also oil in the first part (shallowest) of the Hugin Formation, which was not expected from 4D and simulation model. Averages for the Hugin Formation are given in Chapter 6.6.1 to 6.6.3. Flooded intervals are given in Table 6-5. A flag curve showing flooded intervals is named FLOODED_FLAG (in flooded intervals is FLOODED_FLAG = 1), and plotted as turquoise intervals in track no. 8 in the CPI plot. Below ~ 4701 m MD / 3148 m MSL (a coal layer) the water seems to be initial, due to the low resistivity. This is plotted as dark blue interval in track no. 8 in the CPI plot. Hence 15/9-F-11 B might indicate a WUT = 3148 m MSL, which is not in conflict with the observed ODT (3145 m MSL) in the main field. Since there is a huge contrast between the very saline Hugin Formation water and the fresh, low saline Utsira Formation (injection) water, 3 cases with different formation water salinity are presented in the 15/9-F-11 B CPI in order to illustrated the impact on water saturation. The details of these cases are given in the header of the CPI. LWD log data is of good quality. Classification: Internal Status: Final Expiry date: 2010-12-30 Page 73 of 110

No depth shifting has been applied, except in limited overlap intervals around kick off depths and hole sections in Overburden. These depth shifts and the splicing of the individual runs are reported in Logtek’s info file for each well; WLC_COMPOSITE_1_INF_1.PDF, which is stored in Petrobank. All depths are referenced to driller’s depth. Logtek’s general “philosophy” with respect to depth shifting in the transition zone between a mother well and a kick off well is as follows: aim to keep the stop-coupled depths as shallow as possible, preferably at KOP. But sometimes it has to be set deeper (a few meters deeper, preferably in a shale if possible) due to a) if overlapping curves are not on depth at KOP, or b) if the depth shifts have been so large, that the logs else will be too compressed. # Rev. no. # 6.3 Petrophysical evaluation method and input parameters

# 6.3.1 General

Petrophysical evaluation is performed according to the Volve petrophysical field model, described in report: “Sleipner Øst and Volve Model 2006, Hugin and Skagerrak Formation, Petrophysical Evaluation”. November 2006. Author: Elin Solfjell, Karl Audun Lehne. The petrophysical evaluation software used is Geolog, and the Geolog project is SLEIPNER_OST (at Stavanger server: FROST_SVG). # 6.3.2 Porosity

Total porosity, PHIF (φ ), is derived from the density log, which is calibrated to overburden corrected core porosity for wells drilled with either OBM or WBM. The neutron log, NPHI, has been used to correct for varying mud filtrate invasion. φ = φF + A × (NPHI − φD) + B

where:

φD = (ρma − ρb) / (ρma − ρfl) [fraction]

ρma is the matrix density [g/cm3]

ρb is the measured bulk density (RHOB), [g/cm3]

ρfl is the pore fluid density [g/cm3]

A and B are regression coefficients

NPHI: neutron log in limestone units [fraction]

# 6.3.3 Shale volume, VSH

To determine VSH, the standard model VSHGR from linear GR relationship is applied:

VSHGR = GR − GRmin

VSH = GRₘₐₓ − GRmin

where:

- GR gamma ray reading [API]
- GR min gamma ray reading in clean sand [API]
- GR ₘₐₓ gamma ray reading in shale [API]

VSH is used quantitatively in deriving permeability, and quantitatively indirectly in Netsand cutoff. In order to pick GR min and GRₘₐₓ in a more objective way, and to avoid over-estimating of VSH, GR is “normalized” against VSHDN (VSH from DEN/NEU) by cross plotting GR versus VSHDN. VSHDN is considered to be the most reliable shale indicator (except in intervals dominated by mica and heavy minerals). This shale indicator compares porosity derived from the density log with porosity from the neutron log. If the neutron log reads higher porosity than the density, is this believed to be due to the hydrogen content of present clay minerals. VSHDN is derived from the response equation for the neutron log:

VCLDN = NPHICS − (PHIT * HIFL) / HICL

VSHDN = VCLDN / (1− PHISH)

where:

- VCLDN dry shale volume from density / neutron logs [fraction]
- NPHICS neutron log corrected to sandstone matrix [fraction]
- PHIT total log porosity [fraction]
- HIFL Hydrogen Index to fluid
- HICL Hydrogen Index to clay
- PHISH shale porosity [fraction]

# 6.3.4 Water saturation

Water saturation is calculated using Archie equation, giving a total water saturation:

Sw = (a × Rw) / (φ × Rt) ^ m

where:

- a - Archie (tortuosity) factor
- Rw - resistivity of formation water [Ohmm]
- φ - total porosity [fraction]
- F - cementation exponent
- Rt - true resistivity [Ohmm]
- m - saturation exponent

# 6.3.5 Permeability

The horizontal log permeability, KLOGH, is derived from the following equation based on multivariable regression analysis between log porosity and shale volume (“normalized” against VSHDN) against overburden corrected core permeability:

- Hugin Formation: KLOGH = 10(²⁺⁸×PHIF−⁹×VSH)
- Sleipner Formation: KLOGH = 10(−³⁺³²×PHIF−²×VSH)
- Skagerrak Formation: KLOGH = 10(−¹.85⁺17.4×PHIF−³×VSH)

# 6.4 Flag curves

# Netsand flag

Cut-offs on PHIF and VSH are used to determine Netsand; SAND_FLAG = 1. Hugin Formation: SAND_FLAG = 1 when PHIF > 0.10 and VSH < 0.50. These cut offs correspond to a overburden corrected core permeability of 0.5 mD, and are also applied for the other formations. In addition some manual editing might have been performed on the SAND_FLAG curve. # Carbonate flag

A carbonate flag curve, CARB_FLAG, has been determined manually by visual inspection of the logs. Whenever CARB_FLAG = 1 is SAND_FLAG = 0. # Coal flag

15/9-F-11 T2: 2 coal layers were observed in the Sleipner Formation. 15/9-F-11 A: No coal layers were observed. 15/9-F-11 B: 2 coal layers were observed in the Hugin Formation. The coal flag curve, COAL_FLAG, has been determined by a manually decided cut off on the density log. In addition some editing was needed due to shoulder effects. # 6.5 Nuclear magnetic resonance logging (LWD)

In pilot well 15/9-F-11 T2 Baker’s LWD NMR tool, MagTrak, was run in the 8 ½” section. The tool was ran in silent mode, hence data only in memory.

MagTrak sensor offset to bit was 50.45 m. # 6.5.1 Logging mode and parameters

The tool was logged in “Dual Wait Time” (DTW) mode, also called “PoroPermMT+Light HC” mode, in order to process a salinity independent water saturation. An average ROP of 15 m/h was held in the reservoir interval to assure a sufficient vertical resolution of the MagTrak data, and also to keep the signal/noise ratio high. Vertical resolution with DTW mode and ROP = 20 m/h was calculated to be 1.7 m. Vertical resolution with DTW mode and ROP = 30 m/h was calculated to be 2.6 m. The MagTrak data was logged and final post processed with the following parameters:

- running average (data stacking) of 16: RA = 16
- T2 cutoffs: CBW = 3.3 ms, BVI = 33 ms (standard sandstone T2 cut-offs)
- number of T2 bins: 27 over the range of 0.5-4096 ms. Classification: Internal    Status: Final    Expiry date: 2010-12-30    Page 82 of 110

# Rev. no. # 6.5.3 MagTrak purpose and achievements

# Purpose with running MagTrak was to achieve:

- lithology independent porosity,
- independent (semi-quantitative) permeability indicator, possibility of detecting thief zone,
- salinity independent water/oil saturation,
- discriminate between movable and bound fluid,
- irreducible water saturation,
- independent estimation of saturation exponent, n,
- pore size distribution. Since the reservoir turned out to be more or less water filled, some of the purposes were not any longer applicable. # Achievements

- MagTrak provides both a total and an effective porosity which is lithology independent. Total porosity from MagTrak, MPHS, is plotted (magenta curve) in same track as total porosity, PHIF, from conventional logs in the F-11 T2 CPI. The overall trend in the Hugin Formation is that the MPHS match well with PHIF, which should not be a surprise in a water-filled reservoir. MPHS has (and shows) however a poorer vertical resolution than PHIF. - In the Heather Formation PHIF is lower than MPHS, indicating perhaps a too low matrix density applied for this formation. - MagTrak provides an independent semi-quantitative permeability indicator, MPERM. This curve is plotted (magenta curve) in same track as the permeability from conventional logs and field model, KLOGH, in the 15/9-F-11 T2 CPI. The overall trend is that MPERM > KLOGH with the applied standard sandstone T2 cut-offs. - The thief zone is not detected by MPERM. From the T2 distribution (Figure 6-2) it is however seen that the sand in interval 4449-453 m MD (lower Thief Zone / Hugin 2.1 (7)) has the complete T2 signal coming from the oil-based mud filtrate, hence indicating qualitatively the most permeable zone of this Hugin Formation. Invasion will however of course be dependent of different factors, such as mud properties and ROP. - SCAL data from 15/9-19 A was investigated to check if the default coefficients for the Coates-Timur permeability (MPERM) equation were appropriate (Chapter 6.5.4). This resulted in a permeability curve called MPERM_ADJUSTED which is plotted (light green curve) in the permeability track (track no. 9) in the 15/9-F-11 T2 CPI. - Since the reservoir was more or less water filled, it was no point to order a processing from Baker Hughes to get independent oil saturation. - MagTrak provides discrimination between movable and bound fluid. This is illustrated in track no. 8 in the 15/9-F-11 T2 CPI. Brown shading = clay bound fraction, turquoise shading = capillary bound fraction and yellow shading = free or movable fluid fraction. All permeability data versus irreducible water saturation data derived from different methods listed to the right and separated by different colours. The data from mercury injection should be disregarded. Data from the CCAL (Conventional Core Analysis) plugs are shown as purple squares in Figure 6-3. The water saturation is measured by Dean and Stark method (OBM) and is measured together with porosity at vertical plugs. Permeability is however not measured on same vertical plugs, but on horizontal plugs. Due to this, permeability has been picked from adjacent plugs with approximately same porosity. # Remarks regarding well 15/9-F-11 B CPI

Note that for well 15/9-F-11 B is the CPI separated in 3 parts for better visualisation. Note also that the 15/9-F-11 B CPIs include 3 different formation water salinity cases:

- First case is Hugin Formation water salinity, second case is a mixture of Hugin and Utsira Formation water salinity, third case is pure Utsira Formation water salinity. The details in difference and colour coding are explained in the header of the CPI’s. There is a huge contrast between the very saline Hugin Formation water and the fresh, low saline Utsira Formation water (injection water), and the different cases illustrate the impact on the water saturation. Note also that the salinity is changed in the whole evaluated intervals, and that all other parameters are kept unchanged. - entire Hugin Formation found oil-bearing (82 m TVD) and thicker than expected
- proved communication to main field and injectivity by 2 sets of pressure measurements (1st set after drilling, 2nd set after shut-in injectors and waited 48 h)

# Objectives:

- oil production from infill location between existing producers F-12 and F-14 in the main field
- geosteer in the upper part of the Hugin reservoir, which is expected to be only partially drained

# Summary/results:

- encountered oil-filled Upper Hugin reservoir in heel and toe with good reservoir qualities
- total geosteered section in Hugin Formation was ~1220 m MD long of which ~780 m MD in oil-bearing productive Upper Hugin
- exit field through main fault, as expected, was not observed; TD was set in Sleipner Formation due to dropping towards toe

# Comments:

- 12 1/4" section: trip due to bit change
- 8 1/2" section: exit several times into Heather Fm. above and water-filled Hugin below; dir-MWD failure at 4682 m changed TD criterion

# Subject:

Running 14" casing - several improvements identified on AAR

# Section:

NO 15/9-F-11 T2, 17 1/2"

# Rep date:

24.Apr.2013

# Keywords:

CASING/LINER

# Downtime:

Pot time improvements:

# Comp inv:

Weatherford Norge AS

# References:

Synergi no:

Cost:

Synergi desc:

# Description:

After running of 14" casing on the F-11 T2 well Maersk Drilling performed an After Action Review. The following improvements were identified:

- During the first phase of running casing, the driller used the "drill" function on the link tilt to clear the BX elevator from the casing. Then the bails were tilted out to pick up the next joint. When using the "drill" function the tilt arms are pushed against the bails (compared to "tilt" which pulls on the bails). This stress resulted in nuts from u-clamps coming loose and dropping to the floor inside the red zone (impact 5 joule). The running sequence was revised and the dolly retract was used to clear the top drive/elevators from the casing string. - Weatherford BX elevator need to have correct rotator, it should be supplied with the casing running equipment package. - Ensure stabbing guides and telescopic arms are adjusted correctly on Weatherford casing tong frame. Adjustments should be done offline prior to R/U. - Remove one FMS topguide while running shoetrack to allow centralizers to pass through. - Orientate eye bolts on BX inserts so that they run parallel with the bails and don't interfere with the casing couplings. - The threads are prepared for Baker lock on the pipe deck, when lifting them up use a clean protector cap instead of a rag to protect the threads. # Immediate solution:

Follow up with Weatherford

# Future recommended solution:

Recommended to get the equipment list sent offshore prior to sending equipment offshore. If this is done before the operation takes place, we can check what equipment is compatible with the rig equipment. Classification: Internal

Status: Final

# Subject: Draworks - failure - brake

Section: NO 15/9-F-11 T2, 17 1/2"

Rep date: 24.Apr.2013

Keywords: CASING/LINER

Downtime:

Pot time improvements:

Comp inv: Mærsk Contractors

References:

Synergi no:

Cost:

Synergi desc:

# Description:

0600 hrs the ET was called to the floor because the driller faced problems with draw work. Investigation showed that the draw work motors torque up attempting to take the load from the draw work brake, but when the break released the casing lowered 2-3 inches and the brake hooked in again. Alarm: “LOAD transfer torque failure” occurred. At the moment we only have 3 draw work motors available due to a VFD fault on motor A. The remaining 3 motors only torque up to 46% before the brake was released and then the casing lowered as described. The driller lowered the casing step by step (hard trip) until they were in correct position to nipple up for mud circulation and be able to connect a new stand. Similar problem in 2005. We found the handover from 2005 describing the problem and also with an attached recommendation from Varco. # Immediate solution:

All drilling activity was stopped and we shut down all DWS servers for trying to restart the system. The restart did not solve the problem. The parameter described in 2005 by Varco (Torque correction factor) was checked and we tried to increase the value as recommended up 1.5 (original setting was 1.2). This did not solve the problem either. It was decided to continue the casing job again 1230 hrs. without using the draw work brake. We could make the torque transfer when the hook load was below 250 T. # Future recommended solution:

Follow up with Mærsk

# 4 Activity highlights

# 4.1 NO 15/9-F-11, 36" (07.Mar.2013 00:00)

START: 07.Mar.2013 00:00 146 m MD

END: 14.Mar.2013 20:00 208 m MD

# OBJECTIVE

Spud and drill 36” section to 208 m MD/208 m TVD. # SUMMARY

The well was spudded, and a 36” hole was drilled from 146 m MD to section TD 208 m MD in one run with an average rate of penetration (ROP) of 5.7 m/hr. The hole was drilled with a 36” x 26” hole opener above a 17 ½” bit. At TD 25 m³ sweep was pumped and a wiper trip was performed and the well displaced to 1.40 sg spud mud. After several hours of waiting on weather (WOW) the bit was run back to bottom to check for fill and displaced again with 1.40 sg mud. When on surface found that BHA had parted with top of fish at F-11 template funnel. Recovered 115,91 m of BHA to surface. ROV performed template survey with poor visibility and found a section of BHA laying on sea bed next to template. Evaluated situation and investigated further to determine details of fish in hole and laying on sea bed. Using a serial no. observed on top of fish at template funnel identified that the upper most part of the fish was a 9 11/16" OD wear sleeve which forms the upper part of 11 3/4" stabilizer on the OnTrack tool. Length of fish in hole re-calculated to be 11,90 m, approximately 4-5 ton (in air). Length of BHA on seabed estimated to be 46.62 m, approximately 7 ton (in air). M/U fishing BHA spear assembly to attempt to engage 3" ID opening at top of fish. POOH and M/U 2 1/2" - 3 5/8" Taper Tap BHA. The fish was successfully engaged and fish was layed out on seabed. A new 36” BHA was M/U and RIH. The hole was circulated clean and displaced to 1.40 sg mud, before the BHA was pulled to surface and laid down. The conductor was then run to 202 m MD and cemented to surface without incidents. The conductor was held in place while the cement set up. # BIT RUNS

# RUN # 1

A 17 1/2” Hughes Christensen VM-T303DX0 type bit on an Odfjell 26” x 36”, two stage hole opener (HO) and a Baker Hughes MWD string was run in hole on 5 ½” HWDP and 5 ½” drill pipe (DP) and drilled to TD in one run. Drilling Parameters: 3400 lpm, 77 bar, 62m, 10.8 drill hrs, 14.8 circ hrs, 5.7 m/hr, 2 – 3 tons, 60 – 70 rpm, 4 - 8 kNm

Bit grading: N/A (on seabed)

Ran in hole and drilled out the shoetrack from 197 to 202 m MD. The rat hole was cleaned out and reamed in stages to 208m MD. No restrictions were verified by pulling through the rat hole and the shoe, before commenced drilling. Drilled 26” hole to 281 m MD, evaluated survey data and wellpath. The 8 ½” pilot to North West was kicked off from F-11 T2 at 2586 m MD and drilled in two stages. First stage was drilled to 3300 m MD and a gyro was run. While drilling through Ty Fm a 4m³ LCM pill per stand was added to prevent downhole losses, and no losses were observed. After performing the gyro run, the BHA was pulled into the 14” casing shoe and operation stopped while Lewek Connector was finishing gas pipe work. The hole was circulated ~1 hr every 12 hrs with 1100 lpm. After approx. 50 hr waiting, the hole was circulated clean prior to run back on bottom to continue drilling to 3380 m MD. At this depth the MW was increased from 1.28 sg to 1.32 sg with two circulations, the same method as for the previous pilot hole. Drilled to TD at 3762 m MD without any problems, though TD was set deeper than planned due to Hugin Fm was entered deeper than expected and were also thicker. The hole was circulated clean with 3 x B/U prior to take pressure points with TesTrak. After waiting approx. 48 hrs the BHA was RIH to 3762 m MD and five new pressure points were taken and the BHA pulled to surface. # OBJECTIVE

Sidetrack and drill 12 ¼” hole from 14” casing shoe and perform a gyro verification run when POOH to 14” casing shoe. # SUMMARY

This section was drilled in two runs. A 12 ¼” rotary steerable drilling BHA was picked up and RIH in cased hole. Tagged top of the second cement plug at 2531m MD. The cement inside the casing was drilled while treating the mud. Prior to entering the Ty Fm. A 4m³/stand 1.28 sg LCM pill was bled into the system to prevent any losses. When entering the Shetland chalk, the ROP reduced from 19 m/hr to 11 m/hr and then finally to 5 m/hr. After slow drilling (13 m for 25 hrs) it was decided to POOH for a bit change. The mud motor, CoPilot, AutoTrak and the bit were layed out. These components were replaced prior to RIH. TD was reached at 3197 m MD without any problems, hole circulated clean with 3.5 x B/U while reciprocating the string. A gyro was dropped before pulling the BHA to surface. A 9 5/8” x 10 ¾” casing was run and cemented 400m above casing shoe. Displaced the cement with calculated displacement volume plus 1/2 shoetrack volume. Total displacement volume with rig pumps 116 m3 (5227 strokes). No indication of top plug bumped. Total losses while displacement 7-9 m3. Bleed off pressure and checked for back flow. Monitored for back flow on trip tank. Recorded 1.2 m3 back flow over a periode of 1 hr. Closed full open safety (FOSV) valve and monitored annulus on trip tank. L/D cement head and checked for back flow. Observed flow from string. Closed FOSV and installed circulation swedge. Lined up surface lines to cement unit and applied 32 bar monitoring for pressure build-up. Recorded 35 bar pressure build-up to 67 bar. Pressured up to 69 bar and held back pressure. After 8 hrs, bled off pressure and monitored for pressure build-up. No pressure build-up observed. Before the casing hanger seal assembly was set, the 14 x 9 5/8” annulus was bullheaded with 82 m³ of 1.10sg NaCl, corrosion inhibited packer fluid through a closed BOP and the PADPRT at wellhead level. This was done in order to prevent annulus pressure build up (APB). A casing scraper assembly was picked up and RIH to 140 m MD and the riser mud was displaced to 1.28sg. Scraped inside the 9 5/8” casing. Performed USIT logging of the 9 5/8” casing cement. Top of cement fund at 2679 m MD. # BIT RUNS

REMARKS: 10 3/4" x 9 5/8" casing

# Objective

To cement 10 ¾” x 9 5/8” casing 400m above casing show to provide sufficient cement for production packer. # Execution

Pumped 27 m3 of 1.52 sg spacer, mixed and pumped 16.8 m3 of 1.90 sg cement slurry. Displaced the cement to the rig floor with 3 m3 and zeroed stroke counters and displaced the cement with 92 m3 (4152 strokes) 1.28 sg OBM, 2500 lpm 36 bar. Observed indication of losses. Slowed down pumps and displaced cement to 110 m3 (4954 strokes) with 2000 lpm, 32 bar. Slowed down pumps to 500 lpm (FCP 40 bar) and displaced cement with calculated displacement volume plus 1/2 shoetrack volume. Total displacement volume with rig pumps 116 m3 (5227 strokes). No indication of top plug bumped. # Classification

# Objective:

Bring TOC to TOL 3099 mtr MD to isolate liner lap. Provide structural support to the liner and zonal isolation across the interval. # Execution:

Mix water was mixed with sea water using CMS pumps that get signals from mix water pump and then give accurate volume of chemicals. This happened in one operation right before the dry cement is added. Spacer pit was cleaned, filled with water and then taken a chloride test. The test shows 350 ppm (maximum 2000 accepted for spacer). Then we added the rest of additives to the spacer and checked the rheology to be sure the spacer is as supposed. No losses during the cement job. It was 6 m3 less in return at the end of displacing. Liner was rotating while cementing. After 10 min mixing we lost cement transferring from the silo. We then went to our back-up silo and continued as planned. The silos was function tested before the job. Wiper plug sheared as planned. Classification: Internal

Status: Final

Statoil FINAL WELL REPORT
# 1 General well data

Objective:

The completion is to provide for production of oil from the Hugin reservoir. The completion is designed for easy access when it comes to well interventions and later P&A operations. The well interventions can be PLT and RST runs, plug setting or straddle installation to shut off water production and re-perforation operations, However interventions are not limited to mentioned operations. The well has been brought on production after being perforated using tractor conveyed orientated guns. Planned well life is 10 years. # Well Data:

# Completion Strategy:

The F-11B IOR completion is a 7” monobore design with 7” production tubing and 7” cemented liner. The completion incorporates: 7” x 9 5/8” production packer, single DHG for reservoir pressure and temperature monitoring and a TRSCSSV down hole safety valve. The 7” liner is placed in the high permeable Hugin sand-reservoir in the main Volve field. The well has been perforated using wireline tractor conveyed oriented guns. The wells are tied into the platform with 14” Production Risers from subsea WH to surface WH. Prior to the completion a 10 ¾” surface tieback casing is installed from the subsea WH to the surface WH inside the 14” Production Riser, connecting the subsea and surface WH. The entire riser system is set in app. 150 ton tension provided by the hydraulic tension system. Classification: Internal Status: Final Expiry date: Page 4 of 64

# Perforation:

The well has been perforated using oriented guns on wire-line tractor. Estimated perforated interval in 7” liner is 8 runs, approximately 12.5 meter pr. run.

The perforation guns were 4 ½” OrientXact, PowerPlus Low debris Charges, phasing 10, with 4 shots per foot. # Operational Sections:

# Description:

# Operation:

When running in with the tie back assembly, we lowered it slowly, recorded down and up weights, then tagged the thread of the subsea casing hanger and set down 2 ton of landing string weight and measured from top of the joint in the rotary to the rotary table, we then marked the pipe with a vertical line and rotated the string using the TDS and elevators 1 full turn. We then measured again and compared figures which indicated we had entered the thread, we then proceeded to rotate another full turn and re-measure to confirm and the indication was positive that we were entering the threads. We then removed the elevators and continued to rotate the casing with a chain tong to remove the casing hanger RT, however, once a few rotations were performed (with difficulty) we put the elevators back onto the string and picked up 1 ton and rotated out the casing hanger running tool. # Recommendations for consideration:

To rotate tie back as stated above but after proof by measurement that we were in the threads we would continue with rotations using the TDS and elevators in a controlled manner, this would eventually build a little torque until a point was reached where the casing hanger RT broke out and we would recover to surface in one smooth operation. We would then continue to follow the DOP as planned to finish the setting and testing plan of the operation. # Immediate solution:

# OBJECTIVE

- Make up and land tubing hanger and THRT. - Terminate lines and install communication collar and hanger running tool. - Land and leak test the tubing hanger seal void
- Drop EVO plug ball and circulate down with brine until seated
- Set the production packer and pressure test the completion to 345 bar. - Inflow test the DHSV
- Retrieve EVO plug with tractor. Evaluate if tractor is needed to retrieve plug. (Approx. 62 deg deviation)
- Pressure up annulus to leak test the production packer from above to 345 bar. Inflow test A-annulus inner Valve. - Release and pull out with THRT

# SUMMARY

Picked up and made up tubing hanger to the completion string. Fed the control lines through hanger and terminated. Leak tested all penetrations / fittings to 35 / 450 bar. Installed communication collar according to GE procedure. Made up landing string, ran in hole and landed the Tubing hanger. Pressure tested liner hanger seal to 35 / 345 bar. Rigged up swedge and cement hose. Dropped the ball for activation of EVO plug. Pumped down the ball with 3500
lpm. Pressure increased to 70 bar when the ball landed. Pressure increased to 345 bar and held for 30 min to fully set
the packer. Tubing and annulus tested to 345 bar successfully. Rigged up wireline and retrieved the EVO plug. Pressure tested packer from below to 345 bar with 1,03 sg brine. Tubing hanger released and retrieved. BPV set on 3
1/2" DP. Nippled down BOP and retrieved surface BOP riser. # 4.9 NO 15/9 -F-11B IOR11 B, Install X-mas tree (06.07.2013 12:30)

# SUMMARY

Rigged up wire line equipment for perforation operation with PowerJet Plus Low Debris 4504 HMX Phasing: 10-350 deg4 SPF guns. Gamma ray tool added to string for the first run to correlate liner. Following perforation runs performed:

# Perf#1 (From 4527 to 4539 m MD)

Run no. 1 (Tool length: 29.86m, MAX OD:4.5 in, Tool Weight:1157.65 kg) (Failed / Tractor problem)

The simulation by Aker well service shows that a 2 sonde tractor (with 1000 lbf power) should be able to cope with the resistance forces; particularly at the depth 3700 m which has the highest angle (90 deg). But BHA was not able to pass the mentioned depth.

Back up tool re-run. Same problem was observed at 3700m although after several attempts string passed the problem point. The interval #1 perforated successfully. It was concluded; the actual well friction/resistance is higher than predictions so a new set of tractors with 3 sondes and 1500 lbf power was ordered. Although the gun was fired successfully, when the BHA reached surface and entered to the lubricator, cable was snapped off. It was parted from the weak point on the cable head. Subject: 7in liner, no need to thread lock shoe track

Section: NO 15/9-F-4, COMPL_LINER

Rep date: 05.03.2008

Keywords: CASING/LINER

Downtime: No

Pot time improvements: NA

Comp inv: Statoil

References: No

Synergi no: No

Synergi desc: NA

Description: Shoetrack was bakerlocked when running 7". The liner was unintentionally released/lost in hole when RIH on landing string, fished OOH and racked back as stands in the derrick. It was not re-run, but later broken down and laid out from the derrick. Due to bakerlocked shoetrack, this was very difficult to brake, had to use rig tongs. This is an injector well with a requirement to place the shoe track placed below 40m below the bottom reservoir. The shoe track will never be drilled out. The potential problem would be, if the liner had to be pulled, as did happen here, then the threadlocked joints may have to be heated to back them off and this requires hot work in a tent around the joint. In the event, the joint above the landing collar was not threadlocked and it was possible to rack back the shoe track as a triple. Immediate solution:

(*) Actual cost due to extra time to install plug in bottom of liner to achieve good liner test. # Synergi desc:

Synergi 998874 (Closed) - 09.03.2008 - Other non-conformities - Weatherford wiper plugs for 7" liner did not release. During a cmt job for a 7" liner both bottom and top dart were dropped, but the wiper plugs in the liner hanger RT did not release. # Description:

Cemented liner with rotation according to programme. Did not see any pressures indicating that cement plugs sheared out, but pressure and torque figures showed that cement was displaced in place. Also had returns with spacer and cement when circulating out after releasing running tool. Pumped 2 sponge balls to clean string and POOH, found that the wiper plugs were still on, and found damaged darts on top of plugs with sponge ball wedged in between darts. Dart(s) must have stopped somewhere in the string during cement job, and possibly the sponge balls had pushed them down to top of the plugs. All the string was drifted with 2 3/4" drift, string consisting of 5 1/2" DP and some 5 1/2" HWDP. # Immediate solution:

Tool sent to town for investigation, awaiting result. Evaluate design of darts. # Future recommended solution:

Consider to run bigger drifts, according to pipe size. # Subject: Premature hydraulic release

# Synergi desc:

Synergi 997010 (Closed) - 01.03.2008 - Other non-conformities - 7" liner hanger running tool released from 7" liner hanger. 7" liner hanger running tool released from 7" liner hanger and 7" liner without exceeding the operational criteria. # Description:

7" liner was RIH to 3392 mMD. Took 15 MT at 3392 mMD. Connected TDS and attempted to work past tight spot with 20-30 MT. 181 MT upweight and 141 MT down weight. Applied 9400 Nm right hand torque when working string up and released when working down to avoid mechanical release of running tool. Broke circulation, 230 lpm/37 bar, and established rate at 530 lpm/49 bar. Applied max 20 MT when washing down. SPP increased to 60 bar. Shut down pumps and recorded 22 bar back pressure trapped below shoe. Applied 9400 Nm right hand torque and worked string up. Upweight of 151 MT showed that the running tool had released and liner was left in hole. (Estimated weight of liner in mud at this depth was equal to 30 MT) POOH with liner running tool. The tool was inspected at surface and found that hydraulic release most likely had been activated.

All remaining items on the running tool were intact. # Immediate solution:

# Description:

After the 7" 13CrS110 liner was fished and racked back in the derrick, discussions were ongoing wether the liner should be able to re-run back in hole or wether the Vam Top connections could have been damaged causing more time to lay out the damaged joints while running back in hole with the liner. After discussions with the disipline adviser a complete new liner was RIH. The racked back liner was inspected onshore and showed significant damage on most of the connections. All damaged conections were replaced. This experience shows that chrome liner that has been run in hole should allways be send to shore for inspection prior to install it back in a well. # Immediate solution:

Racked back liner in derrick and run in hole a new liner. # Future recommended solution:

Never run used chroom liner in hole before inspecting the connections for damage. # 2. Subject: Volve F-4 Completion 14.03.2008 Experience when installing Riser Landing Guide Funnel

Synergi desc:

# Description:

The Filter unit was used prior to the clean-up to filter some contaminated Packer-fluid (approx 80 m3 in Pit #4) which was circulated through the filters several times to reduce it from 180 to approximately 50 NTU. The fluid in the pit had been contaminated with Barite.

(Note. Contamination was traced to Barite dust leaking into Plenum Chamber from Surge hopper. Chamber is sealed (to reduce dust) and therefore Barite leak could not be seen). This was a useful field test of the filter units prior to the actual clean-up operation to ensure it was fully working. Run for 1 hour, 52 x 2 = 104 25 micron and 52 x 2 = 104 10 micron filters used. The filter unit was not used on the packer-fluid returns from the well as these went rapidly from being unfilterable (due to polymers) to "in specification" (less than 0.05% solids) and re-circulating used Packer-fluid was not required. The rapid clean up meant that on this occasion, as no re-circulation on a closed (filtered) system was needed the flowline etc could be cleaned off-line thus saving considerable amounts of rig time. # Immediate solution:

None. # Future recommended solution:

Despite the units not being needed during this particular clean up, it is recommended that filter units are onboard on future wells to reduce the amount of slops, particularly as future wells will probably behave differently, or as a contingency for pre-job packer-fluid contamination. Consider increasing the volume of sea water spacer after the last Hi-Vis Soap pill to try and keep polymers separated from the Packer-fluid. We also recommended the practice of filtering the brine while taking it onboard to the rig. This should be done by rigging up a cartridge unit on the loading station using 10 & 2 micron filters. This is to avoid contamination of the brine tank & also to reduce the amount of solids in the brine before it is mixed into Packer-fluid giving a cleaner starting point. On this well the cartridge units were received onboard after the brine so this was not possible. # Subject:

# Experiences during pulling HPDR:

1. After TBC was disconnected and pulled free from 18 3/4" WH, the ROV observed that the TBC internal debris seal was left around 18 3/4" WH (see attached picture). 2.

One of the Fairing halves was broken in two parts (need more info to find out why). 3. All accessible (parted) riser-flanges were inspected by Vector rep., and all seal-surfaces found to be OK, prior to put on preservation fluid (Molykoat ...) and protectors/Handling Tools. 4. When a Fairing was being removed, too much tension was applied on the tugger attached to the Fairing handling Tool, so when it came loose, the person holding onto the tool was lifted along with the Fairing (RUH). --> Put note into DOP, to verify tugger is slack prior to loosening the Fairing from the riser flange. Action: Modify handling tool to make it easier to get it underneath the Fairing. 5. When the TBC was pulled above centralizer deck it was observed that the Guiding Skirt on the TBC (which we knew was damaged during landing of riser on F-4) was loose, and two bolts were missing. Put into DOP that the TBC shall always be

Inspected prior to lifting further up from the Centralizer deck, to avoid dropped objects. Action: The back-up TBC/Stress Joint is going to be used on next well F-14, while the primary is sent onshore for repair. 1. When the Lower Pup was pulled to drillfloor it was observed that the Zink-Anodes attached to it had moved approx 5cm, most probably due to interference with the WH deck openings during pulling riser. Action: The zinc-anodes need to be inspected and repaired, prior to be sent offshore again. 2. The newly trained Vetco riser crew needed some time to find out the best way to break the flange connections using the bandak Bolt Tensioning Tools, but later on they performed well with approx 2 hrs to break one connection. Some of the nuts did not come loose using the small turning-pins, due to some corrosion. Had to hit the nut lightly with a hammer. Had 7 Bolt Tensioning Tools available, but 3 was not in use in the end due to the return-function (springloaded) was not functioning well. Action: Need to send all M45 Tools onshore for full refurbishment. 3. Dummy hotstab (which was installed after TBC was landed) was missing when TBC was inspected at surface. Action: need to order new from Vetco.

4. When M/U Claxton handling and test tool to HPDR BOP Joint it was not possible to M/U it up to required torque for pulling entire riser. Had to disconnect and L/O BOP Joint prior to disconnect TBC. Action: Need to order special purpose built J-GUn adapter to be able to take up reactive torque against locking dogs on Claxton Tool. 5. During pulling of HPDR the significant wave height Hs varied from 1.25 to 2.25, and the observed horizontal pendulous movement of the riser varied up to 1m to each side, based on ROV observations subsea, and visual observation of riser in splash zone. 6. When laying out HPDR doubles and racking them in the Horizontal Racking System in the logical sequence (in order to prepare for running on next well), the lowermost riser double consisting of Lower Pup and Tensioner Joint was not long enough to be resting on all Racking Arms. The risers are now stored without resting in the fwd-most racking arms, but are still resting in 3 racking arms which we have found to be adequate. # Immediate solution:

Synergi desc:

# Description:

Experiences during running Production Riser (see also attached file with pictures):

1. TBC picked up to drillfloor. Flushed & Function tested TBC while TBC/Stress Joint hanging in TDS - No need to shut down Oil Production (Saved about 4 hrs Oil Production)
2. Took off Guide Wire Clamp and Dummy hot stabs prior to P/U TBC/Stress Joint to drillfloor, to reduce potential dropped object risk. 3. Found hole-covers which suited well for covering open hole around PR flanges during M/U operation. But still need to order new aluminum hole-covers (due to the ones purpose built): Action: order 2 sets of aluminum hole covers for PR & HPDR. 4. Vetco proved to perform well on M/U PR flanges using Bandak Bolt Tensioning Tools. 5. Used "Air Hammer" torque tool to remove hex-nuts on Handling Tools, in place of J-Gun (saved 1/2 hr per jt)
6. When TBC at Centraliser Deck:
1. Attached pull in wire to TBC body, approx 0.5m above btm of TBC, and taped wire loops using Duct tape. Attached monkey-fist for easy removal of sling by ROV. 2. Attached Guidewire clamp approx 1m above Stress Joint/TBC flange. 3. Attached 8.5t and 12.5t ROV hooks shackled to Guide Wire Clamp - Here we need to order one more 8.5t ROV hook, due to 12.5t was a bit big. 4.

Stabbed and secured 2 off dummy hot stabs. 5. Ran TBC through Centr Deck, to be able to pull Guidewires from slot 5 and 3 up slot 4, where pre-attached ropes were used for keelhauling wires. A long fishing rod was used to get ropes around from slots. 6. Ran TBC down until upper flange on first double was landed in Spider - then the TBC was about 2-3m above sea level. 7. Ran guidewires to seabed, one by one to avoid tangling wires, and attached them to preinstalled Eagle Clamps on template, and let wires have some slack (to avoid over tensioning wires during running riser). 8.

Had Hs=2.2m when lowered TBC into sea. Some pendulous motion observed, but OK for F-4, since no direct neighboring risers installed (but must consider waiting until lower Hs when having neighboring risers, prior to run TBC into sea!!!). 9. Due to pendulous motion the riser was stabilized with slings at Centraliser Deck. The pendulous motion was approx 1m to each side in Hs around 2m. 10. Tensioner Joint picked up with BX elevator attached to modified handling Sub, where a 3m DP pup was already M/U. Used 12.5t wire slings from bails to BX elevator, to give space for 3m pup between handling sub and TDS. Put hose extensions between TDS and BX. Operation went nice and smoothly.

11. Landed TBC into Riser Landing Guide Funnel, assisting by pushing riser with ROV using C-guide tool. Continued land TBC into Funnel by one inch increments. 12. When TBC was leveled down to just into vertical guide insert, the TBC stabilized fully, the ROV vent down to observe entering of VGX/WH through inspection-window in RLGF, using ROV arm camera. 13. The TBC was then lowered very carefully to 5cm below WH datum, and the RLGF could be removed. 14. The RLGF was opened and pulled aside using Moonpool tugger. The ROV hooks were hooking into some hang-up spots - need to look closer into which type of hooks to be used next time. 15. Landed TBC OK with 10t down, and locked same OK. # Immediate solution:

# Future recommended solution:

Synergi desc:

# Description:

Consequences of last minute decision to run completion with preinstalled ball were not analyzed beforehand. ****************************

Tubing was run with preinstalled NPR plug below SB-3 packer. 1. In the last minute it was informed by BOT that the SB-3 injection packer has to be set against a plug in tailpipe below the packer. This is because the SB-3 packer requires downpull on the packer from the pressure force on the plug, to set the slips fully. From town a joint with preinstalled NPR was mobilized, including backup NPR plug and setting tool. Extra equipment was offshore in advance of start to run tubing (i.e. no lost time). For comparison, The Granite packer used in the production wells can be set by pressuring up the well. 2. Decided to preinstall the ball in the NPR plug before RIH with tubing, to attempt to save some time. This was a wrong decision. While RIH with tubing it was realized that the ball/NPR would function as a check valve when pulling out of the liner PBR if stinging in with the seals. Investigation revealed that a high underbalance pressure could be created in the liner in this situation, if stinging too far into the liner PBR. 3. No downtime logged(94), however situation caused probably 8 hrs extra operational time due to required space out procedure. # Immediate solution:

Therefore it was decided to verify sting in depth by pumping down the annulus and monitoring returns on tubing side. The seals were barely entered into the PBR. This method worked fine. The tubing was successfully spaced out. # Future recommended solution:

Avoid last minute changes to procedures. In this case the decision to preinstall the ball was agreed with relevant personnel. In a situation with time induced stress, the requirement for in depth analysis of the consequences was not seen. # Subject:

Jam tong was set up with wrong factor for tubing type and mix up with ft-lbs and N-m torque values. # Section:

NO 15/9-F-4, COMPL_TBGRN

# Rep date:

08.04.2008

Synergi desc:

# Description:

Found Jam tong was set up with wrong factors for tubing type. Connections were made up to too low torque.

Situation was discovered due to difference between computer reading and manual gauge reading. Had to pull out 5 joints and SB-3 injection packer and run with backup packer assembly. Re-ran same Seal stem. After RIH with SB-3 packer and 1 new tubing joint it was found that the tubing joints had been made up to above maximum torque limit, due to mix up between ft-lbs and N-m. The tong was programmed with wrong torque values. Stopped operation while obtaining advice that it was possible to continue operation with the overtorqued connections. Meanwhile a 2nd backup SB-3 packer was shipped offshore. Performed drift run through 1st coupling, using 5.977” Seawell adjustable drift and tugger. It was found that the incorrect torque values were given in the tally document that was issued. This was probably contributing factor, but experienced Odfjell personnel should have known by experience that something was wrong since the torque figures were "way too high". DBR: 92 / Lost time: 14,75 hrs

# Immediate solution:

Set up Jam tong correctly. # Future recommended solution:

- Make sure experienced personnel operate equipment. Experienced personnel would have known that the torques set in the Jam tong were "way off". - Make sure correct torque values are given in all documents. # Subject:

Synergi desc:

Description:

The riser/lubricator rig up ON DRILLFLOOR required use of high scaffolding to get break point at correct height. This worked fine and there was no additional hazard associated with the operation. The break point above rig floor allowed installing the orienting weight section and the 2 guns sections in the riser before connecting the cable head. Alternatively (preferred and planned method), to get the break point at a height not requiring scaffolding, the toolstring/guns should have been supplied with "special" adapter to allow making up the last gun section to the cable head (orienting weight section and the 1 guns section already in the riser on C-plate). This requirement was missed out in the planning phase. Seawell had plenty of alternative riser and lubricator sections onboard, which proved useful to get the break point as low as possible, in the current situation. Cause: Special requirements for the Schlumberger equipment was not picked up in pre-planning talks between Seawell and Schlumberger. # Immediate solution:

Built workable scaffolding. # Future recommended solution:

Recommendation: Order guns with "special" adapter, if required due to perforation interval length.

# Description:

Seawell pump is not set up for pressuring up the TRSCSSV Control Line in the water injection wells to 570 bar. TRSCSSV pressure relief valve is set to 540 bar.

Test pump could deliver 515 bar. The water injection wells require 570 bar control line pressure while leak testing well to 380 bar. No downtime logged in DBR, but probably used 1 hr extra time. # Immediate solution:

Used Vetco test pump for TRSCSSV operation. Vetco test pump is stored in Vetco container. # Future recommended solution:

Investigate to upgrade Seawell pump to deliver 570 bar on TRSCSSV, to be prepared for completion of future water injection wells. It is safest (and most practical) to operate HMV and TRSCSSV from the same panel. (Otherwise the Vetco pump and the Seawell pump is at different locations which require more personnel to guard during the operation.)

# 4 Activity highlights

# 4.1 NO 15/9-F-4, COMPL_LINER (28.02.2008 03:45)

START: 28.02.2008 03:45 3510 mMD

END : 14.03.2008 07:45 3510 mMD

# OBJECTIVE

RIH with 7" liner to TD. Set and cement liner at 3508 mMD.

Test well to 400 bar/15 minutes seawater equivalent. # SUMMARY

RIH with 7" liner on 5 1/2" DP to 2750 mMD. Broke circulation in the shoe with 1000 lpm/53 bar. Continued to RIH from 2750 m to 3392 mMD. Took 15 MT at 3392 mMD. Connected TDS and attempted to work past tight spot with 20-30 MT. 181 MT upweight and 141 MT down weight. Applied 9400 Nm right hand torque when working string up and released when working down. Broke circulation, 230 lpm/37 bar, and established rate at 530 lpm/49 bar. applied max 20 MT when washing down. SPP increased to 60 bar. shut down pumps and recorded 22 bar back pressure trapped below shoe. Applied 9400 Nm righthand torque and worked string up. Upweight of 151 MT showed that the running tool had released and liner was left in hole. (Estimated weight of liner in mud at this depth was equal to 30 MT) POOH with liner running tool. The tool was inspected at surface and found that hydraulic release most likely had been activated. All remaining items on running tool was intact. RIH with spear assembly to 2450 m, 100 m above shallowest top of liner. Broke circulation and circulated one full circulation with 2000 lpm/88-94 bar. Continued to circulate due to gas peaks of 3.35%. RIH hole from 2450 m to 2562 m while circulating with 490 lpm/18 bar. Tripped slowly when approaching the shallowest expected TOL depth at 2541 mMD. Observed pressure increase at 2562 m to 45 bar indicating that the pack-off had entered the liner hanger area. Engaged grapple and took 10 MT overpull to put the liner in tension. Established circulation at 265 lpm/32 bar for 15 minutes. P/U to 45 MT overpull and observed 7" liner breaking free. Pumped OOH with liner with 265 lpm/32 bar from 3414 m to 2755 m. Circulated at 1000 lpm/59 bar in the casing shoe with a gas peak of 6.8%. POOH. It was discovered that the X/O below the 8 1/2" stabilizer and 3 1/2" DP pup joints was only held by three threads. A separate report on this incident will be prepared. Dis-engaged spear at surface and laid out liner hanger assembly. Neither the TSP nor the liner hanger had any visual signs of being activated. The 7" liner joints were racked back in tripples. The centralizers and the connections were broken without any reported issues. According to the plan, 4 connections were supposed to be thread locked. This was done under supervision.

However, the pin connection on top of the LC released. A wiper trip was performed and the hoe found to be in good condition, tight spots were wiped at xxxm and xxxm. # New liner job

Because of risk of failed pressure test with rerun joints a fresh liner string was run. Straightforward job, One tight spot at 3358 was passed by a 1/2 turn of the string. Washed down last sand and tagged TD. 20 RPM was achieved with <15kNm torque. Centralisers were 16 bowsprings, 1 per joint above the shoe and 8 rigid, 1 per joint in the liner lap. Cement job looked good but the telltale flag took 1 strokes (= half shoe track) before it showed that the darts had passed. good pressure and torque indications showed that the cement moved up the annulus. The plugs failed to bump. When the excess cement was circulated out there were clear indications of spacer and cement but no whole cement. When the running string was retrieved the plugs were in position with the darts on top. (A mark on the bottom dart suggested it may have hung up somewhere on the string) The string was drifted to 2 3/4" before the job on the way out of the hole on the wiper trip. A pressure test was attempted >30 hours after cement in place. Pressure built up in a straight line but dropped off sharply at 290 bar. Clean out run with bit & scrapers using part of the planned clean out string. No cement was found and a 10m area was scraped to allow setting of an EZSV bridge plug. https://partners.statoil.com/sites/a09d1392-d7aa-4bc0-8eeb-1e5c69a6052c/fwr/Document library/FINAL WELL REPORT

- Last minute change to the procedure was required due to SB-3 Injection packer has to be set against a plug in the tailpipe (It was previously assumed to set packer by pressuring up well, similar to granite packer). Therefore a NPR plug was rush preinstalled in a tubing joint below the packer.

To attempt to save time, the ball was preinstalled in the NPR at surface. This was a wrong decision, since it turned out to be impossible to perform the normal space out operation by stinging into and tagging the liner PBR. The spaceout operation was however successfully achieved by pumping down annulus and barely inserting the seal stem seals into the PBR bore. - Positive experience was discussion with "expert center" to reveal potential hazard to well barrier if stinging fully into PBR. When pulling out, the ball/ball-seat would act as a check valve and by suction action the pressure in the liner could be pulled down to far below the inflow test pressure test. - Extra time used for the space out operation was probably 8 hrs. # 4.7 NO 15/9-F-4, COMPL_THINS (11.04.2008 03:30)

# 3.2 Experience listing

# Subject:

to 14" casing shoe. # Section:

NO 15/9-F-14, COMPL OTHER

# Rep date:

31.May.2008

# Keywords:

TEST COMPLETION

# Downtime:

# Pot time improvements:

# Comp inv:

Halliburton

# References:

# Synergi no:

# Synergi desc:

# Description:

A too large volume was pumped while pressure testing RTTS from above. While testing there was unawareness of communication path to 14" csg shoe. MS seal was not installed in wellhead to have communication between well and 10 3/4" x 9 5/8" x 14" annulus as a part of the demand for pressure control in the B annulus due to reinjection in the 20" x 14" csg annulus. When pressure testing RTTS before pulling the HPDR the RTTS was tested from above and the pressure leaked out in the 10 3/4" x 9 5/8" x 14" annulus and broke the shoe at 110 bar/1.30 sg. - There was a number of 'barriers' that was broken during this job which lead to a non acceptable pressure test and a too large volume pumped. These were among others:

During planning - Not including MS seal in wellhead gave a planned communication between well and 10 3/4"-9 5/8" x 14" annulus. However, the consequence of applying a 345 bar pressure test with 1.3 OBM to this annulus was not evaluated enough while making this design change. During planning - Not enough QA was put onto the procedure - No meeting between land and offshore before signing off the procedure. During operation - No company rep present at the cement unit at the last part of the test. Cementer not having enough volume control and pumping a higher volume than specified in the procedure without building the expected pressure. After - It was found that the handover between cementer night and day shift was not good enough - there is no formal written handover with bleed back volumes etc. # Immediate solution:

# Future recommended solution:

A number of IMPROVEMENTS have been implemented:

- Follow up that all procedures/changes are run through between onshore and offshore. - Company supervisor always to be present at cement unit while pressure testing. - Graph with theoretical volumes and corresponding pressures included in DOP where pressure tests are made. # Dispensation:

78185

# Dispensation from Norsok D-010:

Secondary barrier for the gas lift gas is only tested to 100 bar. Gas lift gas will be injected in A-annulus with max 160 bar surface pressure. # Subject:

# Description:

While planning the cutting and bevelling job it came clear that Vetco and Norse Cutting uses opposite angle as reference when defining the name of the cutting steel. I.e. a 15 degree for Vetco is a 75 degree for Norse Cutting. Consequence can be that the wrong bevelling angle is prepared if the wrong type beveling steels are mobilised. For this application it does not have much practical consequence - however at another operation it may be a show stopper. Angle of cut bevel on top of tensioner joint is designed to allow surface wellhead to be installed without damage to internal seal. # Immediate solution:

NA

Ensure that everyone is agreeing on what angle is defined by the use of a diagram - as included in the DOP for this operation. # Subject:

# Description:

Installerte 10 3/4" tie-back inkludert aktivisering av Adjustment Sub ihht programmet og uten problemer. Dril-Quip Adjustment Sub setting tool kunne ikke trekkes ut, men hang seg opp med latchringen i spor i Adjustment sub (sporet som er i bruk ifbm aktivisering av Adjustment sub). Forsøkte å manipulere toolet på forskjellige måter inkludert trekking til max rating av tool (150 ton) uten fa tool ut. Besluttet at det var tryggest à trekke ut 10 3/4" tie-back og kjøre p nytt. Deaktivering av adjustment sub og fraskruing av subsea tie-back ble utført med Dril-Quip tool uten problemer. På overflaten måtte adjustment sub assembly skrues fra tie-back strengen for å komme til DrilQuip toolet som hang i "inner strengen". Da viste det seg at latchringen hadde delt seg på 3 plasser. Prosedyren på boredekk ble utført ihht nøye gjennomtenkt plan og det ble forhindret at deler falt ned i brønnen. Latchringen har 23 segmenter. 6 av segmentene er blitt deformert, bøyd i bue (ref. Bilder). Ifbm prosedyren for å få latchringen til å snappe inn i sporet på toolet så gikk toolet fast i kant mellom adjustment sub og pupjoint. Måtte jobbe toolet og kom fri, 2 ganger. Da kan det være at latchringen ble skadet nok til at det til sist førte til at toolet ikke kunne trekkes ut. Ref.

# References:

Synergi no:

Synergi desc:

Description:

7" Liner was tried made up in stands as a Offline activity. Ref. Teamsite for DOP with recommendations

# Immediate solution:

NA

# Future recommended solution:

1. It should be aimed for start racking back liner in stands as soon as drilling of section commence. 2. To avoid conflict with pipe handling system it is important that centriliser on joints are put 1.5 meters from the bottom (pin) of the joint NOT on the middle of the joint. To avoid conflict with Odfjell stabing guide on tong centrilisers it will be evaluated to put it 2-2.5 meters from pin end. 3. RU of Odfjell tong in V-Door, with personnel standing under cover outside drillers cabin is working OK. Can be improved by either use of wireless control panel by Odfjell, or by building scaffolding tent, as wind and rain protection. 4. Super 13% CR 7", Vam Top HT connections are soft, and it is crucial that the foxhole/slips/tongs and PRS are lined up correct to not damage connections. 5. Use protector on PIN end on all singles as they are very prone to damage while being handled from cantilever deck. 6. It was experienced that joints were slipping from PRS arm. Unsure what the reason is, but is looking into pressure on PRS grip jaw. If problem not solved, evaluate that risk of this operation is too big. Dies in PRS is monel type. Consider if these are the reason for slippage of liner. 7. Keep a good tally and quality check which pipe goes into which stand and in what order. Attached is a suggestion for a tally sheet to be filled in by PDM/HTS Operator. 8. For effective operation (24 hrs) require 2 extra Maersk personnel on each shift as well as 2 x 2 casing operators. 9.

Positive experience: Running the 5 Stands in Hole worked well. (Good weather conditions)

# Weather influence to be considered:

1. "Soft material pipe" Stands racked back in derrick may damage due to movement in pipe in fingerboard. 2. "Soft material connections" might be damaged while RIH in strong wind as stand is moving. Low probability. # Subject:

Synergi desc:

Synergi 1021279 (Closed) - 26.Jun.2008 - Other non-conformities - Leaking fittings on Tubing Hanger

The 5 ea control line penetration fittings in the tubing hanger are tested simultaneously with a multifitting test fixture, which will allow all fittings to be tested in one go. The test fixture fittings are attached to the test ports on the outside of the hanger body.

See drawing. This way all penetration fittings and channels are tested from the inside out. The fittings on top and bottom are 3/8" NPT male x 1½/" Swadgelock. The control lines are run through the hanger with no splices (unbroken). The fittings are made up (3/8" NPT) in top and bottom of each channel. The Swadgelock connection is tightened up to seal around the line. https://partners.statoil.com/sites/deca52cb-3386-423f-a666-40958d0f11a2/FWR/Document library/FWR_NO 15_9-F-14[1] Completion.doc

# 1. Introduction

Then the test fixture is made up to test ports and fittings are tested. # 2. Test Results

The majority of the fittings were leaking during the first 35 bar pressure test. The fittings were made up with more torque and even with high torque it seems like only 3-4 threads were engaged. During next pressure test 5-6 fittings were still leaking. The NPT threads was broken out as far as possible cleaned and treated with Locktite 577 oil and gas sealing compound. Due to the ferrules above and below it is not possible to remove the fitting completely and change out damaged threads. During the 345 bar high pressure test one fitting was still leaking. It was decided to attempt removing the ferrules by filing/grinding away to top section of the ferrules. The operation succeeded without damaging the control line. The fittings were changed out and the female NPT threads were repaired in the hanger. After having applied sealing compound and made up threads to high torque the connection held pressure. A leaking NPT connection during testing can easily have the consequence that the control line have to be spliced below the hanger. Estimated downtime 5.5 hrs. # 3. Description

During pressure testing of the 5 ea control line penetration fittings on the tubing hanger - severe leaks on 3/8" NPT threads were experienced. It seems that the fittings are out of tolerance with respect to the threads in the tubing hanger. Observed that fitting made up with too few threads.

Ref. attached copy of report in DBR

# 4. Immediate Solution

Fittings were MU w/Lock-tite and higher torque. # 5. Future Recommended Solution

Fittings are correct type for threads in hanger. QA/QC onshore to include trial fitting of fittings that will match the threads in hanger. Thread lock fittings offshore. # 6. Valve F-14 Experience Report - Leaking Fittings on Tubing Hanger

M. Inspirer 26.June.2008 / Poul Starck

The following will summarize the problems and experiences when testing control line penetration fittings on the tubing hanger. Refer to the attached detailed drawing. # 7. Problem Discussion

The 5 ea control line penetration fittings in the tubing hanger are tested simultaneously with a multifitting test fixture, which will allow all fittings to be tested in one go. The test fixture fittings are attached to the test ports on the outside of the hanger body. See drawing. This way all penetration fittings and channels are tested from the inside out. The fittings on top and bottom are 3/8" NPT male x ½" Swagelock. The control lines are run through the hanger with no splices (unbroken). The fittings are made up (3/8" NPT) in top and bottom of each channel. The Swagelock connection is tightened up to seal around the line. https://partners.statoil.com/sites/deca52cb-3386-423f-a666-40958d0f11a2/FWR/Document library/FWR NO 15_9-F-14[1] Completion.doc

The operation succeeded without damaging the control line. The fittings were changed out and the female NPT threads were repaired in the hanger. After having applied sealing compound and made up threads to "high" torque the connection held pressure. A leaking NPT connection during testing can easily have the consequence that the control line have to be spliced below the hanger. # Actions and recommendations:

The following will list in random order the present experiences and recommendations for future operations:

- It should be evaluated if the NPT thread is a feasible connection to use in the hanger penetrations ports. It seems like the male thread is too big compare to the female box.

Only a few threads (3-4 ea) are engaged. Maybe an Autoclave connection or similar with a sealing surface instead of threads for sealing. - Action: Supplier to evaluate feasibility of connection. - Quality control of NPT fittings. Seems like male fittings were too big for the box threads. - Action: Evaluate if a quality control of fitting dimension is possible. - Use of thread sealing compound
- Action: Evaluate if use of Locktite 577 is recommended. - Recommendation: If thread compound is used, it should be allowed to harden up (30 min) before pressure testing. # 3/8" NPT Fitting

Subject: During displacing out base oil from the landing string some base oil from the tubing might have been lost

Section: NO 15/9-F-14, COMPL THINS

Rep date: 28.Jun.2008

Keywords: COMPLETION FLUID

Downtime:

Pot time improvements:

Comp inv: Maersk

References:

Cost:

Synergi no:

Synergi desc:

Description:

10 m3 of base oil has been placed in the tubing to reduce the fluid column with 10 bar for easier start-up of the well. After disconnection of the tubing hanger landing string the DOP calls for a circulation due to displace out the base oil from the landing string. During this circulation and while the riser has been static with seawater some gravity displacement of the base oil might have taken place. The volume is not known. Immediate solution: NA

Future recommended solution:

- After disconnection of the landing string displace the riser to base oil
- Pull the landing string
- Run the BPV and install dart
- Run in and displace the riser and BOP to seawater

The dart will then prevent any displacement of base oil. Subject: Installation of 3-way valves for connecting local control panel to DHSV and HMV. Section: NO 15/9-F-14, COMPL_PERF

Rep date: 03.Jul.2008

Keywords: X-MAS TREE

Downtime: Vetco Gray

Pot time improvements:

Comp inv:

References:

Cost:

Synergi no:

Synergi desc:

Description:

The method for hooking up the HMV and DHSV for local control is not ideal. Normally a lock out panel is fitted on the XMT or in the wellbay. Removed instrument piping on HMV in order to hook up the local control panel. When doing this the hydraulic line had to be drained, and some commissioning may have to be done over again. Could not hook the control hose for HMV to the hose bundle as this would incorporate the quick dump (and large hydraulic fluid consumption). However found out that the only reasonable way to hook up the DHSV control line was via the hose bundle, this is a direct supply/return line and the hook up point makes no difference. Also had leakage on one of the fittings on the control hose to the HMV. Immediate solution: Hooked up HMV control hose directly on actuator. Hooked up DHSV control line to connection in hose bundle. Future recommended solution: The simplest solution would be an instrument tee (or three way valve) with a separate fitting for hooking up HMV/DHSV controls. A more standard lock out panel would however be the preferred solution. Subject: Too few Pup-joints and GR-markers in 7" liner

Description:

Due to too few pup-joints/GR-markers in the 7" liner, the correlation passes has to be 200-250 m. When the well deviation is such that a WL tractor is needed to be able to RIH, these correlation passes takes about 1 hour. For F-14 we have 3 perforation runs with tractor and 3 perforation run on WL - but if we include future interventions into this equation, several hours of operational time and risk (using WL tractor) could easily have been avoided by installing pup-joints/joint with different length throughout the reservoar section in the 7" liner. Immediate solution:

NA

Future recommended solution:

Consider future logging requirements to ensure sufficient correlation Pupjoints/RA-markers are included in liner. # 3.3  Incidents by service and company

# OBJECTIVE

Temporary abandone of well - set RTTS barrier and leak test from above

# SUMMARY

1. MU, RIH and set RTTS at 350 m MD.
Leak tested RTTS ---- ref. below!

2. Displaced well to seawater above RTTS. 3. Spotted 10 m3 Hi-vis pill above RTTS. 4. POOH with running tool and LD. 5. Retrieved wearbushing from SSWH. Fractured (110 bar) the formation below 14" shoe when testing the RTTS plug from above. Unaware that there was communication to 14" shoe due to MS seal not installed in subsea wellhead. Due to this, the PR and the Subsea Connector are only tested to 100 bar. A dispensation from Norsok D-010 is required before starting gas-lift (PR and Subsea connector will act as secondary barriers against the gas lift gas which will be injected with max. 160 bar): The design was changed during planning by removing the MS seal in SubSea wellhead. The consequence of this was not evaluated good enough. Need to do a better risk evaluation when changing the design. A meeting between land and offshore to go through detail procedure shall always be performed. A representative from StatoilHydro shall always be present at cement unit during leak testing. Ref.

Run #6 = OK. WITHOUT Tractor. 9. Run #7 = OK. WITHOUT Tractor. Had to use Schlumberger tension and depth system due to software problem in WL winch. 10.

# 8.6.1 Casing/Liner cementing

CASING SIZE: 7"

STAGE CEMENTING: No

REPORT DATE: 18.Jun.2008

THEORETHICAL TOC: 2616 mMD

EVALUATED TOC: 2616 mMD

EVALUATED BY BOND LOG:

LINER ROTATION PLANNED: Y

LINER ROTATION ACHIEVED: Y

REMARKS:

# Objective:

The main objective of this cement job was to provide structural support and zonal isolation across the hole OH section and bring cement to above top of 7" Liner. # Execution:

The job was done by a remote operated cement unit. Mixwater mixed on the fly with CMS system. Tuned Spacer E+ and Base oil was pumped before cmt to ensure proper hole cleaning. No problems during mixing or pumping of cement. Plug bumped as calculated. Document link
